From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Largest city in Hesse, Germany

This article is about the city in Hesse, Germany. For other uses, see [Frankfurt (disambiguation)](/wiki/Frankfurt_(disambiguation) "Frankfurt (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Frankfort](/wiki/Frankfort_(disambiguation) "Frankfort (disambiguation)") or [Frankfurt (Oder)](/wiki/Frankfurt_(Oder) "Frankfurt (Oder)").

**Frankfurt am Main**,[[a]](#cite_note-8) usually shortened to **Frankfurt**, is the most populous city in the [German state](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") of [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse"). Its 778,589 inhabitants as of 2025[[8]](#cite_note-Statistikportal_Frankfurt-9) make it the [fifth-most populous city in Germany](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Germany_by_population "List of cities in Germany by population").[[b]](#cite_note-10) Located in the foreland of the [Taunus](/wiki/Taunus "Taunus") on its namesake [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)"), the city forms a continuous [conurbation](/wiki/Conurbation "Conurbation") with [Offenbach am Main](/wiki/Offenbach_am_Main "Offenbach am Main"); [its urban area](/wiki/Frankfurt_Rhein-Main_Regional_Authority "Frankfurt Rhein-Main Regional Authority") has a population of over 2.7 million. Frankfurt is the heart of the larger [Rhine-Main](/wiki/Rhine-Main "Rhine-Main") metropolitan region, which has a population of more than 5.8 million and is Germany's [second-largest](/wiki/Metropolitan_regions_in_Germany "Metropolitan regions in Germany") metropolitan region after the [Rhine-Ruhr](/wiki/Rhine-Ruhr_metropolitan_region "Rhine-Ruhr metropolitan region") region. Home to the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank"), the city serves as one of the four [institutional seats of the European Union](/wiki/Institutional_seats_of_the_European_Union "Institutional seats of the European Union") (alongside [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg_City "Luxembourg City") and [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg")). Frankfurt is classified by the [GaWC](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") as an [Alpha](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network#Alpha "Globalization and World Cities Research Network")-rated [world city](/wiki/World_city "World city").

Frankfurt was first mentioned in a document in 794 and has been an imperial city since 1372. Frankfurt was a [city state](/wiki/City-state "City-state"), the [Free City of Frankfurt](/wiki/Free_City_of_Frankfurt "Free City of Frankfurt"), for nearly five centuries, and was one of the most important cities of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), as a site of [Imperial coronations](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Coronation of the Holy Roman Emperor"); it lost its [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") upon the collapse of the empire in 1806, regained it in 1815 and then lost it again in 1866, when it was annexed by the [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") following the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War"). It has been part of the state of Hesse since 1945. Frankfurt is culturally, ethnically and religiously diverse, with half of its population, and a majority of its young people, [having a migrant background](/wiki/Immigration_to_Germany "Immigration to Germany"). A quarter of the population consists of foreign nationals, including many [expatriates](/wiki/Expatriate "Expatriate"). In 2015, Frankfurt was home to 1,909 [ultra high-net-worth individuals](/wiki/High-net-worth_individual "High-net-worth individual"), the sixth-highest number of any city.

Frankfurt is a global hub for commerce, culture, education, tourism and transportation, and is the site of many global and European corporate headquarters. Due to its central location in former [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany"), [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport") became the [busiest in Germany](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Germany "List of the busiest airports in Germany"), one of [the busiest](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports "List of the busiest airports") in the world, the airport with the most direct routes in the world, and the primary hub for [Lufthansa](/wiki/Lufthansa "Lufthansa"), the [national airline](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") of Germany and Europe's largest airline. [Frankfurt Central Station](/wiki/Frankfurt_(Main)_Hauptbahnhof "Frankfurt (Main) Hauptbahnhof") is Germany's second-busiest railway station after [Hamburg Hbf](/wiki/Hamburg_Hbf "Hamburg Hbf"), operated by [Deutsche Bahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn"), the world's largest railway company,[[9]](#cite_note-11) whose Frankfurter division [DB InfraGO](/wiki/DB_InfraGO "DB InfraGO") manages the largest railway network in Europe.[[10]](#cite_note-12) [Frankfurter Kreuz](/wiki/Frankfurter_Kreuz "Frankfurter Kreuz") is the most-heavily used [interchange](/wiki/Interchange_(road) "Interchange (road)") in the EU. Frankfurt is one of the major financial and business centers of Europe, with the headquarters of the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank"), [Deutsche Bundesbank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bundesbank "Deutsche Bundesbank"), [Frankfurt Stock Exchange](/wiki/Frankfurt_Stock_Exchange "Frankfurt Stock Exchange"), [Deutsche Bank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank "Deutsche Bank"), [DZ Bank](/wiki/DZ_Bank "DZ Bank"), [KfW](/wiki/KfW "KfW"), [Commerzbank](/wiki/Commerzbank "Commerzbank"), [DekaBank](/wiki/DekaBank "DekaBank"), [Helaba](/wiki/Helaba "Helaba"), several [cloud](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing") and [fintech](/wiki/Fintech "Fintech") [startups](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company"), and other institutes. Automotive, technology and research, services, consulting, media and [creative industries](/wiki/Creative_industries "Creative industries") complement the economic base. Frankfurt's [DE-CIX](/wiki/DE-CIX "DE-CIX") is the world's largest [internet exchange point](/wiki/Internet_exchange_point "Internet exchange point"). [Messe Frankfurt](/wiki/Messe_Frankfurt "Messe Frankfurt") is one of the world's largest [trade fairs](/wiki/Trade_show "Trade show"). Other major fairs include the [Music Fair](/wiki/Musikmesse_Frankfurt "Musikmesse Frankfurt") and the [Frankfurt Book Fair](/wiki/Frankfurt_Book_Fair "Frankfurt Book Fair"), the world's largest book fair. The city also has 93 consulates, among which the largest is the [US Consulate General](/wiki/Consulate_General_of_the_United_States,_Frankfurt "Consulate General of the United States, Frankfurt").

Frankfurt is home to influential [educational](/wiki/Education_in_Germany "Education in Germany") institutions, including the [Goethe University](/wiki/Goethe_University_Frankfurt "Goethe University Frankfurt") with the [Universitätsklinikum Frankfurt](/w/index.php?title=Universit%C3%A4tsklinikum_Frankfurt&action=edit&redlink=1 "Universitätsklinikum Frankfurt (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Universit%C3%A4tsklinikum_Frankfurt "de:Universitätsklinikum Frankfurt")] (Hesse's largest hospital), the [FUAS](/wiki/Frankfurt_University_of_Applied_Sciences "Frankfurt University of Applied Sciences"), the [FUMPA](/wiki/Frankfurt_University_of_Music_and_Performing_Arts "Frankfurt University of Music and Performing Arts"), and graduate schools like the [FSFM](/wiki/Frankfurt_School_of_Finance_%26_Management "Frankfurt School of Finance & Management"). The city is one of two seats of the [German National Library](/wiki/German_National_Library "German National Library") (alongside [Leipzig](/wiki/Leipzig "Leipzig")), the largest library in the German-speaking countries and one of the largest in the world. Its renowned [cultural](/wiki/Culture_of_Germany "Culture of Germany") venues include the concert hall [Alte Oper](/wiki/Alte_Oper "Alte Oper"), continental Europe's largest [English theater](/wiki/The_English_Theatre_Frankfurt "The English Theatre Frankfurt") and many museums, including the [Städel](/wiki/St%C3%A4del "Städel"), [Liebieghaus](/wiki/Liebieghaus "Liebieghaus"), [German Film Museum](/wiki/German_Film_Museum "German Film Museum"), [Senckenberg Natural Museum](/wiki/Naturmuseum_Senckenberg "Naturmuseum Senckenberg"), [Goethe House](/wiki/Goethe_House "Goethe House") and [Schirn](/wiki/Schirn_Kunsthalle_Frankfurt "Schirn Kunsthalle Frankfurt") art venue. [Frankfurt's skyline](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Frankfurt "List of tallest buildings in Frankfurt") is shaped by some of Europe's tallest skyscrapers, which has led to the term *[Mainhattan](/wiki/Mainhattan "Mainhattan")*.

The city has many parks and major [botanical gardens](/wiki/Botanical_garden "Botanical garden"). Roughly 52 percent of the city area is green. Frankfurt is a founding member of the Climate Alliance of European Cities and has pledged to work towards a 50 percent reduction of carbon emissions by 2030.[[11]](#cite_note-13) Frankfurt is the seat of the [German Football Association](/wiki/German_Football_Association "German Football Association"), is home to the first division association football club [Eintracht Frankfurt](/wiki/Eintracht_Frankfurt "Eintracht Frankfurt"), the [Löwen Frankfurt](/wiki/L%C3%B6wen_Frankfurt "Löwen Frankfurt") ice hockey team, and the basketball club [Frankfurt Skyliners](/wiki/Skyliners_Frankfurt "Skyliners Frankfurt"), and is the venue of the [Frankfurt Marathon](/wiki/Frankfurt_Marathon "Frankfurt Marathon") and the [Ironman Germany](/wiki/Ironman_Germany "Ironman Germany").

## Distinctions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Distinctions")]

Frankfurt is the largest financial hub in [continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe"). It is home to the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank"), [Deutsche Bundesbank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bundesbank "Deutsche Bundesbank"), [Frankfurt Stock Exchange](/wiki/Frankfurt_Stock_Exchange "Frankfurt Stock Exchange") and several large commercial banks. Frankfurt has many [high-rise buildings](/wiki/Tower_block "Tower block") that form its renowned [Frankfurt skyline](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Frankfurt "List of tallest buildings in Frankfurt"). In fact, it is one of the few cities in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU) to have such a skyline. The Frankfurt Stock Exchange is one of the world's largest [stock exchanges](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange") by [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") and accounts for more than 90 percent of the turnover in the German market.

In 2010, 63 national and 152 international banks had their registered offices in Frankfurt, including Germany's major banks, notably [Deutsche Bank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank "Deutsche Bank"), [DZ Bank](/wiki/DZ_Bank "DZ Bank"), [KfW](/wiki/KfW "KfW"), [Deka Bank](/wiki/Deka_Bank "Deka Bank") and [Commerzbank](/wiki/Commerzbank "Commerzbank"), as well as 41 representative offices of international banks.[[12]](#cite_note-Internetredaktion-2012-14) Frankfurt has been nicknamed [Mainhattan](/wiki/Mainhattan "Mainhattan") and [Bankfurt](/wiki/Bankfurt "Bankfurt"). The city is also noted for its unique timber-framed [old town](/wiki/Altstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Altstadt (Frankfurt am Main)"). The [Römer](/wiki/R%C3%B6mer "Römer") area has been most recently in the [Dom-Römer Project](/wiki/Dom-R%C3%B6mer_Project "Dom-Römer Project") and hosts the [Frankfurt Christmas Market](/wiki/Frankfurt_Christmas_Market "Frankfurt Christmas Market"). The [Saalgasse](/wiki/Saalgasse "Saalgasse") complements the timbered romanticism with postmodern houses by 12 different architectural firms.[[13]](#cite_note-15)

Frankfurt is considered a [global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city") as listed by the [GaWC](/wiki/Globalization_and_World_Cities_Research_Network "Globalization and World Cities Research Network") group's 2012 inventory.[[14]](#cite_note-The_World_According_to_GaWC_Archived_30_November_2012_at_the_Wayback_Machine-16) Among global cities it was ranked tenth by the [Global Power City Index 2011](/wiki/Global_city#Global_Power_City_Index "Global city") and 11th by the [Global City Competitiveness Index 2012](/wiki/Global_city#Global_City_Competitiveness_Index "Global city"). Among financial hubs, the city was ranked eighth by the [International Financial Centers Development Index 2013](/wiki/Financial_centre#Xinhua–Dow_Jones_Index_(2010–2014) "Financial centre") and ninth in the 2013 [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index").

Its central location in Germany and Europe makes Frankfurt a major air, rail, and road [transport hub](/wiki/Transport_hub "Transport hub"). [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport") is one of the [world's busiest international airports by passenger traffic](/wiki/World%27s_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic "World's busiest airports by passenger traffic") and the main hub for Germany's [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") [Lufthansa](/wiki/Lufthansa "Lufthansa"). [Frankfurt Central Station](/wiki/Frankfurt_(Main)_Hauptbahnhof "Frankfurt (Main) Hauptbahnhof") is one of the largest [rail stations](/wiki/Terminal_station "Terminal station") in Europe and the busiest junction operated by [Deutsche Bahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn"), the German national railway company, with 342 trains a day to domestic and European destinations.[[15]](#cite_note-Bahnhof-17) [Frankfurter Kreuz](/wiki/Frankfurter_Kreuz "Frankfurter Kreuz"), also known as the [Autobahn](/wiki/Autobahn "Autobahn") [interchange](/wiki/Interchange_(road) "Interchange (road)") and located close to the airport, is the most-heavily used interchange in the EU, used by 320,000 cars daily.[[16]](#cite_note-Strassenwaerter_Archived_2_May_2005_at_the_Wayback_Machine-18) In 2011 human-resource-consulting firm [Mercer](/wiki/Mercer_(consulting_firm) "Mercer (consulting firm)") ranked Frankfurt as seventh in its annual 'Quality of Living' survey of cities around the world.[[17]](#cite_note-Mercer-2011-19) According to *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* cost-of-living survey, Frankfurt is Germany's most expensive city and the world's tenth most expensive.[[18]](#cite_note-The_Economist-20)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

*Frankonovurd* (in [Old High German](/wiki/Old_High_German "Old High German")) or *Vadum Francorum* (in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin")) were the first names mentioned in written records from 794.[[19]](#cite_note-21)[[20]](#cite_note-22) It transformed to *Frankenfort* during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and then to *Franckfort* and *Franckfurth* in the [modern era](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"). The name is derived from the *Franconofurd*, from the [Germanic tribe](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") of the [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks") and *Furt* ([cf.](/wiki/Cf. "Cf.") English *[ford](/wiki/Ford_(river) "Ford (river)")*) where the river was shallow enough to be crossed on foot.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Die_Frankenfurt.jpg/250px-Die_Frankenfurt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Die_Frankenfurt.jpg)

"The Discovery of Frankfurt by Charlemagne" by [Leopold Bode](/wiki/Leopold_Bode "Leopold Bode") (1888)

[Thietmar of Merseburg](/wiki/Thietmar_of_Merseburg "Thietmar of Merseburg") in the 11th century reported a [founding legend](/wiki/Founding_myth "Founding myth") in which Charlemagne and his army of Franks discovered the ford at Frankfurt when a [doe](/wiki/Deer "Deer") crossed over it, showing them the way; in honor of this the city was named "ford of the Franks". By the 19th century, the name *Frankfurt* had been established as the official spelling. The older English spelling of *Frankfort* is now rarely seen in reference to Frankfurt am Main, although more than a dozen other towns and cities, mainly in the United States, use this spelling, including [Frankfort, Kentucky](/wiki/Frankfort,_Kentucky "Frankfort, Kentucky"), [Frankfort, New York](/wiki/Frankfort_(town),_New_York "Frankfort (town), New York"), and [Frankfort, Illinois](/wiki/Frankfort,_Illinois "Frankfort, Illinois"). *The New York Times* first used the Frankfurt spelling for Frankfurt am Main on 24 October 1953 and last used the Frankfort spelling on 10 June 1954.

The suffix *am Main* has been used regularly since the 14th century. In English, the city's full name of *Frankfurt am Main* means "Frankfurt on the Main" (pronounced like English *mine* or German *mein*). Frankfurt is located on an ancient ford (German: **Furt**) on the river [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)"). As a part of early [Franconia](/wiki/Franconia "Franconia"), the inhabitants were the early [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks"), thus the city's name reveals its legacy as "the ford of the Franks on the Main".[[21]](#cite_note-23)

Among English speakers, the city is commonly known simply as Frankfurt, but Germans occasionally call it by its full name to distinguish it from the other (significantly smaller) German city of [Frankfurt an der Oder](/wiki/Frankfurt_(Oder) "Frankfurt (Oder)") in the [*Land*](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") of [Brandenburg](/wiki/Brandenburg "Brandenburg") on the Polish border. The common abbreviations for the city, primarily used in railway services and on road signs, are *Frankfurt (Main)*, *Frankfurt (M)*, *Frankfurt a. M.*, *Frankfurt/Main* or *Frankfurt/M*. The common [abbreviation](/wiki/Abbreviation "Abbreviation") for the name of the city is "FFM". Also in use is "FRA", the [IATA code](/wiki/International_Air_Transport_Association_airport_code "International Air Transport Association airport code") for Frankfurt Airport.

### Early history and Holy Roman Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early history and Holy Roman Empire")]

At the western borders of Frankfurt lies the [Kapellenberg](/wiki/Michelsberg_culture#Settlements "Michelsberg culture") as part of the Taunus with one of the first Stone Age cities in Europe.[[22]](#cite_note-24) The Celts had different settlements in the Taunus mountains north of Frankfurt, the biggest one the [Heidetrank Oppidum](/wiki/Heidetrank_Oppidum "Heidetrank Oppidum"). The first traces of [Roman settlements](/wiki/Germania "Germania") established in the area of the [river Nidda](/wiki/Nidda_River "Nidda River") date to the reign of Emperor [Vespasian](/wiki/Vespasian "Vespasian") in the years 69 to 79 AD. [Nida](/wiki/Nida_(Roman_town) "Nida (Roman town)") (modern [Heddernheim](/wiki/Heddernheim "Heddernheim"), [Praunheim](/wiki/Praunheim "Praunheim")) was a Roman [civitas](/wiki/Civitas "Civitas") capital (*Civitas Taunensium*).

[Alemanni](/wiki/Alemanni "Alemanni") and Franks [lived there](/wiki/Francia "Francia"), and by 794, [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") presided over an [imperial assembly](/wiki/Council_of_Frankfurt "Council of Frankfurt") and church synod, at which *Franconofurd* (alternative spellings end with -furt and -vurd) was first mentioned. It was one of the two capitals of Charlemagne's grandson [Louis the German](/wiki/Louis_the_German "Louis the German"), together with [Regensburg](/wiki/Regensburg "Regensburg"). Louis founded the [collegiate church](/wiki/Collegiate_church "Collegiate church"), rededicated in 1239 to [Bartholomew the Apostle](/wiki/Bartholomew_the_Apostle "Bartholomew the Apostle") and now [Frankfurt Cathedral](/wiki/Frankfurt_Cathedral "Frankfurt Cathedral").[[23]](#cite_note-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Martin_van_Meytens_015.jpg/250px-Martin_van_Meytens_015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin_van_Meytens_015.jpg)

Coronation of [Archduke Joseph](/wiki/Joseph_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor") in the Frankfurt Cathedral, 3 April 1764

Frankfurt was one of the most important cities in the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). From 855, the [German kings](/wiki/German_kings "German kings") were elected and crowned in [Aachen](/wiki/Aachen "Aachen"). In 1372, Frankfurt became a *Reichsstadt* ([Imperial Free City](/wiki/Imperial_Free_City "Imperial Free City")) and was as such directly subordinate to the [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") rather than a regional ruler or a local nobleman. From 1562 onward the kings and [emperors were crowned](/wiki/Coronation_of_the_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Coronation of the Holy Roman Emperor") and [elected](/wiki/Imperial_election "Imperial election") in Frankfurt, starting with [Maximilian II](/wiki/Maximilian_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Maximilian II, Holy Roman Emperor"). This tradition ended in 1792, when [Francis II](/wiki/Francis_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Francis II, Holy Roman Emperor") was elected. His coronation was deliberately held on 14 July [Bastille Day](/wiki/Bastille_Day "Bastille Day"), the anniversary of the [storming of the Bastille](/wiki/Storming_of_the_Bastille "Storming of the Bastille").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The elections and coronations took place in [St. Bartholomäus Cathedral](/wiki/Frankfurt_Cathedral "Frankfurt Cathedral"), known as the *Kaiserdom* (Imperial Cathedral), or the four other structures known to have existed at this location. The cathedral spire was constructed according to plans by [Madern Gerthener](/wiki/Madern_Gerthener "Madern Gerthener") in 1415 and is 95 meters tall.[[24]](#cite_note-26)

The [Messe Frankfurt](/wiki/Messe_Frankfurt "Messe Frankfurt") ('Frankfurt Trade Fair') was first mentioned in 1150. In 1240, [Emperor Frederick II](/wiki/Emperor_Frederick_II "Emperor Frederick II") granted an imperial privilege to its visitors, meaning they would be protected by the empire. The fair became particularly important when similar fairs in French Beaucaire lost attraction around 1380. [Book trade fairs](/wiki/Frankfurt_Book_Fair "Frankfurt Book Fair") began in 1478. In 1585, Frankfurt traders established a system of [exchange rates](/wiki/Exchange_rate "Exchange rate") for the various currencies that were circulating to prevent cheating and extortion. Therein lay the early roots for the [Frankfurt Stock Exchange](/wiki/Frankfurt_Stock_Exchange "Frankfurt Stock Exchange").

Frankfurt managed to remain neutral during the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War"), but suffered from the [bubonic plague](/wiki/Bubonic_plague "Bubonic plague") that refugees brought to the city. After the war, Frankfurt regained its wealth. In the late 1770s the theater principal [Abel Seyler](/wiki/Abel_Seyler "Abel Seyler") was based in Frankfurt, and established the city's theatrical life.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

### Impact of French revolution and the Napoleonic Wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Impact of French revolution and the Napoleonic Wars")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Frankfurt_Am_Main-H_Worms-Stadtansicht_vom_Muehlberg.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Am_Main-H_Worms-Stadtansicht_vom_Muehlberg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Am_Main-H_Worms-Stadtansicht_vom_Muehlberg.jpg)

View of Frankfurt am Main from *Sachsenhaeuser Berg* (Mountain of Sachsenhausen) showing the spire of Frankfurt Cathedral in 1845

Following the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), Frankfurt was occupied or bombarded several times by French troops. It remained a [Free city](/wiki/Free_imperial_city "Free imperial city") until the collapse of the Holy Roman Empire in 1805/6. In 1806, it became part of the [principality of Aschaffenburg](/wiki/Principality_of_Aschaffenburg "Principality of Aschaffenburg") under the *Fürstprimas* ([Prince-Primate](/wiki/Prince-Primate "Prince-Primate")), [Karl Theodor Anton Maria von Dalberg](/wiki/Karl_Theodor_Anton_Maria_von_Dalberg "Karl Theodor Anton Maria von Dalberg"). This meant that Frankfurt was incorporated into the [Confederation of the Rhine](/wiki/Confederation_of_the_Rhine "Confederation of the Rhine"). In 1810, Dalberg adopted the title of a [Grand Duke of Frankfurt](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Frankfurt "Grand Duchy of Frankfurt"). [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon_I_of_France "Napoleon I of France") intended to make his adopted son [Eugène de Beauharnais](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_de_Beauharnais "Eugène de Beauharnais"), already *Prince de Venise* ("[prince of Venice](/wiki/Prince_of_Venice "Prince of Venice")", a newly established primogeniture in Italy), Grand Duke of Frankfurt after Dalberg's death (since the latter as a Catholic bishop had no legitimate heirs). The Grand Duchy remained a short episode lasting from 1810 to 1813 when the military tide turned in favor of the Anglo-Prussian-led allies that overturned the Napoleonic order. Dalberg abdicated in favor of Eugène de Beauharnais, which of course was only a symbolic action, as the latter effectively never ruled after the ruin of the French armies and Frankfurt's takeover by the allies.

### Frankfurt as a fully sovereign state

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Frankfurt as a fully sovereign state")]

After Napoleon's final defeat and abdication, the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") (1814–1815) dissolved the grand-duchy and Frankfurt became a fully sovereign city-state with a republican form of government. Frankfurt entered the newly founded [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation") (till 1866) as a free city, becoming the seat of its *Bundestag*, the confederal parliament where the nominally presiding [Habsburg Emperor of Austria](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") was represented by an Austrian "presidential envoy".

After the ill-fated [revolution of 1848](/wiki/German_revolutions_of_1848%E2%80%931849 "German revolutions of 1848–1849"), Frankfurt was the seat of the first democratically elected German parliament, the [Frankfurt Parliament](/wiki/Frankfurt_Parliament "Frankfurt Parliament"), which met in the *[Frankfurter Paulskirche](/wiki/Frankfurter_Paulskirche "Frankfurter Paulskirche")* (St. Paul's Church) and was opened on 18 May 1848. In the year of its existence, the assembly developed a common constitution for a unified Germany, with the Prussian king as its monarch. The institution failed in 1849 when the [Prussian](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") king, [Frederick William IV](/wiki/Frederick_William_IV_of_Prussia "Frederick William IV of Prussia"), declared that he would not accept "a crown from the gutter".

### Frankfurt after the loss of sovereignty

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Frankfurt after the loss of sovereignty")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Frankfurt_Nationalversammlung_1848.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Nationalversammlung_1848.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Nationalversammlung_1848.jpg)

The Frankfurt Parliament at St. Paul's Church in 1848

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Courbet_Frankfurt.jpg/250px-Courbet_Frankfurt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Courbet_Frankfurt.jpg)

*View of Frankfurt am Main*, including the [Alte Brücke](/wiki/Alte_Br%C3%BCcke_(Frankfurt) "Alte Brücke (Frankfurt)") (Old Bridge), by [Gustave Courbet](/wiki/Gustave_Courbet "Gustave Courbet") (1858)

Frankfurt lost its independence following the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War") of 1866, when Prussia annexed several smaller states, including the [Free City of Frankfurt](/wiki/Free_City_of_Frankfurt "Free City of Frankfurt"). The city was subsequently incorporated into the Prussian province of [Hesse-Nassau](/wiki/Hesse-Nassau "Hesse-Nassau"). The occupation and annexation were widely regarded in Frankfurt as a grave injustice, yet the city retained its distinctly Western European, urban, and cosmopolitan character. The formerly independent towns of [Bornheim](/wiki/Bornheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bornheim (Frankfurt am Main)") and [Bockenheim](/wiki/Bockenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bockenheim (Frankfurt am Main)") were incorporated in 1890.

In 1914, the citizens founded the University of Frankfurt, later named [Goethe University Frankfurt](/wiki/Goethe_University_Frankfurt "Goethe University Frankfurt"). This marked the only civic foundation of a university in Germany; today it is one of Germany's largest.

From 6 April to 17 May 1920, following military intervention to put down the [Ruhr uprising](/wiki/Ruhr_uprising "Ruhr uprising"), [Frankfurt was occupied by French troops](/wiki/French_occupation_of_Frankfurt "French occupation of Frankfurt").[[26]](#cite_note-28) The French claimed that Articles 42 to 44 of the [peace treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles_(1919) "Treaty of Versailles (1919)") concerning the demilitarization of the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhine_Province "Rhine Province") had been broken.[[27]](#cite_note-29) In 1924, [Ludwig Landmann](/wiki/Ludwig_Landmann "Ludwig Landmann") became the first Jewish mayor of the city, and led a significant expansion during the following years. During the [Nazi era](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany"), the synagogues of the city were destroyed and the vast majority of the Jewish population fled or was killed.[[28]](#cite_note-frankfurt-tourismus.de-30)

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Frankfurt was the location of a Nazi prison for underage girls with several [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour_under_German_rule_during_World_War_II "Forced labour under German rule during World War II") camps,[[29]](#cite_note-31) a camp for [Sinti](/wiki/Sinti "Sinti") and [Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") (see *[Romani Holocaust](/wiki/Romani_Holocaust "Romani Holocaust")*),[[30]](#cite_note-32) the Dulag Luft West transit camp for [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") prisoners of war,[[31]](#cite_note-33) and a subcamp of the [Natzweiler-Struthof concentration camp](/wiki/Natzweiler-Struthof_concentration_camp "Natzweiler-Struthof concentration camp").[[32]](#cite_note-34)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Frankfurt_Am_Main-Altstadt-Zerstoerung-Luftbild_1944.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Am_Main-Altstadt-Zerstoerung-Luftbild_1944.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Am_Main-Altstadt-Zerstoerung-Luftbild_1944.jpg)

Aerial view of the cathedral in May 1945

Frankfurt was severely [bombed in World War II](/wiki/Bombing_of_Frankfurt_am_Main_in_World_War_II "Bombing of Frankfurt am Main in World War II") (1939–1945). About 5,500 residents were killed during the raids, and the once-famous [medieval city center](/wiki/Altstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Altstadt (Frankfurt am Main)"), by that time one of the largest in Germany, was almost completely destroyed. It became a [ground battlefield](/wiki/Battle_of_Frankfurt "Battle of Frankfurt") on 26 March 1945, when the Allied advance into Germany was forced to take the city in contested urban combat that included a river assault. The [5th Infantry Division](/wiki/5th_Infantry_Division_(United_States) "5th Infantry Division (United States)") and the [6th Armored Division](/wiki/6th_Armored_Division_(United_States) "6th Armored Division (United States)") of the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army") captured Frankfurt after several days of intense fighting, and it was declared largely secure on 29 March 1945.[[33]](#cite_note-35) In spite of the wartime destruction, over 40% of Frankfurt's buildings still date from before the Second World War.[[34]](#cite_note-zensus2011.de-36)

After the end of the war, Frankfurt became a part of the newly founded state of Hesse, consisting of the old [Hesse-(Darmstadt)](/wiki/People%27s_State_of_Hesse "People's State of Hesse") and the [Prussian Hesse](/wiki/Province_of_Kurhessen "Province of Kurhessen") provinces. The city was part of the [American Zone of Occupation](/wiki/Allied_Occupation_Zones_in_Germany#American_Zone_of_Occupation "Allied Occupation Zones in Germany") of Germany. The Military Governor for the United States Zone (1945–1949) and the United States High Commissioner for Germany (HICOG) (1949–1952) had their headquarters in the [IG Farben Building](/wiki/IG_Farben_Building "IG Farben Building"), intentionally left undamaged by the Allies' wartime bombardment.

Frankfurt was the original choice for the provisional capital city of the newly founded state of [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") in 1949. The city constructed a parliament building that was never used for its intended purpose (it housed the radio studios of [Hessischer Rundfunk](/wiki/Hessischer_Rundfunk "Hessischer Rundfunk")). In the end, [Konrad Adenauer](/wiki/Konrad_Adenauer "Konrad Adenauer"), the first postwar [Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany_(Federal_Republic) "Chancellor of Germany (Federal Republic)"), preferred the town of [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn"), for the most part because it was close to his hometown, but also because many other prominent politicians opposed the choice of Frankfurt out of concern that Frankfurt would be accepted as the permanent capital, thereby weakening the West German population's support for a [reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification") with [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") and the eventual return of the capital to [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Frankfurt_Am_Main-Samstagsberg-20070607.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Am_Main-Samstagsberg-20070607.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Am_Main-Samstagsberg-20070607.jpg)

Reconstruction (1981–1984) of six houses at the east side of the Römerberg which were destroyed in World War II

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Frankfurt_am_Main_Hauptbahnhof_von_oben.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_am_Main_Hauptbahnhof_von_oben.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_am_Main_Hauptbahnhof_von_oben.jpg)

[Frankfurt Central Station](/wiki/Frankfurt_(Main)_Hauptbahnhof "Frankfurt (Main) Hauptbahnhof"), view from the roof of the [Maintower](/wiki/Maintower "Maintower") in 2003

Postwar reconstruction took place in a sometimes simple modern style, thus changing Frankfurt's architectural face. A few landmark buildings were reconstructed historically, albeit in a simplified manner (e.g., [Römer](/wiki/R%C3%B6mer "Römer"), [St. Paul's Church](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_Church,_Frankfurt_am_Main "St. Paul's Church, Frankfurt am Main"), and [Goethe House](/wiki/Goethe_House "Goethe House")). The collection of historically significant [Cairo Genizah](/wiki/Cairo_Genizah "Cairo Genizah") documents of the Municipal Library was destroyed by the bombing. According to [Arabist](/wiki/Arabist "Arabist") and Genizah scholar [S.D. Goitein](/wiki/S.D._Goitein "S.D. Goitein"), "not even handlists indicating its contents have survived."[[35]](#cite_note-37)

The end of the war marked Frankfurt's comeback as Germany's leading financial hub. In 1948, the Allies founded the [Bank deutscher Länder](/wiki/Bank_deutscher_L%C3%A4nder "Bank deutscher Länder"), the forerunner of the [Deutsche Bundesbank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bundesbank "Deutsche Bundesbank"). Following this decision, more financial institutions were re-established, such as [Deutsche Bank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank "Deutsche Bank") and [Dresdner Bank](/wiki/Dresdner_Bank "Dresdner Bank"). In the 1950s, [Frankfurt Stock Exchange](/wiki/Frankfurt_Stock_Exchange "Frankfurt Stock Exchange") regained its position as the country's leading stock exchange. In 1998, the [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank") was founded in Frankfurt, followed by the [European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority](/wiki/European_Insurance_and_Occupational_Pensions_Authority "European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority") in 2011, and the [Anti-Money Laundering Authority](/wiki/Anti-Money_Laundering_Authority "Anti-Money Laundering Authority") in 2024.

Frankfurt also reemerged as Germany's transportation hub and [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport") became Europe's second-busiest airport behind [London Heathrow Airport](/wiki/London_Heathrow_Airport "London Heathrow Airport") in 1961.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] During the 1970s, the city created one of Europe's most efficient underground transportation systems.[[36]](#cite_note-38) That system includes a suburban rail system ([S-Bahn](/wiki/Frankfurt_S-Bahn "Frankfurt S-Bahn")) linking outlying communities with the city center, and a deep underground light rail system with smaller coaches ([U-Bahn](/wiki/Frankfurt_U-Bahn "Frankfurt U-Bahn")) also capable of travelling above ground on rails.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Taunus_-_Deutsche_Mittelgebirge%2C_Serie_A-de.png/250px-Taunus_-_Deutsche_Mittelgebirge%2C_Serie_A-de.png)](/wiki/File:Taunus_-_Deutsche_Mittelgebirge,_Serie_A-de.png)

Frankfurt on the mouth of the [Nidda](/wiki/Nidda_(river) "Nidda (river)") into the [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)"), which flows into the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") between the [Rhineland-Palatine](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatine "Rhineland-Palatine") capital of [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz") and the [Hessian](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse") (historically [Nassauian](/wiki/Nassau_(region) "Nassau (region)")) capital of [Wiesbaden](/wiki/Wiesbaden "Wiesbaden"). Also visible the [Taunus](/wiki/Taunus "Taunus") suburbs of the districts of [High Taunus](/wiki/Hochtaunuskreis "Hochtaunuskreis") and [Main-Taunus](/wiki/Main-Taunus-Kreis "Main-Taunus-Kreis"), two of the wealthiest districts in Germany.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Frankfurt_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-07-23.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_by_Sentinel-2%2C_2020-07-23.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_by_Sentinel-2,_2020-07-23.jpg)

Frankfurt as seen by the [European Space Agency](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency")'s [Sentinel-2A](/wiki/Sentinel-2 "Sentinel-2")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Frankfurt_am_Main%2C_Germany%2C_March_25%2C_2018_SkySat.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_am_Main%2C_Germany%2C_March_25%2C_2018_SkySat.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_am_Main,_Germany,_March_25,_2018_SkySat.jpg)

The central Innenstadt district, as seen by a [SkySat](/wiki/SkySat "SkySat") satellite

Frankfurt is the largest city in the [state](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") of [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse") in the western part of Germany.

### Site

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Site")]

Frankfurt is located on both sides of the river [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)"), south-east of the [Taunus](/wiki/Taunus "Taunus") mountain range. The southern part of the city contains the [Frankfurt City Forest](/wiki/Frankfurt_City_Forest "Frankfurt City Forest"), Germany's largest city forest. The city area is 248.31 km2 (95.87 sq mi) and extends over 23.4 km (14.54 mi) east to west and 23.3 km (14.48 mi) north to south. Its downtown is north of the river Main in [Altstadt](/wiki/Altstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Altstadt (Frankfurt am Main)") district (the historical center) and the surrounding [Innenstadt](/wiki/Innenstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Innenstadt (Frankfurt am Main)") district. The geographical center is in [Bockenheim](/wiki/Bockenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bockenheim (Frankfurt am Main)") district near [Frankfurt West station](/wiki/Frankfurt_West_station "Frankfurt West station").

Frankfurt at the heart of the densely populated [Frankfurt Rhine-Main Metropolitan Region](/wiki/Frankfurt_Rhine-Main "Frankfurt Rhine-Main") with a population of 5.5 million. Other important cities in the region are [Wiesbaden](/wiki/Wiesbaden "Wiesbaden") (capital of [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse")), [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz") (capital of [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate")), [Darmstadt](/wiki/Darmstadt "Darmstadt"), [Offenbach am Main](/wiki/Offenbach_am_Main "Offenbach am Main"), [Hanau](/wiki/Hanau "Hanau"), [Aschaffenburg](/wiki/Aschaffenburg "Aschaffenburg"), [Bad Homburg vor der Höhe](/wiki/Bad_Homburg_vor_der_H%C3%B6he "Bad Homburg vor der Höhe"), [Rüsselsheim](/wiki/R%C3%BCsselsheim "Rüsselsheim"), [Wetzlar](/wiki/Wetzlar "Wetzlar") and [Marburg](/wiki/Marburg "Marburg").

### Districts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Districts")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Frankfurt_Subdivisions_boroughs.svg/250px-Frankfurt_Subdivisions_boroughs.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Subdivisions_boroughs.svg)

The 46 *Stadtteile* (city districts) of central Frankfurt, 2010

The city is divided into 46 city districts (*Stadtteile*), which are in turn divided into 121 city boroughs (*Stadtbezirke*) and 448 electoral districts (*Wahlbezirke*). The 46 city districts combine into 16 area districts (*[Ortsbezirke](/wiki/Ortsbezirk_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Ortsbezirk (Frankfurt am Main)")*), which each have a district committee and chairperson.

The largest city district by population and area is [Sachsenhausen](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Sachsenhausen (Frankfurt am Main)"), while the smallest is [Altstadt](/wiki/Altstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Altstadt (Frankfurt am Main)"), Frankfurt's historical center. Three larger city districts (Sachsenhausen, [Westend](/wiki/Westend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Westend (Frankfurt am Main)") and [Nordend](/wiki/Nordend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Nordend (Frankfurt am Main)")) are divided for administrative purposes into a northern (*-Nord*) and a southern (*-Süd*) part, respectively a western (*-West*) and an eastern (*-Ost*) part, but are generally considered as one city district (which is why often only 43 city districts are mentioned, even on the city's official website).[[37]](#cite_note-39)

Some larger housing areas are often falsely called city districts, even by locals, like Nordweststadt (part of [Niederursel](/wiki/Niederursel_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Niederursel (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Heddernheim](/wiki/Heddernheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Heddernheim (Frankfurt am Main)") and [Praunheim](/wiki/Praunheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Praunheim (Frankfurt am Main)")), Goldstein (part of [Schwanheim](/wiki/Schwanheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Schwanheim (Frankfurt am Main)")), Riedberg (part of [Kalbach-Riedberg](/wiki/Kalbach-Riedberg_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Kalbach-Riedberg (Frankfurt am Main)")) and [Europaviertel](/wiki/Europaviertel "Europaviertel") (part of [Gallus](/wiki/Gallus_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Gallus (Frankfurt am Main)")). The [Bankenviertel](/wiki/Bankenviertel "Bankenviertel") (*banking district*), Frankfurt's financial district, is also not an administrative city district (it covers parts of the western [Innenstadt](/wiki/Innenstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Innenstadt (Frankfurt am Main)") district, the southern [Westend](/wiki/Westend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Westend (Frankfurt am Main)") district and the eastern [Bahnhofsviertel](/wiki/Bahnhofsviertel_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bahnhofsviertel (Frankfurt am Main)") district).

Many city districts are incorporated suburbs (*[Vororte](/wiki/Vororte "Vororte")*) or were previously independent cities, such as [Höchst](/wiki/H%C3%B6chst_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Höchst (Frankfurt am Main)"). Some like [Nordend](/wiki/Nordend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Nordend (Frankfurt am Main)") and [Westend](/wiki/Westend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Westend (Frankfurt am Main)") arose during the rapid growth of the city in the [Gründerzeit](/wiki/Gr%C3%BCnderzeit "Gründerzeit") following the [Unification of Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany"), while others were formed from territory which previously belonged to other city district(s), such as [Dornbusch](/wiki/Dornbusch_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Dornbusch (Frankfurt am Main)") and [Riederwald](/wiki/Riederwald_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Riederwald (Frankfurt am Main)").

### History of incorporations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: History of incorporations")]

Until the year 1877 the city's territory consisted of the present-day inner-city districts of [Altstadt](/wiki/Altstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Altstadt (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Innenstadt](/wiki/Innenstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Innenstadt (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Bahnhofsviertel](/wiki/Bahnhofsviertel_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bahnhofsviertel (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Gutleutviertel](/wiki/Gutleutviertel_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Gutleutviertel (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Gallus](/wiki/Gallus_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Gallus (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Westend](/wiki/Westend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Westend (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Nordend](/wiki/Nordend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Nordend (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Ostend](/wiki/Ostend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Ostend (Frankfurt am Main)") and [Sachsenhausen](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Sachsenhausen (Frankfurt am Main)").

At the end of the 19th century the city of Frankfurt was enlarged. The central train station was built in 1888 to support the incorporation of existing population centers.[[38]](#cite_note-40) [Bornheim](/wiki/Bornheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bornheim (Frankfurt am Main)") was incorporated in 1877, followed by [Bockenheim](/wiki/Bockenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bockenheim (Frankfurt am Main)") in 1895. [Seckbach](/wiki/Seckbach_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Seckbach (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Niederrad](/wiki/Niederrad_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Niederrad (Frankfurt am Main)") and [Oberrad](/wiki/Oberrad_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Oberrad (Frankfurt am Main)") followed on 1 July 1900. The *Landkreis Frankfurt* was finally dispersed on 1 April 1910, and therefore [Berkersheim](/wiki/Berkersheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Berkersheim (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Bonames](/wiki/Bonames_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bonames (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Eckenheim](/wiki/Eckenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Eckenheim (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Eschersheim](/wiki/Eschersheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Eschersheim (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Ginnheim](/wiki/Ginnheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Ginnheim (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Hausen](/wiki/Hausen_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Hausen (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Heddernheim](/wiki/Heddernheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Heddernheim (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Niederursel](/wiki/Niederursel_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Niederursel (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Praunheim](/wiki/Praunheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Praunheim (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Preungesheim](/wiki/Preungesheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Preungesheim (Frankfurt am Main)") and [Rödelheim](/wiki/R%C3%B6delheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Rödelheim (Frankfurt am Main)") joined the city. In the same year a new city district, [Riederwald](/wiki/Riederwald_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Riederwald (Frankfurt am Main)"), was created on territory that had formerly belonged to Seckbach and Ostend.

On 1 April 1928 the City of [Höchst](/wiki/H%C3%B6chst_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Höchst (Frankfurt am Main)") became part of Frankfurt, as well as its city districts [Sindlingen](/wiki/Sindlingen_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Sindlingen (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Unterliederbach](/wiki/Unterliederbach_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Unterliederbach (Frankfurt am Main)") and [Zeilsheim](/wiki/Zeilsheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Zeilsheim (Frankfurt am Main)"). Simultaneously the *Landkreis Höchst* was dispersed with its member cities either joining Frankfurt ([Fechenheim](/wiki/Fechenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Fechenheim (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Griesheim](/wiki/Griesheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Griesheim (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Nied](/wiki/Nied_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Nied (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Schwanheim](/wiki/Schwanheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Schwanheim (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Sossenheim](/wiki/Sossenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Sossenheim (Frankfurt am Main)")) or joining the newly established *Landkreis* of [Main-Taunus-Kreis](/wiki/Main-Taunus-Kreis "Main-Taunus-Kreis"). [Dornbusch](/wiki/Dornbusch_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Dornbusch (Frankfurt am Main)") became a city district in 1946. It was created on territory that had formerly belonged to Eckenheim and Ginnheim.

On 1 August 1972, Hesse's smaller suburbs of [Harheim](/wiki/Harheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Harheim (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Kalbach](/wiki/Kalbach_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Kalbach (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Nieder-Erlenbach](/wiki/Nieder-Erlenbach_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Nieder-Erlenbach (Frankfurt am Main)"), and [Nieder-Eschbach](/wiki/Nieder-Eschbach_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Nieder-Eschbach (Frankfurt am Main)") became districts while other neighboring suburbs chose to join the Main-Taunus-Kreis, the [Landkreis Offenbach](/wiki/Landkreis_Offenbach "Landkreis Offenbach"), the [Kreis Groß-Gerau](/wiki/Kreis_Gro%C3%9F-Gerau "Kreis Groß-Gerau"), the [Hochtaunuskreis](/wiki/Hochtaunuskreis "Hochtaunuskreis"), the [Main-Kinzig-Kreis](/wiki/Main-Kinzig-Kreis "Main-Kinzig-Kreis") or the [Wetteraukreis](/wiki/Wetteraukreis "Wetteraukreis"). [Bergen-Enkheim](/wiki/Bergen-Enkheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bergen-Enkheim (Frankfurt am Main)") was the last suburb to become part of Frankfurt on 1 January 1977.

[Flughafen](/wiki/Flughafen_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Flughafen (Frankfurt am Main)") became an official city district in 1979. It covers the area of Frankfurt Airport that had belonged to Sachsenhausen and the neighboring city of [Mörfelden-Walldorf](/wiki/M%C3%B6rfelden-Walldorf "Mörfelden-Walldorf").

Frankfurt's youngest city district is [Frankfurter Berg](/wiki/Frankfurter_Berg "Frankfurter Berg"). It was part of Bonames until 1996. Kalbach was officially renamed [Kalbach-Riedberg](/wiki/Kalbach-Riedberg "Kalbach-Riedberg") in 2006 because of the large residential housing development in the area known as Riedberg.

### Neighboring districts and cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Neighboring districts and cities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/DSC08349_Blick_von_Burgruine_Falkenstein_auf_Frankfurt_und_Mainebene.jpg/250px-DSC08349_Blick_von_Burgruine_Falkenstein_auf_Frankfurt_und_Mainebene.jpg)](/wiki/File:DSC08349_Blick_von_Burgruine_Falkenstein_auf_Frankfurt_und_Mainebene.jpg)

View from [Falkenstein Castle](/wiki/Falkenstein_Castle_(Taunus) "Falkenstein Castle (Taunus)") to Frankfurt and parts of its [agglomeration](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area"). On the left in the foreground [Kronberg Castle](/wiki/Kronberg_Castle "Kronberg Castle") can be seen.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Locator_map_of_Planungsverband_Ballungsraum_Frankfurt_Rhein-Main_in_Hesse.svg/250px-Locator_map_of_Planungsverband_Ballungsraum_Frankfurt_Rhein-Main_in_Hesse.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Locator_map_of_Planungsverband_Ballungsraum_Frankfurt_Rhein-Main_in_Hesse.svg)

Frankfurt urban area within [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse")

To the west Frankfurt borders the [administrative district](/wiki/Districts_of_Germany "Districts of Germany") (*[Landkreis](/wiki/List_of_rural_districts_of_Germany "List of rural districts of Germany")*) of [Main-Taunus-Kreis](/wiki/Main-Taunus-Kreis "Main-Taunus-Kreis") with towns such as [Hattersheim am Main](/wiki/Hattersheim_am_Main "Hattersheim am Main"), [Kriftel](/wiki/Kriftel "Kriftel"), [Hofheim am Taunus](/wiki/Hofheim_am_Taunus "Hofheim am Taunus"), [Kelkheim](/wiki/Kelkheim_(Taunus) "Kelkheim (Taunus)"), [Liederbach am Taunus](/wiki/Liederbach_am_Taunus "Liederbach am Taunus"), [Sulzbach](/wiki/Sulzbach_(Taunus) "Sulzbach (Taunus)"), [Schwalbach am Taunus](/wiki/Schwalbach_am_Taunus "Schwalbach am Taunus") and [Eschborn](/wiki/Eschborn "Eschborn"); to the northwest the [Hochtaunuskreis](/wiki/Hochtaunuskreis "Hochtaunuskreis") with [Steinbach](/wiki/Steinbach_(Taunus) "Steinbach (Taunus)"), [Oberursel (Taunus)](/wiki/Oberursel_(Taunus) "Oberursel (Taunus)") and [Bad Homburg vor der Höhe](/wiki/Bad_Homburg_vor_der_H%C3%B6he "Bad Homburg vor der Höhe"); to the north the [Wetteraukreis](/wiki/Wetteraukreis "Wetteraukreis") with [Karben](/wiki/Karben "Karben") and [Bad Vilbel](/wiki/Bad_Vilbel "Bad Vilbel"); to the northeast the [Main-Kinzig-Kreis](/wiki/Main-Kinzig-Kreis "Main-Kinzig-Kreis") with [Niederdorfelden](/wiki/Niederdorfelden "Niederdorfelden") and [Maintal](/wiki/Maintal "Maintal"); to the southeast the city of [Offenbach am Main](/wiki/Offenbach_am_Main "Offenbach am Main"); to the south the [Kreis Offenbach](/wiki/Offenbach_(district) "Offenbach (district)") with [Neu-Isenburg](/wiki/Neu-Isenburg "Neu-Isenburg") and to the southwest the [Kreis Groß-Gerau](/wiki/Gro%C3%9F-Gerau_(district) "Groß-Gerau (district)") with [Mörfelden-Walldorf](/wiki/M%C3%B6rfelden-Walldorf "Mörfelden-Walldorf"), [Rüsselsheim](/wiki/R%C3%BCsselsheim "Rüsselsheim") and [Kelsterbach](/wiki/Kelsterbach "Kelsterbach").

Together with these towns (and some larger nearby towns, e.g., [Hanau](/wiki/Hanau "Hanau"), [Rodgau](/wiki/Rodgau "Rodgau"), [Dreieich](/wiki/Dreieich "Dreieich"), [Langen](/wiki/Langen,_Hesse "Langen, Hesse")) Frankfurt forms a contiguous built-up urban area called *Stadtregion Frankfurt* which is not an official administrative district. The urban area had an estimated population of 2.3 million in 2010, and is the [13th-largest urban area in the EU](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_in_the_European_Union "List of urban areas in the European Union").

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Climate")]

Frankfurt has a [temperate](/wiki/Temperateness "Temperateness") [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfb*, [Trewartha](/wiki/Trewartha_climate_classification "Trewartha climate classification"): *Dobk*). Its climate features cool winters with frequent rain showers and overcast skies, and warm summers. The average annual temperature is 11.4 °C (52.5 °F), with monthly mean temperatures ranging from 2.7 °C (36.9 °F) in January to 20.8 °C (69.4 °F) in July. The city is one of the warmest in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") in winter.[[39]](#cite_note-41)

The [growing season](/wiki/Growing_season "Growing season") is longer when compared to the rest of Germany, thus resulting in an early arrival of springtime in the region, with trees typically leafing out already toward the end of March.

Winters in Frankfurt are generally mild or at least not freezing with a small possibility of snow, especially in January and February but dark and often overcast. Frankfurt is, on average, covered with snow only for around 10 to 20 days per year.[[40]](#cite_note-42) The temperature falls below 0 °C on about 64 days and the daily maximum stays below freezing for about 10 days on average per year.

Because of the mild climate in the region, there are some well-known wine regions in the vicinity such as [Rhenish Hesse](/wiki/Rhenish_Hesse "Rhenish Hesse"), [Rheingau](/wiki/Rheingau "Rheingau"), [Franconia (wine region)](/wiki/Franconia_(wine_region) "Franconia (wine region)") and [Bergstraße (route)](/wiki/Bergstra%C3%9Fe_(route) "Bergstraße (route)"). There is also a microclimate on the northern bank of the river [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)") which allows [palms](/wiki/Palm_tree "Palm tree"), [fig trees](/wiki/Fig_trees "Fig trees"), [lemon trees](/wiki/Lemon_trees "Lemon trees") and southern European plants to grow in that area. The area is called the "[Nizza](/wiki/Nizza,_Frankfurt "Nizza, Frankfurt")" (the German word for the southern French town [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice")) and is one of the biggest parks with Mediterranean vegetation north of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps").[[41]](#cite_note-43)

With a population of 763,380 (2019) within its administrative boundaries[[47]](#cite_note-49) and of 2,300,000 in the actual [urban area](/wiki/List_of_urban_areas_of_the_European_Union "List of urban areas of the European Union"),[[48]](#cite_note-European_Union-50) Frankfurt is the [fifth-largest city in Germany](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Germany_by_population "List of cities in Germany by population"), after [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), Hamburg, [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") and [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne"). Central Frankfurt has been a *[Großstadt](/wiki/Gro%C3%9Fstadt "Großstadt")* (a city with at least 100,000 residents by definition) since 1875. With 414,576 residents in 1910, it was the ninth largest city in Germany and the number of inhabitants grew to 553,464 before [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). After the war, at the end of the year 1945, the number had dropped to 358,000. In the following years, the population grew again and reached an all-time-high of 691,257 in 1963. It dropped again to 592,411 in 1986 but has increased since then. According to the demographic forecasts for central Frankfurt, the city will have a population up to 813,000 within its administrative boundaries in 2035[[49]](#cite_note-51) and more than 2.5 million inhabitants in its urban area.

As of 2015, Frankfurt had 1909 [ultra high-net-worth individuals](/wiki/Ultra_high-net-worth_individual "Ultra high-net-worth individual"), the sixth-highest number of any city in the world.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

During the 1970s, the state government of [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse") wanted to expand the city's administrative boundaries to include the entire urban area. This would have made Frankfurt officially the second-largest city in Germany after Berlin with up to 3 million inhabitants.[[51]](#cite_note-53) However, because local authorities did not agree, the administrative territory is still much smaller than its actual urban area.

### Immigration and foreign nationals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Immigration and foreign nationals")]

According to data from the city [register of residents](/wiki/Resident_registration#Germany "Resident registration"), 51.2% of the population had a *migration background* as of 2015, which means that a person or at least one of their parents was born with foreign citizenship. For the first time, a majority of the city residents had an at least part non-German background.[[52]](#cite_note-54) Moreover, three of four children in the city under the age of six had full or partial immigrant backgrounds,[[53]](#cite_note-55) and 27.7% of residents had a foreign citizenship.[[54]](#cite_note-56)

According to statistics, 46.7% of immigrants in Frankfurt come from other countries in the EU; 24.5% come from European countries that are not part of the EU; 15.7% come from Asia (including Western Asia and South Asia); 7.3% come from Africa; 3.4% come from North America (including the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") and Central America); 0.2% come from [Australia](/wiki/Australia_(continent) "Australia (continent)") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"); 2.3% come from South America; and 1.1% come from Pacific island nations. Because of this the city is often considered to be a multicultural city, and has been compared to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and [London](/wiki/London "London").[[55]](#cite_note-57)

Frankfurt has the largest Moroccan community in Germany, numbering about 8,000 people, and the [Rhine-Main area](/wiki/Frankfurt_Rhine-Main "Frankfurt Rhine-Main") has about 20,000. Many [Moroccans](/wiki/Moroccans "Moroccans") came as guest workers in the 1970s.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Religion")]

Frankfurt is historically a [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant")-dominated city. However, during the 19th century, an increasing number of [Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Germany "Roman Catholicism in Germany") moved to Frankfurt. As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit), the largest Christian denominations were Catholicism (22.7% of the population) and Protestantism, especially Lutheranism (19.4%).[[58]](#cite_note-60)

Main article: [Jewish community of Frankfurt am Main](/w/index.php?title=Jewish_community_of_Frankfurt_am_Main&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jewish community of Frankfurt am Main (page does not exist)")

The Jewish community has a history dating back to medieval times and has always ranked among the largest in Germany. Over 7,200 inhabitants are affiliated with the Jewish community, making it the third largest in Germany after [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") and [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich").[[28]](#cite_note-frankfurt-tourismus.de-30) Frankfurt has four active synagogues.[[59]](#cite_note-Jewish_Community_Frankfurt_am_Main-61)

Frankfurt has a Muslim community estimated at 12% in 2006.[[60]](#cite_note-62)
According to calculations based on census data for 21 countries of origin, the number of Muslim migrants in Frankfurt amounted to about 84,000 in 2011, making up 12.6% of the population.[[61]](#cite_note-63) The most prevalent countries of origin were [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") and [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"). The Ahmadiyya Noor Mosque, constructed in 1959, is the city's largest mosque and the third largest in Germany. In 2020, the number of Muslims in Frankfurt's total population was estimated at 18%.[[62]](#cite_note-64)
