### Landtag election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Landtag election")]

For elections to the [Hesse State Parliament](/wiki/Landtag_of_Hesse "Landtag of Hesse"), Frankfurt am Main is split up into six constituencies. In total 15 delegates represent the city in the [Landtag](/wiki/Landtag "Landtag") in [Wiesbaden](/wiki/Wiesbaden "Wiesbaden"). The last election took place in October 2018. Six members of parliament were directly elected in their respective constituencies: Uwe Serke (CDU, Frankfurt am Main I), [Miriam Dahlke](/wiki/Miriam_Dahlke "Miriam Dahlke") (Greens, Frankfurt am Main II), Ralf-Norbert Bartel (CDU, Frankfurt am Main III), [Michael Boddenberg](/wiki/Michael_Boddenberg "Michael Boddenberg") (CDU, Frankfurt am Main IV), Markus Bocklet (Greens, Frankfurt am Main V) and Boris Rhein (CDU, Frankfurt am Main VI).

Delegates from Frankfurt often serve high-ranking positions in Hessian politics, e.g. [Michael Boddenberg](/wiki/Michael_Boddenberg "Michael Boddenberg") is Hessian Minister of Finance and Boris Rhein was elected President of the Landtag of Hesse in 2019.

### German federal election

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: German federal election")]

For federal elections which are held every four years, Frankfurt is split up into two constituencies. In the [German federal election 2017](/wiki/2017_German_federal_election "2017 German federal election"), [Matthias Zimmer](/wiki/Matthias_Zimmer "Matthias Zimmer") (CDU) and [Bettina Wiesmann](/wiki/Bettina_Wiesmann "Bettina Wiesmann") were elected to the Bundestag by directe mandate in [Frankfurt am Main I](/wiki/Frankfurt_am_Main_I_(electoral_district) "Frankfurt am Main I (electoral district)") and [Frankfurt am Main II](/wiki/Frankfurt_am_Main_II_(electoral_district) "Frankfurt am Main II (electoral district)") respectively. [Nicola Beer](/wiki/Nicola_Beer "Nicola Beer") (FDP), [Achim Kessler](/wiki/Achim_Kessler "Achim Kessler") (Linke), [Ulli Nissen](/wiki/Ulli_Nissen "Ulli Nissen") (SPD) and [Omid Nouripour](/wiki/Omid_Nouripour "Omid Nouripour") (Greens) were elected as well.

Nicola Beer resigned as a member of parliament in 2019 following her [election to the European Parliament](/wiki/2019_European_Parliament_election "2019 European Parliament election") where she now serves as vice president.

## Economy and business

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Economy and business")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Deutsche-boerse-parkett-ffm001.jpg/250px-Deutsche-boerse-parkett-ffm001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deutsche-boerse-parkett-ffm001.jpg)

[Deutsche Börse](/wiki/Deutsche_B%C3%B6rse "Deutsche Börse") trading floor in Frankfurt

Frankfurt is one of the world's most important financial hubs and Germany's financial capital, followed by [Hamburg](/wiki/Hamburg "Hamburg") and [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart"). Frankfurt was ranked eighth at the [International Financial Centers Development Index](/wiki/Financial_centre#Xinhua–Dow_Jones_Index_(2010–2014) "Financial centre") (2013), eighth at the [Worldwide Centres of Commerce Index](/wiki/Worldwide_Centres_of_Commerce_Index "Worldwide Centres of Commerce Index") (2008), ninth at the [Global Financial Centres Index](/wiki/Global_Financial_Centres_Index "Global Financial Centres Index") (September 2013),[[63]](#cite_note-65) tenth at the [Global Power City Index](/wiki/Global_city#Global_Power_City_Index "Global city") (2011), 11th at the [Global City Competitiveness Index](/wiki/Global_city#Global_City_Competitiveness_Index "Global city") (2012), 12th at the Innovation Cities Index (2011),[[64]](#cite_note-66) 14th at the [World City Survey](/wiki/Global_city#World_City_Survey "Global city") (2011) and 23rd at the [Global Cities Index](/wiki/Global_city#Global_Cities_Index_(Kearney) "Global city") (2012).[[65]](#cite_note-Foreign_Policy-67)

The city's importance as a financial hub has risen since the [eurozone crisis](/wiki/Eurozone_crisis "Eurozone crisis"). Indications are the establishment of two institutions of the [European System of Financial Supervisors](/wiki/European_System_of_Financial_Supervisors "European System of Financial Supervisors") ([European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority](/wiki/European_Insurance_and_Occupational_Pensions_Authority "European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority") and [European Systemic Risk Board](/wiki/European_Systemic_Risk_Board "European Systemic Risk Board")) in 2011, and the entry into force in 2014 of [European Banking Supervision](/wiki/European_Banking_Supervision "European Banking Supervision"), by which the European Central Bank has become the central supervisory authority for the euro area banking sector.

According to an annual study by [Cushman & Wakefield](/wiki/Cushman_%26_Wakefield "Cushman & Wakefield"), the European Cities Monitor (2010), Frankfurt has been one of the top three cities for international companies in Europe, after London and Paris, since the survey started in 1990.[[66]](#cite_note-68) It is the only German city considered to be an [alpha world city (category 3)](/wiki/Global_city#GaWC_study "Global city") as listed by the [Loughborough University](/wiki/Loughborough_University "Loughborough University") group's 2010 inventory,[[67]](#cite_note-Beaverstock-69) which was a promotion from the group's 2008 inventory when it was ranked as an alpha minus world city (category 4).[[68]](#cite_note-70)

With over 922 jobs per 1,000 inhabitants, Frankfurt has the highest concentration of jobs in Germany. On work days and Saturdays, one million people commute from all over the [Rhein-Main-Area](/wiki/Rhein-Main_Region "Rhein-Main Region"). The GRP per capita was €96,670 in 2019.[[69]](#cite_note-71)

The city is expected to benefit from international banks relocating jobs from London to Frankfurt as a result of [Brexit](/wiki/Brexit "Brexit") to retain access to the EU market.[[70]](#cite_note-72)[[71]](#cite_note-Bloomberg.com-73) Thus far, Morgan Stanley, Citigroup Inc., Standard Chartered Plc and Nomura Holdings Inc. announced they would move their EU headquarters to Frankfurt.[[71]](#cite_note-Bloomberg.com-73)

### Central banks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Central banks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Europ%C3%A4ische_Zentralbank_Frankfurt.jpg/250px-Europ%C3%A4ische_Zentralbank_Frankfurt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Europ%C3%A4ische_Zentralbank_Frankfurt.jpg)

The new headquarters of the European Central Bank in the Ostend district

Frankfurt is home to two important [central banks](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank"): the German Bundesbank and the European Central Bank (ECB).[[72]](#cite_note-74)

#### European Central Bank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: European Central Bank")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Euro-Symbol_von_Ottmar_H%C3%B6rl%2C_vor_dem_Geb%C3%A4ude_der_EZB_in_Frankfurt_am_Main.jpg/250px-Euro-Symbol_von_Ottmar_H%C3%B6rl%2C_vor_dem_Geb%C3%A4ude_der_EZB_in_Frankfurt_am_Main.jpg)](/wiki/File:Euro-Symbol_von_Ottmar_H%C3%B6rl,_vor_dem_Geb%C3%A4ude_der_EZB_in_Frankfurt_am_Main.jpg)

[Euro-Skulptur](/wiki/Euro-Skulptur "Euro-Skulptur")

The European Central Bank (*Europäische Zentralbank*) is one of the world's most important central banks with a balance sheet total of around 7 trillion.[[73]](#cite_note-75) The ECB sets monetary policy for the Eurozone, consisting of 19 [EU member states](/wiki/Member_state_of_the_European_Union "Member state of the European Union") that have adopted the [Euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro") (€) as their common currency. From 1998 the ECB Headquarters have been located in Frankfurt, first in the [Eurotower](/wiki/Eurotower_(Frankfurt) "Eurotower (Frankfurt)") at Willy-Brandt-Platz and in two other nearby high-rises. The new [Seat of the European Central Bank](/wiki/Seat_of_the_European_Central_Bank "Seat of the European Central Bank") in the [Ostend](/wiki/Ostend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Ostend (Frankfurt am Main)") district, consisting of the former wholesale market hall (*[Großmarkthalle](/wiki/Gro%C3%9Fmarkthalle "Großmarkthalle")*) and a newly built 185-meter skyscraper, was completed in late 2014. The new building complex was designed to accommodate up to 2,300 ECB personnel. The location is a few kilometers away from downtown and borders an industrial area as well as the Osthafen (*East Harbor*), It was primarily chosen because of its large premises which allows the ECB to install security arrangements without high fences.

The city honors the importance of the ECB by officially using the slogan "The City of the Euro" since 1998.

#### Deutsche Bundesbank

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Deutsche Bundesbank")]

The [Deutsche Bundesbank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bundesbank "Deutsche Bundesbank") (German Federal Bank), located in [Ginnheim](/wiki/Bockenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bockenheim (Frankfurt am Main)"), was established in 1957 as the central bank for the Federal Republic of Germany. Until the euro (€) was introduced in 1999, the Deutsche Bundesbank was responsible for the monetary policy of Germany and for the German currency, the [Deutsche Mark](/wiki/Deutsche_Mark "Deutsche Mark") (DM). The Bundesbank was greatly respected for its control of inflation through the second half of the 20th century. Today the Bundesbank is an integral part of the [European System of Central Banks](/wiki/European_System_of_Central_Banks "European System of Central Banks") (ESCB) which is formed by all 27 EU member states. Its balance sheet total is around 2,7 trillion,[[74]](#cite_note-76) making it the 4th biggest central bank.

### Commercial banks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Commercial banks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Deutsche_Bank_Taunusanlage.jpg/250px-Deutsche_Bank_Taunusanlage.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deutsche_Bank_Taunusanlage.jpg)

[Deutsche Bank Twin Towers](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank_Twin_Towers "Deutsche Bank Twin Towers")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/DZ_BANK_AG_Frankfurt.jpg/250px-DZ_BANK_AG_Frankfurt.jpg)](/wiki/File:DZ_BANK_AG_Frankfurt.jpg)

[Westend Tower](/wiki/Westend_Tower "Westend Tower"), also known as *Westendstraße 1* or *Crown Tower*, headquarters of [DZ Bank](/wiki/DZ_Bank "DZ Bank")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/OpernturmVoll.jpg/250px-OpernturmVoll.jpg)](/wiki/File:OpernturmVoll.jpg)

[Opernturm](/wiki/Opernturm "Opernturm"), headquarters of UBS Germany, at the Opernplatz

In 2010, 63 national and 152 international banks had a registered office, including the headquarters of the major German banks, as well as 41 offices of international banks.[[12]](#cite_note-Internetredaktion-2012-14) Frankfurt is therefore known as Bankenstadt ("City of the banks") and nicknamed "Mainhattan" (a [portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of the local [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)") river and [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") in New York City) or "Bankfurt". 73,200 people were employed at banks in 2010.

* [Deutsche Bank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank "Deutsche Bank") — Germany's largest [commercial bank](/wiki/Commercial_bank "Commercial bank"). It had 15% share of private customers and total assets of €1,900 billion in 2010. Deutsche Bank ranks among the 30 largest banks in the world and the ten largest banks in Europe.[[75]](#cite_note-77) Deutsche Bank is listed on the [DAX](/wiki/DAX "DAX"), the [stock market index](/wiki/Stock_market_index "Stock market index") of the 30 largest German business companies at the Frankfurt Stock Exchange. In November 2010 Deutsche Bank bought the majority of shares of competitor [Postbank](/wiki/Deutsche_Postbank "Deutsche Postbank"). Its [headquarters](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank_Twin_Towers "Deutsche Bank Twin Towers") are located at Taunusanlage in the financial district.
* [DZ Bank](/wiki/DZ_Bank "DZ Bank") — Central institution for more than 900 [co-operative banks](/wiki/Cooperative_banking "Cooperative banking") (*[Volksbanken und Raiffeisenbanken](/wiki/Bundesverband_der_Deutschen_Volksbanken_und_Raiffeisenbanken "Bundesverband der Deutschen Volksbanken und Raiffeisenbanken")*) and their 12,000 branch offices in Germany and is a corporate and investment bank. It is Germany's second-largest bank (total assets: €509 billion). The DZ Bank Group defines itself primarily as a service provider for the local Volksbanken and Raiffeisenbanken and their 30 million clients. The DZ Bank headquarters are the [Westend Tower](/wiki/Westend_Tower "Westend Tower") and the [City-Haus](/wiki/City-Haus "City-Haus") at Platz der Republik. The DZ Bank Group includes [Union Investment](/wiki/Union_Investment "Union Investment"), DVB Bank and [Reisebank](/wiki/Reisebank "Reisebank"), which are also headquartered in Frankfurt.
* [KfW Bankengruppe](/wiki/KfW "KfW") — Government-owned development bank formed in 1948 as part of the [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan"). KfW provides loans for approved purposes at lower rates than commercial banks, especially to medium-sized businesses. With total assets of €507 billion (2017), it is Germany's third-largest bank. The KfW headquarters are located in the [Westend](/wiki/Westend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Westend (Frankfurt am Main)") district at Bockenheimer Landstraße and Senckenberganlage.
* [Commerzbank](/wiki/Commerzbank "Commerzbank") — Germany's fourth-largest bank by total assets (2017). In 2009, Commerzbank merged with competitor [Dresdner Bank](/wiki/Dresdner_Bank "Dresdner Bank"), then the third-largest German bank. Due to the merger and the higher credit risks, Commerzbank was 25% [nationalized](/wiki/Nationalization "Nationalization") during the [Great Recession](/wiki/Great_Recession "Great Recession"). It is listed in the DAX. Its headquarters are at [Commerzbank Tower](/wiki/Commerzbank_Tower "Commerzbank Tower") (259 meters), the second-tallest building in the EU, at Kaiserplatz.
* [Landesbank Hessen-Thüringen](/wiki/Helaba "Helaba") – Landesbank Hessen-Thüringen, or short Helaba, is a commercial bank owned by the states of [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse") and [Thuringia](/wiki/Thuringia "Thuringia") (*[Landesbank](/wiki/Landesbank "Landesbank")*). As such, it is a service provider for the local [Sparkassen](/wiki/Sparkasse_(Germany) "Sparkasse (Germany)"). Helaba is one of nine *Landesbanken* and is the fifth-largest in Germany. It is located in the 200-metre-tall (660 ft) [Main Tower](/wiki/Main_Tower "Main Tower") in the financial district, the only skyscraper in Frankfurt with an observation desk open to the public.
* [DekaBank](/wiki/DekaBank "DekaBank") – DekaBank is the central asset manager of the *Sparkassen* in Germany. The headquarters of DekaBank are located at the [Trianon](/wiki/Trianon_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Trianon (Frankfurt am Main)") skyscraper at Mainzer Landstraße.
* [ING Diba](/wiki/ING_Group "ING Group") Germany – Germany's largest [direct bank](/wiki/Direct_bank "Direct bank"), headquartered in [Bockenheim](/wiki/Bockenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bockenheim (Frankfurt am Main)")

Other major German banks include [Frankfurter Volksbank](/wiki/Frankfurter_Volksbank "Frankfurter Volksbank"), the second-largest *Volksbank* in Germany, Frankfurter Sparkasse and old-established private banks such as [Bankhaus Metzler](/wiki/Metzler_Bank "Metzler Bank"), [Hauck & Aufhäuser](/wiki/Hauck_%26_Aufh%C3%A4user "Hauck & Aufhäuser") and [Delbrück Bethmann Maffei](/wiki/Bethmann_bank "Bethmann bank").

Many international banks have a registered or a representative office, e.g., [Credit Suisse](/wiki/Credit_Suisse "Credit Suisse"), [UBS](/wiki/UBS "UBS"), [Bank of America](/wiki/Bank_of_America "Bank of America"), [Morgan Stanley](/wiki/Morgan_Stanley "Morgan Stanley"), [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs"), [Merrill Lynch](/wiki/Merrill_Lynch "Merrill Lynch"), [JPMorgan Chase & Co.](/wiki/JPMorgan_Chase_%26_Co. "JPMorgan Chase & Co."), [Bank of China](/wiki/Bank_of_China "Bank of China"), [Banco do Brasil](/wiki/Banco_do_Brasil "Banco do Brasil"), [Itaú Unibanco](/wiki/Ita%C3%BA_Unibanco "Itaú Unibanco") [Société Générale](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_G%C3%A9n%C3%A9rale "Société Générale"), [BNP Paribas](/wiki/BNP_Paribas "BNP Paribas"), [SEB](/wiki/Skandinaviska_Enskilda_Banken "Skandinaviska Enskilda Banken"), [Royal Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Royal_Bank_of_Scotland "Royal Bank of Scotland") and [Barclays](/wiki/Barclays "Barclays").

### Frankfurt Stock Exchange

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Frankfurt Stock Exchange")]

Main article: [Frankfurt Stock Exchange](/wiki/Frankfurt_Stock_Exchange "Frankfurt Stock Exchange")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Bulle_und_B%C3%A4r_Frankfurt.jpg/250px-Bulle_und_B%C3%A4r_Frankfurt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bulle_und_B%C3%A4r_Frankfurt.jpg)

[Bull and bear](/wiki/Market_trend "Market trend") in front of the Frankfurt Stock Exchange

The Frankfurt Stock Exchange (*Frankfurter Wertpapierbörse*) began in the ninth century. By the 16th century Frankfurt had developed into an important European hub for trade fairs and financial services. Today the Frankfurt Stock Exchange is by far the largest in Germany, with a turnover of more than 90 percent of the German [stock market](/wiki/Stock_market "Stock market") and is the third-largest in Europe after the [London](/wiki/London_Stock_Exchange "London Stock Exchange") and the [Paris](/wiki/Euronext_Paris "Euronext Paris"). The most important [stock market index](/wiki/Stock_market_index "Stock market index") is the [DAX](/wiki/DAX "DAX"), the index of the 30 largest German business companies listed at the stock exchange. The stock exchange is owned and operated by [Deutsche Börse](/wiki/Deutsche_B%C3%B6rse "Deutsche Börse"), which is itself listed in the DAX. Deutsche Börse also owns the European [futures exchange](/wiki/Futures_exchange "Futures exchange") [Eurex](/wiki/Eurex "Eurex") and clearing company [Clearstream](/wiki/Clearstream "Clearstream").
On 1 February 2012 European Commission blocked the proposed merger of Deutsche Börse and [NYSE Euronext](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange"). "The merger between Deutsche Börse and NYSE Euronext would have led to a near-monopoly in European financial derivatives worldwide. These markets are at the heart of the financial system and it is crucial for the whole European economy that they remain competitive. We tried to find a solution, but the remedies offered fell far short of resolving the concerns."[[76]](#cite_note-78) European competition commissioner [Joaquín Almunia](/wiki/Joaqu%C3%ADn_Almunia "Joaquín Almunia") said.

It is located downtown at the Börsenplatz. Deutsche Börse's headquarters are formally registered in Frankfurt, but it moved most of its employees to a high-rise called "The Cube" in [Eschborn](/wiki/Eschborn "Eschborn") in 2010, primarily due to significantly lower local [corporate taxes](/wiki/Corporate_tax "Corporate tax").

### Frankfurt Trade Fair

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Frankfurt Trade Fair")]

Main article: [Frankfurt Trade Fair](/wiki/Frankfurt_Trade_Fair "Frankfurt Trade Fair")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Messefrankfurt-messeturm-und-messebesucher.jpg/250px-Messefrankfurt-messeturm-und-messebesucher.jpg)](/wiki/File:Messefrankfurt-messeturm-und-messebesucher.jpg)

[Messeturm](/wiki/Messeturm "Messeturm") seen from the trade fair premises

Frankfurt Trade Fair (*Messe Frankfurt*) has the third-largest exhibition site in the world with a total of 578,000 m2 (6,220,000 sq ft). The trade fair premises are located in the western part between [Bockenheim](/wiki/Bockenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bockenheim (Frankfurt am Main)"), the [Westend](/wiki/Westend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Westend (Frankfurt am Main)") and the [Gallus](/wiki/Gallus_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Gallus (Frankfurt am Main)") district. It houses ten exhibition halls with a total of 321,754 m2 (3,463,330 sq ft) of space and 96,078 m2 (1,034,170 sq ft) of outdoor space.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Frankfurt and the river [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") tributary [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)") acquired transnational economic importance in the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). In 1337 the [Holy Roman Emperor Louis IV of Bavaria](/wiki/Louis_IV,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Louis IV, Holy Roman Emperor") issued a diploma privilege so that any other city was not allowed to establish a market or trade fair if this could harm the Frankfurt Trade Fair in any way. This imperial backing allowed Frankfurt to retain the commanding importance of the Frankfurt Trade Fair for centuries. In the Middle Ages the full range of manufactured and semi-processed commodities were trade at Frankfurt Fairs.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

Hosted in Frankfurt are the [Frankfurt Book Fair](/wiki/Frankfurt_Book_Fair "Frankfurt Book Fair") (*Frankfurter Buchmesse*), the world's largest book fair, the Ambiente Frankfurt, the world's largest [consumer goods](/wiki/Final_good "Final good") fair, the Achema, the world's largest plant engineering fair, and many more like Paperworld, Christmasworld, Beautyworld, Tendence Lifestyle or Light+Building. Messe Frankfurt GmbH, the owner and operator company, organized 87 exhibitions in 2010, 51 thereof in foreign countries. It is one of the largest trade fair companies with commercial activities in over 150 countries.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Aviation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Aviation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/2010-07-21_A380_LH_D-AIMB_EDDF_06.jpg/250px-2010-07-21_A380_LH_D-AIMB_EDDF_06.jpg)](/wiki/File:2010-07-21_A380_LH_D-AIMB_EDDF_06.jpg)

Two Lufthansa Airbus A380s at [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport")

[Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport") is one of the busiest airports in the world and is also the single largest place of work in Germany with over 500 companies which employ 71,500 people (2010).[[78]](#cite_note-80)

[Fraport](/wiki/Fraport "Fraport") is the owner and operator of Frankfurt Airport. It is the airport's second-largest employer (19,800 workers in 2010).[[79]](#cite_note-81) Fraport also operates other airports worldwide, e.g., [King Abdulaziz International Airport](/wiki/King_Abdulaziz_International_Airport "King Abdulaziz International Airport") in [Jeddah](/wiki/Jeddah "Jeddah"), [Jorge Chávez International Airport](/wiki/Jorge_Ch%C3%A1vez_International_Airport "Jorge Chávez International Airport") in Lima and [Antalya Airport](/wiki/Antalya_Airport "Antalya Airport").

The largest company at Frankfurt Airport is Lufthansa, Germany's [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") and Europe's largest airline. Lufthansa employs 35,000 people in Frankfurt.[[80]](#cite_note-82)[[81]](#cite_note-83) The Lufthansa Aviation Center (LAC) is the main operation base of Lufthansa at Frankfurt Airport. The airport serves as Lufthansa's primary [hub](/wiki/Airline_hub "Airline hub") with 157 worldwide destinations (compared to 110 destinations at [Munich Airport](/wiki/Munich_Airport "Munich Airport"), Lufthansa's second-largest hub). [Lufthansa Cargo](/wiki/Lufthansa_Cargo "Lufthansa Cargo") is based in Frankfurt and operates its largest cargo center (LCC) at Frankfurt Airport. [Lufthansa Flight Training](/wiki/Lufthansa_Flight_Training "Lufthansa Flight Training") is also based here.

[Condor](/wiki/Condor_Flugdienst "Condor Flugdienst") is a German airline based at Frankfurt Airport.

### Other industries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Other industries")]

#### Accountancy and professional services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Accountancy and professional services")]

Three of the four largest international [accountancy](/wiki/Accountancy "Accountancy") and [professional services](/wiki/Professional_services "Professional services") firms *([Big Four](/wiki/Big_Four_(audit_firms) "Big Four (audit firms)"))* are present.

[PricewaterhouseCoopers](/wiki/PricewaterhouseCoopers "PricewaterhouseCoopers") (PwC) German headquarters are located at [Tower 185](/wiki/Tower_185 "Tower 185"). [KPMG](/wiki/KPMG "KPMG") moved its European Headquarters ([KPMG Europe LLP](/wiki/KPMG#Recent_history "KPMG")) to [The Squaire](/wiki/The_Squaire "The Squaire"). [Deloitte Touche Tohmatsu](/wiki/Deloitte_Touche_Tohmatsu "Deloitte Touche Tohmatsu") are present, while [Ernst & Young](/wiki/Ernst_%26_Young "Ernst & Young") is located in [Eschborn](/wiki/Eschborn "Eschborn").

#### Credit rating agencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Credit rating agencies")]

The three major international [credit rating agencies](/wiki/Credit_rating_agency "Credit rating agency") – [Standard & Poor's](/wiki/Standard_%26_Poor%27s "Standard & Poor's"), [Moody's](/wiki/Moody%27s "Moody's") and [Fitch Ratings](/wiki/Fitch_Group "Fitch Group") – have their German headquarters in Frankfurt.

#### Investment trust companies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Investment trust companies")]

DWS Investments is one of the largest [investment trust](/wiki/Investment_trust "Investment trust") company in Germany and manages €859 billion fund assets. It is one of the ten largest investment trust companies in the world.[[82]](#cite_note-84) Other large investment trust companies are [Universal Investment](/wiki/Universal_Investment "Universal Investment"),[[83]](#cite_note-85) Allianz Global Investors Europe (a division of [Allianz SE](/wiki/Allianz_SE "Allianz SE"), and a top-five global active investment manager), [Union Investment](/wiki/Union_Investment "Union Investment") and Deka Investmentfonds.

#### Management consultancies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Management consultancies")]

Many of the largest international [management consultancies](/wiki/Management_consulting "Management consulting") are represented, including [Arthur D. Little](/wiki/Arthur_D._Little "Arthur D. Little"), [McKinsey & Company](/wiki/McKinsey_%26_Company "McKinsey & Company"), [Boston Consulting Group](/wiki/Boston_Consulting_Group "Boston Consulting Group"), [Booz & Company](/wiki/Booz_%26_Company "Booz & Company"), [Oliver Wyman](/wiki/Oliver_Wyman "Oliver Wyman"), [Bearing Point](/wiki/Bearing_Point "Bearing Point"), [Capgemini](/wiki/Capgemini "Capgemini"), [Bain & Company](/wiki/Bain_%26_Company "Bain & Company") and [Roland Berger Strategy Consultants](/wiki/Roland_Berger_Strategy_Consultants "Roland Berger Strategy Consultants").

#### Real estate services companies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Real estate services companies")]

Located in Frankfurt are the German headquarters of [Jones Lang LaSalle](/wiki/Jones_Lang_LaSalle "Jones Lang LaSalle") and [BNP Paribas Real Estate](/wiki/BNP_Paribas_Real_Estate "BNP Paribas Real Estate").

#### Law firms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Law firms")]

Frankfurt has the highest concentration of lawyers in Germany, with one lawyer per 97 inhabitants (followed by [Düsseldorf](/wiki/D%C3%BCsseldorf "Düsseldorf") with a ratio of 1/117 and [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") with 1/124) in 2005.[[84]](#cite_note-86)

Most of the large international [law firms](/wiki/Law_firms "Law firms") maintain offices, among them [A&O Shearman](/wiki/A%26O_Shearman "A&O Shearman"), [Baker McKenzie](/wiki/Baker_McKenzie "Baker McKenzie"), [Bird & Bird](/wiki/Bird_%26_Bird "Bird & Bird"), [Clifford Chance](/wiki/Clifford_Chance "Clifford Chance"), [Cleary Gottlieb Steen & Hamilton](/wiki/Cleary_Gottlieb_Steen_%26_Hamilton "Cleary Gottlieb Steen & Hamilton"), [Debevoise & Plimpton](/wiki/Debevoise_%26_Plimpton "Debevoise & Plimpton"), [DLA Piper](/wiki/DLA_Piper "DLA Piper"), [Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer](/wiki/Freshfields_Bruckhaus_Deringer "Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer"), [Hogan Lovells](/wiki/Hogan_Lovells "Hogan Lovells"), [Jones Day](/wiki/Jones_Day "Jones Day"), [K&L Gates](/wiki/K%26L_Gates "K&L Gates"), [Latham & Watkins](/wiki/Latham_%26_Watkins "Latham & Watkins"), [Linklaters](/wiki/Linklaters "Linklaters"), [Mayer Brown](/wiki/Mayer_Brown "Mayer Brown"), [Milbank, Tweed, Hadley & McCloy](/wiki/Milbank,_Tweed,_Hadley_%26_McCloy "Milbank, Tweed, Hadley & McCloy"), [Norton Rose](/wiki/Norton_Rose "Norton Rose"), [Sidley Austin](/wiki/Sidley_Austin "Sidley Austin"), [SJ Berwin](/wiki/SJ_Berwin "SJ Berwin"), [Skadden, Arps, Slate, Meagher & Flom](/wiki/Skadden,_Arps,_Slate,_Meagher_%26_Flom "Skadden, Arps, Slate, Meagher & Flom"), [Sullivan & Cromwell](/wiki/Sullivan_%26_Cromwell "Sullivan & Cromwell"), [Taylor Wessing](/wiki/Taylor_Wessing "Taylor Wessing") and [White & Case](/wiki/White_%26_Case "White & Case").

#### Advertising agencies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Advertising agencies")]

According to a ranking of German [FOCUS magazine](/wiki/Focus_(German_magazine) "Focus (German magazine)") (November 2007) seven of the 48 largest [advertising agencies](/wiki/Advertising_agency "Advertising agency") in Germany are based in Frankfurt, including [Havas](/wiki/Havas "Havas"), [Dentsu](/wiki/Dentsu_Aegis_Network "Dentsu Aegis Network"), [McCann-Erickson](/wiki/McCann-Erickson "McCann-Erickson"), [Saatchi & Saatchi](/wiki/Saatchi_%26_Saatchi "Saatchi & Saatchi"), [JWT](/wiki/J._Walter_Thompson "J. Walter Thompson"), and [Publicis](/wiki/Publicis "Publicis").[[85]](#cite_note-87) Frankfurt is a media [business cluster](/wiki/Business_cluster "Business cluster"). Around 570 companies of the advertising industry and 270 [public relations](/wiki/Public_relations "Public relations") companies are located in Frankfurt.

#### Food

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Food")]

Frankfurt is home to the German headquarters of [Nestlé](/wiki/Nestl%C3%A9 "Nestlé"), the world's largest [food company](/wiki/Food_industry "Food industry"), located in [Niederrad](/wiki/Niederrad_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Niederrad (Frankfurt am Main)"). Other important food companies are [Ferrero SpA](/wiki/Ferrero_SpA "Ferrero SpA") (German headquarters) and Radeberger Gruppe KG, the largest private brewery group in Germany.

#### Automotive

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Automotive")]

The South-Korean [automobile manufacturer](/wiki/Automotive_industry "Automotive industry") [Kia Motors](/wiki/Kia_Motors "Kia Motors") moved its European headquarters to Frankfurt in 2007. In the same year, Italian manufacturer [Fiat](/wiki/Fiat "Fiat") opened its new German headquarters. The automotive supplier [Continental AG](/wiki/Continental_AG "Continental AG") has the headquarters and a major manufacturing plant of its Chassis & Safety division (formerly ITT Automotive) located in Frankfurt Rödelheim.

#### Construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Construction")]

Some of the largest German construction companies have offices, e.g., [Bilfinger Berger](/wiki/Bilfinger_Berger "Bilfinger Berger"), [Hochtief](/wiki/Hochtief "Hochtief"), Züblin and BAM Deutschland.

#### Property and real estate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Property and real estate")]

Frankfurt has Germany's highest concentration of homeowners. This is partly attributed to the financial sector, but also to its cosmopolitan nature, with expatriates and immigrants representing one-fourth of its population. For this reason, Frankfurt's property market often operates differently than the rest of the country where the prices are generally flatter.

#### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Frankfurt is one of Germany's leading tourist destinations. In addition to its infrastructure and economy, its diversity supports a vibrant cultural scene. This blend of attractions led 4.3 million tourists (2012) to visit Frankfurt.[[86]](#cite_note-88) The Hotels in central Frankfurt offer 34,000 beds in 228 hotels, of which 13 are luxury hotels and 46 are first-class hotels.[[87]](#cite_note-89)

Industriepark Höchst

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Mainova_-_M%C3%BCllheizkraftwerk_Nordweststadt_-_Frankfurt_am_Main.jpg/250px-Mainova_-_M%C3%BCllheizkraftwerk_Nordweststadt_-_Frankfurt_am_Main.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mainova_-_M%C3%BCllheizkraftwerk_Nordweststadt_-_Frankfurt_am_Main.jpg)

[Mainova](/wiki/Mainova "Mainova") heating plant

Frankfurt is home to companies from the chemical, transportation, telecommunication and energy industries. Some of the larger companies are:

* Industriepark Höchst — An [industrial park](/wiki/Industrial_park "Industrial park") in [Höchst](/wiki/H%C3%B6chst_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Höchst (Frankfurt am Main)"). It is one of Germany's largest with over 90 companies from the [pharmaceutical](/wiki/Pharmaceutical_industry "Pharmaceutical industry"), the [chemical](/wiki/Chemical_industry "Chemical industry") and the [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology") industry, including [Celanese](/wiki/Celanese "Celanese"), [Clariant](/wiki/Clariant "Clariant"), [BASF](/wiki/BASF "BASF"), [Merck KGaA](/wiki/Merck_KGaA "Merck KGaA") and [Siemens](/wiki/Siemens "Siemens"). It was founded by chemical company [Hoechst AG](/wiki/Hoechst_AG "Hoechst AG") in 1874. At the beginning of the 1980s Hoechst AG was the largest pharmaceutical corporation and Industriepark Höchst was known as "the pharmacy of the world". Hoechst AG merged with [Rhône-Poulenc](/wiki/Rh%C3%B4ne-Poulenc "Rhône-Poulenc") to become [Aventis](/wiki/Aventis "Aventis") in 1999 and in 2004 Aventis merged with [Sanofi-Synthélabo](/wiki/Sanofi-Synth%C3%A9labo "Sanofi-Synthélabo") to become [Sanofi-Aventis](/wiki/Sanofi-Aventis "Sanofi-Aventis"). In 2005, around 22,000 people worked at Industriepark Höchst. In 2011, [Ticona](/wiki/Ticona "Ticona") now part of [Celanese](/wiki/Celanese "Celanese"), an international manufacturer of engineering [polymers](/wiki/Polymer "Polymer"), moved to Industriepark Höchst.
* Deutsche Bahn – Deutsche Bahn subsidiaries [DB Fernverkehr](/wiki/DB_Fernverkehr "DB Fernverkehr"), [DB Regio](/wiki/DB_Regio "DB Regio"), [DB Stadtverkehr](/wiki/DB_Stadtverkehr "DB Stadtverkehr"), [DB Netz](/wiki/DB_Netz "DB Netz"), [DB Schenker](/wiki/DB_Schenker "DB Schenker") and the corporate development department of Deutsche Bahn are Frankfurt-based.
* [Deutsche Telekom](/wiki/Deutsche_Telekom "Deutsche Telekom") – Deutsche Telekom's subsidiary [T-Systems](/wiki/T-Systems "T-Systems") is Frankfurt-based.
* [COLT](/wiki/COLT_Group "COLT Group") – telecommunications company with Frankfurt-based German headquarters
* [Nintendo](/wiki/Nintendo "Nintendo") — In 2014, [Nintendo of Europe](/wiki/Nintendo_of_Europe "Nintendo of Europe") moved its headquarters from [Großostheim](/wiki/Gro%C3%9Fostheim "Großostheim") to Frankfurt.[[88]](#cite_note-90)
* [CenturyLink](/wiki/CenturyLink "CenturyLink") — [internet service provider](/wiki/Internet_service_provider "Internet service provider") with German headquarters in Frankfurt
* [DE-CIX](/wiki/DE-CIX "DE-CIX") – Frankfurt is an important location for [electronic communication](/wiki/Electronic_communication "Electronic communication"), especially the Internet. It is home to [DE-CIX](/wiki/DE-CIX "DE-CIX"), the world's largest [internet exchange point](/wiki/Internet_exchange_point "Internet exchange point").
* [Mainova](/wiki/Mainova "Mainova") – The largest regional [energy supplier](/wiki/Energy_supply "Energy supply") in Germany with about one million customers in [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse"). It provides electricity, gas, heat and water. Its headquarters are Frankfurt-based.

In addition, several [cloud](/wiki/Cloud_computing "Cloud computing") and [fintech](/wiki/Financial_technology "Financial technology") [startups](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company") have their headquarters in Frankfurt.[[89]](#cite_note-91)

### Urban area (suburban) businesses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Urban area (suburban) businesses")]

Within Frankfurt's urban area are several important companies.

The business hub of Eschborn is located right at Frankfurt's city limits in the west and attracts businesses with significantly lower [corporate taxes](/wiki/Corporate_tax "Corporate tax") compared to Frankfurt. Major companies in Eschborn include [Ernst & Young](/wiki/Ernst_%26_Young "Ernst & Young"), [Vodafone Germany](/wiki/Vodafone_Germany "Vodafone Germany"), [Randstad NV](/wiki/Randstad_NV "Randstad NV") and VR Leasing. [Deutsche Börse](/wiki/Deutsche_B%C3%B6rse "Deutsche Börse") moved most of its employees to Eschborn in 2010.

[Rüsselsheim](/wiki/R%C3%BCsselsheim "Rüsselsheim") is internationally known for its [automobile manufacturer](/wiki/Automotive_industry "Automotive industry") [Opel](/wiki/Opel "Opel"), one of the biggest automobile manufacturers in Germany. With 20,000 employees in 2003, Opel was one of the five largest employers in [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse").

[Offenbach am Main](/wiki/Offenbach_am_Main "Offenbach am Main") is home to the European headquarters of automobile manufacturer [Hyundai Motor Company](/wiki/Hyundai_Motor_Company "Hyundai Motor Company"), to the German headquarters of automobile manufacturer [Honda](/wiki/Honda "Honda"), to [Honeywell Germany](/wiki/Honeywell "Honeywell") and to [Deutscher Wetterdienst](/wiki/Deutscher_Wetterdienst "Deutscher Wetterdienst"), the central scientific agency that monitors weather and [meteorological](/wiki/Meteorology "Meteorology") conditions over Germany.

Two [DAX](/wiki/DAX "DAX") companies are located in [Bad Homburg vor der Höhe](/wiki/Bad_Homburg_vor_der_H%C3%B6he "Bad Homburg vor der Höhe"), [Fresenius SE & Co. KGaA](/wiki/Fresenius_(company) "Fresenius (company)") and [Fresenius Medical Care](/wiki/Fresenius_Medical_Care "Fresenius Medical Care"). Other major companies are [Hewlett-Packard](/wiki/Hewlett-Packard "Hewlett-Packard"), [Bridgestone](/wiki/Bridgestone "Bridgestone"), Deutsche Leasing and Basler Versicherungen.

[Kronberg im Taunus](/wiki/Kronberg_im_Taunus "Kronberg im Taunus") is home of the German headquarters of automobile manufacturer [Jaguar Cars](/wiki/Jaguar_Cars "Jaguar Cars") as well as the German headquarters of [Accenture](/wiki/Accenture "Accenture").

[Lufthansa Systems](/wiki/Lufthansa_Systems "Lufthansa Systems"), a subsidiary of [Lufthansa](/wiki/Lufthansa "Lufthansa"), is located in [Kelsterbach](/wiki/Kelsterbach "Kelsterbach").

[LSG Sky Chefs](/wiki/LSG_Sky_Chefs "LSG Sky Chefs"), another subsidiary of Lufthansa, is located in [Neu-Isenburg](/wiki/Neu-Isenburg "Neu-Isenburg").

The German headquarters of [Thomas Cook Group](/wiki/Thomas_Cook_Group "Thomas Cook Group") are based in [Oberursel](/wiki/Oberursel_(Taunus) "Oberursel (Taunus)").

[Langen](/wiki/Langen,_Hesse "Langen, Hesse") is home to [Deutsche Flugsicherung](/wiki/Deutsche_Flugsicherung "Deutsche Flugsicherung"), the German [air traffic control](/wiki/Air_traffic_control "Air traffic control").

## Landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Landmarks")]

### Römer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Römer")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Frankfurter_R%C3%B6mer.jpg/250px-Frankfurter_R%C3%B6mer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurter_R%C3%B6mer.jpg)

[Römer](/wiki/R%C3%B6mer "Römer"), the city hall

[Römer](/wiki/R%C3%B6mer "Römer"), the German word for [Roman](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), is a complex of nine houses that form the Frankfurt [city hall](/wiki/City_hall "City hall") (*Rathaus*). The houses were acquired by the city council in 1405 from a wealthy merchant family. The middle house became the city hall and was later connected with its neighbors. The *Kaisersaal* ("Emperor's Hall") is located on the upper floor and is where the newly crowned emperors held their banquets. The Römer was partially destroyed in World War II and later rebuilt. The surrounding square, the Römerberg, is named after the city hall.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Dom-Roemer-Projekt-Huehnermarkt-06-2018-Ffm-Altstadt-10008-9.jpg/250px-Dom-Roemer-Projekt-Huehnermarkt-06-2018-Ffm-Altstadt-10008-9.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dom-Roemer-Projekt-Huehnermarkt-06-2018-Ffm-Altstadt-10008-9.jpg)

The [New Frankfurt Old Town](/wiki/Dom-R%C3%B6mer_Project "Dom-Römer Project") was completed in 2018, including 15 reconstructed historical buildings

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Frankfurt_Am_Main-Samstagsberg_von_Suedwesten-20110705.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Am_Main-Samstagsberg_von_Suedwesten-20110705.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Am_Main-Samstagsberg_von_Suedwesten-20110705.jpg)

[Römerberg](/wiki/R%C3%B6merberg_(Frankfurt) "Römerberg (Frankfurt)")

The former [Altstadt](/wiki/Altstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Altstadt (Frankfurt am Main)") (old town) quarter between the Römer and the Frankfurt Cathedral was redeveloped as the [Dom-Römer Quarter](/wiki/Dom-R%C3%B6mer_Project "Dom-Römer Project") from 2012 to 2018, including 15 [reconstructions](/wiki/Reconstruction_(architecture) "Reconstruction (architecture)") of historical buildings that were destroyed during World War II.

### Frankfurt Cathedral

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Frankfurt Cathedral")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Frankfurter_Dom_Eiserner_Steg.jpg/250px-Frankfurter_Dom_Eiserner_Steg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurter_Dom_Eiserner_Steg.jpg)

Frankfurt Cathedral

[Frankfurt Cathedral](/wiki/Frankfurt_Cathedral "Frankfurt Cathedral") (*Frankfurter Dom*) is not a cathedral, but the main Catholic church, dedicated to [St. Bartholomew](/wiki/Bartholomew_the_Apostle "Bartholomew the Apostle"). The [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") building was constructed in the 14th and 15th centuries on the foundation of an earlier church from the [Merovingian](/wiki/Merovingian "Merovingian") time. From 1356 onwards, kings of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") were elected in this church, and from 1562 to 1792, [Roman-German](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") emperors were crowned there.

Since the 18th century, St. Bartholomew's has been called *Dom*, although it was never a bishop's seat. In 1867 it was destroyed by fire and rebuilt in its present style. It was again partially destroyed in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and rebuilt in the 1950s. Its height is 95 m (312 ft). The cathedral tower has a viewing platform open to the public at a height of 66 m (217 ft), accessed through a narrow spiral staircase with 386 steps.

### Central Station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Central Station")]

[Frankfurt Central Station](/wiki/Frankfurt_Central_Station "Frankfurt Central Station") (*Frankfurt Hauptbahnhof*), which opened in 1888, was built as the central train station for Frankfurt to replace three smaller downtown train stations and to boost the needed capacity for travellers. It was constructed as a [terminus station](/wiki/Terminal_station "Terminal station") and was the largest train station in Europe by floor area until 1915 when [Leipzig Central Station](/wiki/Leipzig_Hauptbahnhof "Leipzig Hauptbahnhof") was opened. Its three main halls were constructed in a [neo-Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_Revival_architecture "Renaissance Revival architecture") style, while the later enlargement with two outer halls in 1924 was constructed in [neoclassical](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture") style.

### Alte Oper

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: Alte Oper")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Alte_Oper_Frankfurt_Winter_2008.jpg/250px-Alte_Oper_Frankfurt_Winter_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alte_Oper_Frankfurt_Winter_2008.jpg)

[Alte Oper](/wiki/Alte_Oper "Alte Oper"), now a concert hall, at Opernplatz

The [Alte Oper](/wiki/Alte_Oper "Alte Oper") is a former [opera house](/wiki/Opera_house "Opera house"), hence the name "Old Opera". The opera house was built in 1880 by architect Richard Lucae. It was one of the major opera houses in Germany until it was heavily damaged in World War II. Until the late 1970s, it was a ruin, nicknamed "Germany's most beautiful ruin". Former Frankfurt [Lord Mayor](/wiki/Lord_Mayor "Lord Mayor") Rudi Arndt called for blowing it up in the 1960s, which earned him the nickname "Dynamite-Rudi". (Later on, Arndt said he never had meant his suggestion seriously.)

Public pressure led to its refurbishment and reopening in 1981. Today, it functions as a famous concert hall, while operas are performed at the "new" Frankfurt Opera. The inscription on the [frieze](/wiki/Frieze "Frieze") of the Alte Oper says: "*Dem Wahren, Schönen, Guten*" ("To the true, the beautiful, the good").

### St. Paul's Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: St. Paul's Church")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Frankfurt_Paulskirche_au%C3%9Fen_2011a.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Paulskirche_au%C3%9Fen_2011a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Paulskirche_au%C3%9Fen_2011a.jpg)

[St. Paul's Church](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_Church,_Frankfurt "St. Paul's Church, Frankfurt")

[St. Paul's Church](/wiki/St._Paul%27s_Church,_Frankfurt "St. Paul's Church, Frankfurt") is a national historic monument in Germany because it was the seat of the first democratically elected parliament in 1848. It was established in 1789 as a [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") church, but was not completed until 1833. Its importance has its roots in the [Frankfurt Parliament](/wiki/Frankfurt_Parliament "Frankfurt Parliament"), which met in the church during the revolutionary years of 1848/49 in order to write a constitution for a united Germany. The attempt failed because the monarchs of [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") and Austria did not want to lose power. In 1849, Prussian troops ended the democratic experiment by force and the parliament dissolved; the building was once more used for religious services.

St. Paul's was partially destroyed in World War II with its interior significantly damaged. It was quickly and symbolically rebuilt after the war albeit with modern alterations particularly to the interior; today it is used mainly for exhibitions and events.

### Archäologischer Garten Frankfurt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Archäologischer Garten Frankfurt")]

The Archaeological Garden contains small parts of the oldest recovered buildings: an ancient Roman settlement and the Frankfurt Royal Palace (*Kaiserpfalz Frankfurt*) from the sixth century. The garden is located between the Römerberg and the cathedral. It was discovered after World War II when the area was heavily bombed and later partly rebuilt. The remains were preserved and are now open to the public. From 2013 until 2015 an event building, the Stadthaus ("City house"), has been built on top of the garden, but it remains open to the public free of charge.

### Haus Wertheim

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=53 "Edit section: Haus Wertheim")]

Wertheim House is the only [timbered house](/wiki/Timber_framing "Timber framing") in the [Altstadt](/wiki/Altstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Altstadt (Frankfurt am Main)") district that survived the heavy bombings of World War II undamaged. It is located on the Römerberg next to the Historical Museum.

### Saalhof

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Saalhof")]

The [Saalhof](/w/index.php?title=Saalhof&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saalhof (page does not exist)") is the oldest conserved building in the [Altstadt](/wiki/Altstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Altstadt (Frankfurt am Main)") district and dates to the 12th century. It was used as an exhibition hall by Dutch [clothiers](/wiki/Cloth_merchant "Cloth merchant") when trade fairs were held during the 14th and 15th centuries. The Saalhof was partly destroyed in World War II and later rebuilt. Today it serves as a part of the [Historical Museum](/wiki/Historical_museum,_Frankfurt "Historical museum, Frankfurt").

### Eiserner Steg

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Eiserner Steg")]

The Eiserner Steg (Iron Bridge) is a pedestrian-only bridge across the Main that connects Römerberg and Sachsenhausen. It was built in 1868 and was the second bridge to cross the river. After World War II, when it was blown up by the [Wehrmacht](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht"), it was quickly rebuilt in 1946. Today some 10,000 people cross the bridge on a daily basis.

### German National Library

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: German National Library")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Deutsche_Nationalbibliothek%2C_Frankfurt.jpg/250px-Deutsche_Nationalbibliothek%2C_Frankfurt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Deutsche_Nationalbibliothek,_Frankfurt.jpg)

DNB building in Frankfurt

Frankfurt is one of two sites of the [German National Library](/wiki/German_National_Library "German National Library") (*Deutsche Nationalbibliothek*), the other being [Leipzig](/wiki/Leipzig "Leipzig"). The Deutsche Nationalbibliothek is a [universal library](/wiki/Universal_library "Universal library") in Germany. Its task, unique in Germany, is to collect, permanently archive, comprehensively document and record bibliographically all German and German-language publications from 1913 on, foreign publications about Germany, translations of German works and the works of German-speaking emigrants published abroad between 1933 and 1945, and to make them available to the public.[[90]](#cite_note-92)

### Eschenheimer Turm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=57 "Edit section: Eschenheimer Turm")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Frankfurt_Nextower.20130514.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Nextower.20130514.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Nextower.20130514.jpg)

[Eschenheimer Turm](/wiki/Eschenheimer_Turm "Eschenheimer Turm")

The Eschenheim Tower (*[Eschenheimer Turm](/wiki/Eschenheimer_Turm "Eschenheimer Turm")*) was erected at the beginning of the 15th century and served as a city gate as part of late-medieval fortifications. It is the oldest and most unaltered building in the [Innenstadt](/wiki/Innenstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Innenstadt (Frankfurt am Main)") district.

### St. Catherine's Church

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=58 "Edit section: St. Catherine's Church")]

[St. Catherine's Church](/wiki/St._Catherine%27s_Church,_Frankfurt "St. Catherine's Church, Frankfurt") is the largest [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant_Church_in_Germany "Protestant Church in Germany") church, dedicated to [Catherine of Alexandria](/wiki/Catherine_of_Alexandria "Catherine of Alexandria"), a [martyred](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr") early Christian saint. It is located downtown at the entrance to the Zeil, the central pedestrian shopping street.

### Hauptwache

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=59 "Edit section: Hauptwache")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Frankfurt_Main_Hauptwache_Katharinenkirche_20120707.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Main_Hauptwache_Katharinenkirche_20120707.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Main_Hauptwache_Katharinenkirche_20120707.jpg)

[Hauptwache](/wiki/Hauptwache_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Hauptwache (Frankfurt am Main)") and [St. Catherine's Church](/wiki/St._Catherine%27s_Church,_Frankfurt "St. Catherine's Church, Frankfurt")

Although today [Hauptwache](/wiki/Hauptwache_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Hauptwache (Frankfurt am Main)") is mostly associated with the inner-city underground train station [of the same name](/wiki/Frankfurt_(Main)_Hauptwache_station "Frankfurt (Main) Hauptwache station"), the name originates from a [baroque](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") building on the square above the station. The Hauptwache building was constructed in 1730 and was used as a prison, therefore the name that translates as "main guard-house". Today the square surrounding the building is also called "Hauptwache" (formal "An der Hauptwache"). It is situated downtown opposite to St. Catherine's Church.

### Frankfurter Hof

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=60 "Edit section: Frankfurter Hof")]

The Frankfurter Hof is a landmark downtown hotel at Kaiserplatz, built from 1872 to 1876. It is part of [Steigenberger Hotels](/wiki/Steigenberger_Hotels "Steigenberger Hotels") group and is considered the city's most prestigious.

### St. Leonhard

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=61 "Edit section: St. Leonhard")]

[St. Leonhard](/wiki/St._Leonhard,_Frankfurt "St. Leonhard, Frankfurt"), on the Main close to the bridge Eiserner Steg, is a Catholic [late Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") [hall church](/wiki/Hall_church "Hall church"), derived from a [Romanesque style](/wiki/Romanesque_architecture "Romanesque architecture") [basilica](/wiki/Basilica "Basilica") beginning in 1425. It is the only one of nine churches in the Old Town that survived World War II almost undamaged. The parish serves the English-speaking community. The church has been under restoration from 2011 until 2019.[[91]](#cite_note-93)

### Gründerzeit quarters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=62 "Edit section: Gründerzeit quarters")]

Around the city centre there are widespread quarters full of [Gründerzeit architecture](/wiki/Gr%C3%BCnderzeit#Design_and_architecture "Gründerzeit"). Buildings of that type often sport richly decorated façades in the form of Historicism such as Gothic Revival, Renaissance Revival, German Renaissance and Baroque Revival.

### Timber-framed old towns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=63 "Edit section: Timber-framed old towns")]

Around the city center of Frankfurt are several former independent villages, now part of the city, with [timber-framed](/wiki/Timber-framed "Timber-framed") centres and even whole old towns like [Höchst](/wiki/H%C3%B6chst_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Höchst (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Seckbach](/wiki/Seckbach_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Seckbach (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Niederursel](/wiki/Niederursel "Niederursel") and [Bergen-Enkheim](/wiki/Bergen-Enkheim "Bergen-Enkheim"). Overall there are around 14.500 buildings in Frankfurt built before 1919[[34]](#cite_note-zensus2011.de-36) and around 3.000 of them are [timber framed](/wiki/Timber_framed "Timber framed") houses.[[92]](#cite_note-94)

## Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=64 "Edit section: Architecture")]

### 20th-century architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=65 "Edit section: 20th-century architecture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2f/DSC09338_Frank_Gehry_Siedlung_Schwanheim_Goldstein.jpg/250px-DSC09338_Frank_Gehry_Siedlung_Schwanheim_Goldstein.jpg)](/wiki/File:DSC09338_Frank_Gehry_Siedlung_Schwanheim_Goldstein.jpg)

Public housing designed by [Frank Gehry](/wiki/Frank_Gehry "Frank Gehry") in [Frankfurt-Schwanheim](/wiki/Schwanheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Schwanheim (Frankfurt am Main)") (1994-1996)

* [Frauenfriedenskirche](/wiki/Frauenfriedenskirche "Frauenfriedenskirche") and [Holy Cross Church](/wiki/Holy_Cross_Church,_Frankfurt-Bornheim "Holy Cross Church, Frankfurt-Bornheim")), both consecrated in 1929, are examples of early modernist church buildings during the time of the [New Frankfurt](/wiki/New_Frankfurt "New Frankfurt").
* [Großmarkthalle](/wiki/Gro%C3%9Fmarkthalle "Großmarkthalle"), built 1926–1928 as a part of the [New Frankfurt](/wiki/New_Frankfurt "New Frankfurt")-project, the former wholesale market hall was repaired after the second world war and integrated into the new [seat of the European Central Bank](/wiki/Seat_of_the_European_Central_Bank "Seat of the European Central Bank") between 2010 and 2014.
* [Goethe House](/wiki/Goethe_House "Goethe House"), rebuilt 1947. The birthplace of [Johann Wolfgang von Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe") from 1749 was destroyed in World War II and then rebuilt true to the original.
* Junior-Haus, built 1951, an example of early post-World War II architecture located at Kaiserplatz.
* Bayer-Haus, built 1952, another example of early post-World War II architecture.
* [Museum für angewandte Kunst](/wiki/Museum_f%C3%BCr_angewandte_Kunst_Frankfurt "Museum für angewandte Kunst Frankfurt"), built 1985, designed by [Richard Meier](/wiki/Richard_Meier "Richard Meier").
* Gehry-Siedlung: A public housing area in [Frankfurt-Schwanheim](/wiki/Schwanheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Schwanheim (Frankfurt am Main)") designed by [Frank Gehry](/wiki/Frank_Gehry "Frank Gehry") (built 1994-1996).
* [IG Farben Building](/wiki/IG_Farben_Building "IG Farben Building") – Also known as Poelzig Building (*Poelzig-Bau*) after its architect [Hans Poelzig](/wiki/Hans_Poelzig "Hans Poelzig"), it was built from 1928 to 1930 as the corporate headquarters of [I.G. Farbenindustrie AG](/wiki/IG_Farben "IG Farben"). It is located in the [Westend](/wiki/Westend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Westend (Frankfurt am Main)") district and borders [Grüneburgpark](/wiki/Gr%C3%BCneburgpark "Grüneburgpark") in the west. Upon its completion, the complex was the [largest office building](/wiki/List_of_largest_buildings_in_the_world "List of largest buildings in the world") in Europe and remained so until the 1950s. The building served as headquarters for research projects relating to the development of [synthetic oil](/wiki/Synthetic_oil "Synthetic oil") and [rubber](/wiki/Synthetic_rubber "Synthetic rubber") and the manufacturing of [magnesium](/wiki/Magnesium "Magnesium"), lubricating oil, explosives, [methanol](/wiki/Methanol "Methanol"), and [Zyklon B](/wiki/Zyklon_B "Zyklon B"), the lethal gas used in [concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps").[[93]](#cite_note-Robertson,_Staun-95)[[94]](#cite_note-Historical_Frankfurt-96) After World War II, it served as the headquarters for the [Supreme Allied Command](/wiki/Supreme_Allied_Commander "Supreme Allied Commander") and from 1949 to 1952 the High Commissioner for Germany (HICOG). It became the principal location for implementing the [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan"), which largely financed the post-war reconstruction of Europe. The state apparatus of the [Federal German Government](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") was devised there. It served as the headquarters for the [US Army's V Corps](/wiki/U.S._V_Corps "U.S. V Corps") and the Northern Area Command (NACOM) until 1995 when the US Army returned control of the IG Farben Building to the German government. It was purchased on behalf of the [Goethe University Frankfurt](/wiki/Goethe_University_Frankfurt "Goethe University Frankfurt") by the state of [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse"). In October 2001 it became part of the Westend Campus of Goethe University.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/IG_Farben_Gebaeude_Uni_Frankfurt.jpg/960px-IG_Farben_Gebaeude_Uni_Frankfurt.jpg)](/wiki/File:IG_Farben_Gebaeude_Uni_Frankfurt.jpg "IG Farben Building, now the central lecture building of the Westend Campus of the Goethe University")

[IG Farben Building](/wiki/IG_Farben_Building "IG Farben Building"), now the central lecture building of the Westend Campus of the Goethe University

### 21st-century architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=66 "Edit section: 21st-century architecture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/The_Squaire%2C_Frankfurt_am_Main-1060.jpg/250px-The_Squaire%2C_Frankfurt_am_Main-1060.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Squaire,_Frankfurt_am_Main-1060.jpg)

The Squaire in 2017

* Die Welle (*The Wave*), built 1998–2003, a complex of three wavelike-formed office buildings next to the Opernplatz.
* Alte Stadtbibliothek, rebuilt 2003–2005, reconstruction of the old public library house originally built 1820–1825.
* [Palais Thurn und Taxis](/wiki/Palais_Thurn_und_Taxis "Palais Thurn und Taxis"), rebuilt 2004–2009, reconstruction of a palace originally built 1731–1739.
* [MyZeil](/wiki/MyZeil "MyZeil"), built 2004–2009, shopping mall at the Zeil with an imposing vaulted glass-structure.
* [The Squaire](/wiki/The_Squaire "The Squaire") ([portmanteau](/wiki/Portmanteau "Portmanteau") of *square* and *air*), also known as Airrail Center Frankfurt, is a 660 m (2,165.35 ft) long and 45 m (147.64 ft) tall office building located at [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport"). It was built from 2006 to 2011 on top of an existing railway station ([Frankfurt Airport long distance Station](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport_long-distance_station "Frankfurt Airport long-distance station")) and has a connecting bridge to Terminal 1 for pedestrians. Its total of 140,000 m2 (1,506,947 sq ft) rentable floor space makes it Germany's largest office building.

### Skyscrapers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=67 "Edit section: Skyscrapers")]

See also: [List of tallest buildings in Frankfurt](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Frankfurt "List of tallest buildings in Frankfurt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Frankfurt_am_Main_Skyline_%2851395421615%29.jpg/960px-Frankfurt_am_Main_Skyline_%2851395421615%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_am_Main_Skyline_(51395421615).jpg "View of Frankfurt from the Taunus")

View of Frankfurt from the [Taunus](/wiki/Taunus "Taunus")

Frankfurt is unique among German cities with regards to [skyscrapers](/wiki/Skyscraper "Skyscraper"). Since the 1970s some of the tallest buildings in Germany have been built along the Mainzer Landstrasse. The [Main Tower](/wiki/Main_Tower "Main Tower") was completed in 1999 with its top floor skydeck and penthouse restaurant designed to be open to tourists.[[95]](#cite_note-97) Frankfurt hosts 20 out of Germany's 21 [skyscrapers](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Germany "List of tallest buildings in Germany"). Most skyscrapers and high-rise office buildings are located in the financial district ([Bankenviertel](/wiki/Bankenviertel "Bankenviertel")), around the trade fair premises ([Europaviertel](/wiki/Europaviertel_(Frankfurt) "Europaviertel (Frankfurt)")) and at [Mainzer Landstraße](/wiki/Mainzer_Landstra%C3%9Fe "Mainzer Landstraße") between Opernplatz and Platz der Republik, which connects the two areas.

The 20 skyscrapers are:

* [Commerzbank Tower](/wiki/Commerzbank_Tower "Commerzbank Tower"), 259.0 m (849.74 ft) – designed by [Norman Foster](/wiki/Norman_Foster "Norman Foster"), when the tower was completed in 1997 it was the tallest building in Europe with 259 meters. It is still the tallest building in Frankfurt and constructed in steel.[[96]](#cite_note-98)
* [Messeturm](/wiki/Messeturm "Messeturm"), 256.5 m (841.54 ft) – The EU's third-tallest building, the tallest building in Europe 1990–1997; main tenant is [Goldman Sachs](/wiki/Goldman_Sachs "Goldman Sachs") (Germany).
* [Four I](/wiki/Four_(Frankfurt) "Four (Frankfurt)"), 233.0 m (764.44 ft)
* [Westend Tower](/wiki/Westend_Tower "Westend Tower"), 208.0 m (682.41 ft) – [DZ Bank](/wiki/DZ_Bank "DZ Bank") headquarters
* [Main Tower](/wiki/Main_Tower "Main Tower"), 200.0 m (656.17 ft) – [Landesbank Hessen-Thüringen](/wiki/Helaba "Helaba") and [Standard & Poor's](/wiki/Standard_%26_Poor%27s "Standard & Poor's") (Germany) headquarters
* [Tower 185](/wiki/Tower_185 "Tower 185"), 200.0 m (656.17 ft) – [PricewaterhouseCoopers](/wiki/PricewaterhouseCoopers "PricewaterhouseCoopers") (Germany) headquarters
* ONE, 191 m (626.64 ft)
* [Omniturm](/wiki/Omniturm "Omniturm"), 190.0 m (623.36 ft)
* [Trianon](/wiki/Trianon_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Trianon (Frankfurt am Main)"), 186.0 m (610.24 ft) – [DekaBank](/wiki/DekaBank "DekaBank") headquarters
* [Seat of the European Central Bank](/wiki/Seat_of_the_European_Central_Bank "Seat of the European Central Bank"), 185.0 m (606.96 ft) – [European Central Bank](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank") headquarters
* [Grand Tower](/wiki/Grand_Tower_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Grand Tower (Frankfurt am Main)"), 179.9 m (590.22 ft) – Tallest residential tower
* [Four I](/wiki/Four_(Frankfurt) "Four (Frankfurt)"), 179.0 m (587.27 ft) – Residential tower
* [Opernturm](/wiki/Opernturm "Opernturm"), 170.0 m (557.74 ft) – [UBS](/wiki/UBS "UBS") (Germany) headquarters
* [Taunusturm](/wiki/Taunusturm "Taunusturm"), 170.0 m (557.74 ft)
* [Silberturm](/wiki/Silberturm "Silberturm"), 166.3 m (545.60 ft) – Germany's tallest building 1978–1990, Main tenant is [Deutsche Bahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn").
* [Westend Gate](/wiki/Westend_Gate "Westend Gate"), 159.3 m (522.64 ft) – Germany's tallest building 1976–1978, Main tenant is [Marriott](/wiki/Marriott_International "Marriott International") Frankfurt Hotel.
* [Deutsche Bank I](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank_Twin_Towers "Deutsche Bank Twin Towers"), 155.0 m (508.53 ft) – [Deutsche Bank](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank "Deutsche Bank") headquarters
* [Deutsche Bank II](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank_Twin_Towers "Deutsche Bank Twin Towers"), 155.0 m (508.53 ft)
* [Marienturm](/wiki/Marienturm "Marienturm"), 155.0 m (508.53 ft)
* [Skyper](/wiki/Skyper "Skyper"), 153.8 m (504.59 ft) – Main tenant is DekaBank.

Other high-rise buildings include:

* [Eurotower](/wiki/Eurotower_(Frankfurt) "Eurotower (Frankfurt)"), 148.0 m (485.56 ft) – [Former European Central Bank headquarters](/wiki/European_Central_Bank "European Central Bank")
* One Forty West, 145 m (475.72 ft) – [Meliá Hotels International](/wiki/Meli%C3%A1_Hotels_International "Meliá Hotels International"), Residential
* [Frankfurter Büro Center](/wiki/Frankfurter_B%C3%BCro_Center "Frankfurter Büro Center"), 142.4 m (467.19 ft) – Main tenant is [Clifford Chance](/wiki/Clifford_Chance "Clifford Chance") (Germany).
* [City-Haus](/wiki/City-Haus "City-Haus"), 142.1 m (466.21 ft) – Main tenant is DZ Bank.
* [Gallileo](/wiki/Gallileo_(skyscraper) "Gallileo (skyscraper)"), 136.0 m (446.19 ft) – Main tenant is Commerzbank.
