### History of high-rise buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=68 "Edit section: History of high-rise buildings")]

See also: [List of tallest buildings in Frankfurt](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_Frankfurt "List of tallest buildings in Frankfurt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Skyline-sued-ffm002_henninger_turm.jpg/250px-Skyline-sued-ffm002_henninger_turm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skyline-sued-ffm002_henninger_turm.jpg)

The original Henninger Turm in 2007

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Frankfurt_Skyline_%2816259801511%29.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Skyline_%2816259801511%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Skyline_(16259801511).jpg)

Skyline at dusk, seen from Deutschherrnbrücke (2014)

[![Frankfurt Skyline at River Main](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/River_Main_Frankfurt.jpg/250px-River_Main_Frankfurt.jpg)](/wiki/File:River_Main_Frankfurt.jpg)

Bridges over River Main in Frankfurt 2023

The [Henninger Turm](/wiki/Henninger_Turm "Henninger Turm") was opened in the 1960s and the silo was topped by a [revolving restaurant](/wiki/Revolving_restaurant "Revolving restaurant") designed to be a landmark. The Henninger Turm was extensively refurbished and is now a residential high-rise.[[97]](#cite_note-99)

The first high-rise building boom came in the 1970s when [Westend Gate](/wiki/Westend_Gate "Westend Gate") (then called *Plaza Büro Center*) and [Silberturm](/wiki/Silberturm "Silberturm") were constructed and became the tallest buildings in Germany with a height of 159.3 meters and 166.3 meters, respectively. Around the same time, [Frankfurter Büro Center](/wiki/Frankfurter_B%C3%BCro_Center "Frankfurter Büro Center") and [City-Haus](/wiki/City-Haus "City-Haus") (142.4 meters and 142.1 meters) were constructed at Mainzer Landstraße and [Eurotower](/wiki/Eurotower_(Frankfurt) "Eurotower (Frankfurt)") (148.0 meters) and [Garden Tower](/wiki/Garden_Tower "Garden Tower") (127.0 meters; then called *Helaba-Hochhaus*) were constructed in the financial district.

None of the buildings constructed during the 1980s surpassed Silberturm. The most famous buildings from this decade are the [Deutsche Bank Twin Towers](/wiki/Deutsche_Bank_Twin_Towers "Deutsche Bank Twin Towers") at Taunusanlage, both 155.0 meters tall.

The 1990s featured a second wave. [Messeturm](/wiki/Messeturm "Messeturm"), built on the trade fair site, reached a height of 256.5 meters (842 ft) and became the tallest building in Europe by 1991. It was overtaken by the 259 m-high (850 ft) [Commerzbank Tower](/wiki/Commerzbank_Tower "Commerzbank Tower") in 1997. Other tall buildings from this decade are [Westendstrasse 1](/wiki/Westendstrasse_1 "Westendstrasse 1") (208 meters (682 ft)), [Main Tower](/wiki/Main_Tower "Main Tower") (200 meters (660 ft)) and [Trianon](/wiki/Trianon_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Trianon (Frankfurt am Main)") (186 meters (610 ft)).

In 21st-century Frankfurt, more high-rise buildings and skyscrapers (e.g., [Skyper](/wiki/Skyper "Skyper"), [Opernturm](/wiki/Opernturm "Opernturm"), [Tower 185](/wiki/Tower_185 "Tower 185"), [Seat of the European Central Bank](/wiki/Seat_of_the_European_Central_Bank "Seat of the European Central Bank"), [Taunusturm](/wiki/Taunusturm "Taunusturm")) emerged, but none have surpassed Commerzbank Tower.

### Other tall structures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=69 "Edit section: Other tall structures")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Europaturm_Abend.jpg/250px-Europaturm_Abend.jpg)](/wiki/File:Europaturm_Abend.jpg)

Top of the [Europaturm](/wiki/Europaturm "Europaturm"), a 337 m (1,106 ft) communications tower

* [Europaturm](/wiki/Europaturm "Europaturm") — The Europe Tower is a [telecommunications tower](/wiki/Radio_masts_and_towers "Radio masts and towers"), also known as the Frankfurt TV Tower, built from 1974 to 1979. With a height of 337.5 meters it is the tallest tower and the second tallest structure in Germany after the [Fernsehturm Berlin](/wiki/Fernsehturm_Berlin "Fernsehturm Berlin"). It was open to the public until 1999, with an entertainment establishment in the revolving top. It is normally referred to by locals as the "Ginnheimer Spargel" (*Ginnheim Asparagus*), but stands a few meters within [Bockenheim](/wiki/Bockenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bockenheim (Frankfurt am Main)") district.
* [Henninger Turm](/wiki/Henninger_Turm "Henninger Turm") — The Henninger Tower was a 120-mete-high [grain silo](/wiki/Grain_silo "Grain silo") built from 1959 to 1961 and owned by [Henninger Brewery](/wiki/Henninger_Brewery "Henninger Brewery"). It was the highest structure until 1974. The Henninger Tower had two rotating restaurants at the height of 101 and 106 meters and an open-air observation deck at the height of 110 meters. The tower closed to the public in October 2002 and was demolished in 2013 to be replaced by a 140 m (459 ft) tall residential tower, which is externally inspired by the old Henninger Turm. The cornerstone for this project was laid in June 2014 and construction was completed in summer 2017. The new tower offers 207 luxury flats[[98]](#cite_note-100) and houses the non-rotating restaurant "Franziska". From 1962 to 2008 a famous yearly [cycling race](/wiki/Road_bicycle_racing "Road bicycle racing") was named after the tower, the "Radrennen Rund um den Henninger Turm" (*Cycling race around Henninger Tower*). The now-renamed race is still a yearly event.
* [Goetheturm](/wiki/Goethe_Tower "Goethe Tower") – The Goethe Tower was a 43 m-high (141 ft) tower on the northern edge of the [Frankfurt City Forest](/wiki/Frankfurt_City_Forest "Frankfurt City Forest") in [Sachsenhausen](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Sachsenhausen (Frankfurt am Main)"). It was the fifth tallest wood construction structure in Germany. It was built in 1931 and was a popular place for day-trippers until it burned down in 2017. A faithful reconstruction has been opened to the public on 12 October 2020, exactly three years after the original's destruction.[[99]](#cite_note-101)

## Parks and green spaces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=70 "Edit section: Parks and green spaces")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/FFM_Jacobiweiher_Uferblick_von_NO_1.jpg/250px-FFM_Jacobiweiher_Uferblick_von_NO_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:FFM_Jacobiweiher_Uferblick_von_NO_1.jpg)

[Frankfurt City Forest](/wiki/Frankfurt_City_Forest "Frankfurt City Forest")

With a large forest, many parks, the Main riverbanks and the two botanical gardens, Frankfurt is considered a "green city": More than 50 percent of the area within the city limits are protected green areas.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

* Frankfurter Grüngürtel – The Green Belt is a ring-shaped public green space around the city. With 8,000 [ha](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare") it covers a third of the administrative area. It includes the Frankfurter Stadtwald (*[Frankfurt City Forest](/wiki/Frankfurt_City_Forest "Frankfurt City Forest")*, Germany's largest forest within a city), the Schwanheimer Düne (*[Schwanheim](/wiki/Schwanheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Schwanheim (Frankfurt am Main)") [Dune](/wiki/Dune "Dune")*), the Niddatal (*[Nidda](/wiki/Nidda_(river) "Nidda (river)") [Valley](/wiki/Valley "Valley")*), the Niddapark, the Lohrberg (*Lohr Mountain*, Frankfurt's only [vineyard](/wiki/Vineyard "Vineyard")), the Huthpark, the Enkheimer Ried (*[Enkheim](/wiki/Frankfurt-Bergen-Enkheim "Frankfurt-Bergen-Enkheim") [Marsh](/wiki/Marsh "Marsh")*), the Seckbacher Ried (*[Seckbach](/wiki/Seckbach_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Seckbach (Frankfurt am Main)") [Marsh](/wiki/Marsh "Marsh")*) and the Fechenheimer Mainbogen (a S-shaped part of the [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)") river in [Fechenheim](/wiki/Fechenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Fechenheim (Frankfurt am Main)")). The Green Belt is a [protected area](/wiki/Protected_area "Protected area") which means that housing is not allowed. The Green Belt was formally created in 1991 with its own constitution.
* Mainuferpark – The Mainuferpark (*Main Riverbanks Park*) is the common term to describe the inner-city Main riverbanks. It is an auto-free zone with large green areas that is popular with strollers and tourists, especially in the summertime, when it can become crowded. The southern riverbank, which continues further to [Offenbach am Main](/wiki/Offenbach_am_Main "Offenbach am Main") and [Hanau](/wiki/Hanau "Hanau"), offers the best skyline views. The northern riverbank ends in the west at the former Westhafen (*West Harbor*, a residential housing area) and is growing to the east: A former industrial-used area between the new [Seat of the European Central Bank](/wiki/Seat_of_the_European_Central_Bank "Seat of the European Central Bank") and the Osthafen (*East Harbor*) has become a park named Hafenpark (*Harbor Park*), which offers outdoor courts for basketball, soccer and a skatepark.
* Wallanlagen – The [Wallanlagen](/wiki/Wallanlagen "Wallanlagen") (former *[ramparts](/wiki/Defensive_wall "Defensive wall")*) relate to the former ring-shaped [city wall fortifications](/wiki/Defensive_wall "Defensive wall") around the [Altstadt](/wiki/Altstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Altstadt (Frankfurt am Main)") and the [Innenstadt](/wiki/Innenstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Innenstadt (Frankfurt am Main)") district (abolished 1804–1812), now a series of parks. Building is not allowed, with a few exceptions, the most famous being the [Alte Oper](/wiki/Alte_Oper "Alte Oper") (built 1880) at the Opernplatz. The part between the northern Main riverbank and the Opernplatz, referred to officially as Taunusanlage and Gallusanlage, is locally known as "Central Park" (a reference to the [famous park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park") in [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan")), because of the skyscrapers which stand on both sides.
* Nizza Park – At the juncture of the northern Main riverbank and the Wallanlagen is a famous small park called Nizza. The name of the park recalls [Nice](/wiki/Nice "Nice") in southern France, because it is one of the warmest areas with a nearly [mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate"). Numerous [Mediterranean flora](/wiki/Mediterranean_flora "Mediterranean flora") grow there and can survive outside during the winter.
* Garten des Himmlischen Friedens – "Garden of Heavenly Peace", named after the [Tiananmen Gate](/wiki/Tiananmen_Square "Tiananmen Square") in Beijing, is a Chinese-styled park in the [Nordend](/wiki/Nordend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Nordend (Frankfurt am Main)") district and part of the larger Bethmannpark. It contains Chinese buildings, with building materials imported from China and built by Chinese workers in the 1980s. Hosts traditional Chinese plants and herbs.
* Niddapark – after the economic failure of a Federal Garden Show the area was opened to the public as a public park in 1990 without elaborately designed plantings. With 168 hectares, the Niddapark is the largest public park in Frankfurt. The park is popular with runners, walkers, cyclists, dog owners, families, and is used for weekend leisure activities. Apart from sports fields, one fitness trail is open to the general public.[[101]](#cite_note-103)
* Other parks – the Ostpark (32 ha) and the [Grüneburgpark](/wiki/Gr%C3%BCneburgpark "Grüneburgpark") (29 ha).

### Botanical gardens

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=71 "Edit section: Botanical gardens")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Tropicarium_-_Palmengarten_Frankfurt_-_DSC01889.JPG/250px-Tropicarium_-_Palmengarten_Frankfurt_-_DSC01889.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tropicarium_-_Palmengarten_Frankfurt_-_DSC01889.JPG)

Greenhouse in the Palmengarten

Frankfurt is home to two major botanical gardens:

* [Palmengarten](/wiki/Palmengarten "Palmengarten") is located in the [Westend](/wiki/Westend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Westend (Frankfurt am Main)") district and is Hesse's largest botanical garden, covering 22 ha (54 acres). It opened to the public in 1871.[[102]](#cite_note-104) Designed by the architect Heinrich Siesmayer the botanical exhibits in free-air or in greenhouses include [agave](/wiki/Agave "Agave"), [succulents](/wiki/Succulents "Succulents") and [azaleas](/wiki/Azaleas "Azaleas").[[103]](#cite_note-105)
* [Botanischer Garten der Goethe-Universität](/wiki/Botanischer_Garten_der_Johann_Wolfgang_Goethe-Universit%C3%A4t_Frankfurt_am_Main "Botanischer Garten der Johann Wolfgang Goethe-Universität Frankfurt am Main") is [arboretum](/wiki/Arboretum "Arboretum"). It contains about 5,000 [species](/wiki/Species "Species"), with special collections of *[Rubus](/wiki/Rubus "Rubus")* (45 species) and indigenous plants of central Europe. It is organized into two major areas: The [geobotanical](/wiki/Geobotany "Geobotany") area contains an [alpine garden](/wiki/Alpine_garden "Alpine garden"), [arboretum](/wiki/Arboretum "Arboretum"), [meadows](/wiki/Meadow "Meadow"), [steppes](/wiki/Steppe "Steppe"), [marsh](/wiki/Marsh "Marsh"), and a pond, as well as collections of plants from the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands"), [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"), East Asia, [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean"), and North America and the systematic and ecological collection includes crop plants, [endangered species](/wiki/Endangered_species "Endangered species"), ornamental plants, [roses](/wiki/Rose "Rose"), and the *Neuer Senckenbergischer Arzneipflanzengarten* (New Senckenberg Medicinal Plant Garden), which measures 1,200 m2 (13,000 ft2). The Botanical Garden, Palmengarten, [Grüneburgpark](/wiki/Gr%C3%BCneburgpark "Grüneburgpark") collectively form the largest inner-city green area.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=72 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=73 "Edit section: Museums")]

Main article: [Museumsufer](/wiki/Museumsufer "Museumsufer")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/FrankfurtM_Staedel.jpg/250px-FrankfurtM_Staedel.jpg)](/wiki/File:FrankfurtM_Staedel.jpg)

The [Städel](/wiki/St%C3%A4del "Städel")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Frankfurt_Am_Main-Senckenberg_Naturmuseum_von_Osten-20120325.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Am_Main-Senckenberg_Naturmuseum_von_Osten-20120325.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Am_Main-Senckenberg_Naturmuseum_von_Osten-20120325.jpg)

[Senckenberg Natural History Museum](/wiki/Naturmuseum_Senckenberg "Naturmuseum Senckenberg")

With more than 30 museums, Frankfurt has one of the largest variety of museums in Europe. Most museums are part of the [Museumsufer](/wiki/Museumsufer "Museumsufer"), located on the front row of both sides of the [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)") riverbank or nearby, which was created on an initiative by cultural politician [Hilmar Hoffmann](/wiki/Hilmar_Hoffmann "Hilmar Hoffmann").[[104]](#cite_note-Museumsufer_Frankfurt-106)

Ten museums are located on the southern riverbank in [Sachsenhausen](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Sachsenhausen (Frankfurt am Main)") between the Eiserner Steg and the Friedensbrücke. The street itself, [Schaumainkai](/wiki/Schaumainkai "Schaumainkai"), is partially closed to traffic on Saturdays for Frankfurt's largest [flea market](/wiki/Flea_market "Flea market").

* [Deutsches Architekturmuseum](/wiki/German_Architecture_Museum "German Architecture Museum") (German Architecture Museum)
* [German Film Museum](/wiki/German_Film_Museum "German Film Museum") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deutsches_Filmmuseum "de:Deutsches Filmmuseum")] (Deutsches Filmmuseum)[[c]](#cite_note-107)
* [Deutsches Romantik-Museum](/wiki/Deutsches_Romantik-Museum "Deutsches Romantik-Museum")
* Frankfurter Ikonenmuseum (Icon Museum Frankfurt)
* [Liebieghaus](/wiki/Liebieghaus "Liebieghaus") (Museum of sculptures)
* [Museum Angewandte Kunst](/wiki/Museum_Angewandte_Kunst "Museum Angewandte Kunst") (Museum of Applied Arts)
* [Museum Giersch](/wiki/Museum_Giersch "Museum Giersch") (Museum for Regional Art)
* [Museum für Kommunikation](/wiki/Museum_f%C3%BCr_Kommunikation_Frankfurt "Museum für Kommunikation Frankfurt") (Museum of Communications)
* [Museum der Weltkulturen](/wiki/Museum_der_Weltkulturen "Museum der Weltkulturen") (Museum of World Cultures)
* [Städel](/wiki/St%C3%A4del "Städel"), one of the most famous art museums in Germany
* [Museum für elektronische Musik](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Electronic_Music "Museum of Modern Electronic Music") (Museum of Modern Electronic Music)
* Bibelhaus Erlebnis Museum (Bible House Experience Museum)

Two museums are located on the northern riverbank:

* [Jüdisches Museum Frankfurt](/wiki/Jewish_Museum_Frankfurt "Jewish Museum Frankfurt") (Jewish Museum Frankfurt)
* [Historisches Museum Frankfurt](/wiki/Historical_museum,_Frankfurt "Historical museum, Frankfurt") (Historical Museum Frankfurt)

Not directly located on the northern riverbank in the [Altstadt](/wiki/Altstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Altstadt (Frankfurt am Main)") district are:

* [Museum für Moderne Kunst](/wiki/Museum_f%C3%BCr_Moderne_Kunst "Museum für Moderne Kunst") (Museum of Modern Art)
* [Schirn Kunsthalle Frankfurt](/wiki/Schirn_Kunsthalle_Frankfurt "Schirn Kunsthalle Frankfurt") (Schirn Art Gallery Frankfurt)
* [Frankfurter Kunstverein](/wiki/Frankfurter_Kunstverein "Frankfurter Kunstverein") (Art Association Frankfurt)
* [Museum Judengasse](/wiki/Frankfurter_Judengasse "Frankfurter Judengasse") (Jews' Alley Museum)
* [Goethe-Haus](/wiki/Goethe_House "Goethe House") (Goethe House)
* Archäologisches Museum Frankfurt (Archaeological Museum Frankfurt)
* [Caricatura Museum für Komische Kunst](/wiki/Caricatura_Museum_Frankfurt "Caricatura Museum Frankfurt") (Caricatura Museum of Comic Art)
* Dommuseum Frankfurt (Frankfurt Cathedral Museum)

Another important museum is located in the [Westend](/wiki/Westend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Westend (Frankfurt am Main)") district:

* [Naturmuseum Senckenberg](/wiki/Naturmuseum_Senckenberg "Naturmuseum Senckenberg") (Senckenberg Natural History Museum), the second-largest [natural history](/wiki/Natural_history "Natural history") museum in Germany

Other museums are the Dialogmuseum (Dialogue Museum) in the [Ostend](/wiki/Ostend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Ostend (Frankfurt am Main)") district, [Eintracht Frankfurt](/wiki/Eintracht_Frankfurt "Eintracht Frankfurt") Museum at [Deutsche Bank Park](/wiki/Waldstadion_(Frankfurt) "Waldstadion (Frankfurt)"), the Frankfurter Feldbahnmuseum (Light Railway Museum Frankfurt) in the [Gallus](/wiki/Gallus_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Gallus (Frankfurt am Main)") district, the Verkehrsmuseum Frankfurt (Transport Museum Frankfurt) in the [Schwanheim](/wiki/Schwanheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Schwanheim (Frankfurt am Main)") district, the Hammer Museum in the [Bahnhofsviertel](/wiki/Bahnhofsviertel_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bahnhofsviertel (Frankfurt am Main)") district and the Geldmuseum der Deutschen Bundesbank (Money Museum of the German Federal Bank) in the [Ginnheim](/wiki/Ginnheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Ginnheim (Frankfurt am Main)") district.
The Explora Museum+Wissenschaft+Technik (Explora Museum of Science and Engineering) in the [Nordend](/wiki/Nordend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Nordend (Frankfurt am Main)") district was closed in 2016. Most museums open around 10:00 am local time, and it is possible to comfortably visit four museums in one day, a fact many tourists take advantage of.

### Performing arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=74 "Edit section: Performing arts")]

#### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=75 "Edit section: Music")]

[Eurodance](/wiki/Eurodance "Eurodance") and [Trance music](/wiki/Trance_music "Trance music") originated in Frankfurt. In 1989 German producers Michael Münzing and Luca Anzilotti (under the pseudonyms Benito Benites and John "Virgo" Garrett III) formed the [Snap!](/wiki/Snap! "Snap!") project. Snap! songs combined [Rap](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") and [Soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") vocals adding rhythm by using computer technology and mixing electronic sounds, bass and drums. By doing so a new genre was born: Eurodance.[[105]](#cite_note-108) In the early 1990s, DJs including [Sven Väth](/wiki/Sven_V%C3%A4th "Sven Väth") and DJ DAG (of [Dance 2 Trance](/wiki/Dance_2_Trance "Dance 2 Trance")) first played a harder, deeper style of [acid house](/wiki/Acid_house "Acid house") that became popular worldwide over the next decade as Trance music. Some of the early and most influential Eurodance, Trance and [Techno](/wiki/Techno_music "Techno music") acts, e.g., [La Bouche](/wiki/La_Bouche "La Bouche"), [Jam and Spoon](/wiki/Jam_and_Spoon "Jam and Spoon"), [Magic Affair](/wiki/Magic_Affair "Magic Affair"), [Culture Beat](/wiki/Culture_Beat "Culture Beat"), [Snap!](/wiki/Snap! "Snap!"), [Dance 2 Trance](/wiki/Dance_2_Trance "Dance 2 Trance"), [Oliver Lieb](/wiki/Oliver_Lieb "Oliver Lieb") and [Hardfloor](/wiki/Hardfloor "Hardfloor"), and record labels such as [Harthouse](/wiki/Harthouse "Harthouse") and [Eye Q](/wiki/Eye_Q_(record_label) "Eye Q (record label)"), were based in the city in the early 1990s.

#### Venues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=76 "Edit section: Venues")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Festhalle-Frankfurt-am-Main-2.jpg/250px-Festhalle-Frankfurt-am-Main-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Festhalle-Frankfurt-am-Main-2.jpg)

[Festhalle Frankfurt](/wiki/Festhalle_Frankfurt "Festhalle Frankfurt")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/The_English_Theatre_Ffm_DSC_0808.jpg/250px-The_English_Theatre_Ffm_DSC_0808.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_English_Theatre_Ffm_DSC_0808.jpg)

The English Theatre

* [Oper Frankfurt](/wiki/Oper_Frankfurt "Oper Frankfurt") – A leading Germany opera company and one of Europe's most important. It was elected *[Opera house of the year](/wiki/Opera_house_of_the_year "Opera house of the year")* (of Germany, Austria and German-speaking Switzerland) by German magazine *[Opernwelt](/wiki/Opernwelt "Opernwelt")* several times.[[106]](#cite_note-p023-109) Its orchestra was voted *Orchestra of the year* in 2009, 2010, 2011 and 2025.[[107]](#cite_note-h764-110) It is part of the Double System [Städtische Bühnen Frankfurt](/wiki/St%C3%A4dtische_B%C3%BChnen_Frankfurt "Städtische Bühnen Frankfurt").
* [Bockenheimer Depot](/wiki/Bockenheimer_Depot "Bockenheimer Depot"), venue of the Städtische Bühnen Frankfurt for operas, formerly also of the [Theater am Turm](/wiki/Theater_am_Turm "Theater am Turm"), where Handke's *[Offending the Audience](/wiki/Offending_the_Audience "Offending the Audience")* was performed in 1966[[108]](#cite_note-b488-111)
* [Schauspiel Frankfurt](/wiki/Schauspiel_Frankfurt "Schauspiel Frankfurt") – Theater at [Willy-Brandt-Platz](/wiki/Willy-Brandt-Platz "Willy-Brandt-Platz") in the financial district, next to the Frankfurt Opera in the Double System Städtische Bühnen Frankfurt.
* [Festhalle Frankfurt](/wiki/Festhalle_Frankfurt "Festhalle Frankfurt") – Multi-purpose hall next to the [Messeturm](/wiki/Messeturm "Messeturm") at the grounds of the [Frankfurt Trade Fair](/wiki/Frankfurt_Trade_Fair "Frankfurt Trade Fair"). It is mostly used for concerts, exhibitions or sport events and can accommodate up to 13,500.
* [Deutsche Bank Park](/wiki/Waldstadion_(Frankfurt) "Waldstadion (Frankfurt)") – Frankfurt's largest sports stadium and the seventh largest in Germany. It is located in the [Frankfurt City Forest](/wiki/Frankfurt_City_Forest "Frankfurt City Forest") near [Niederrad](/wiki/Niederrad "Niederrad"). It is primarily used for soccer and concerts with a capacity up to 58,000. It opened in 1925 and underwent several major reconstructions. Locals still prefer to call the stadium by its traditional name, Waldstadion (*Forest Stadium*). Home to [Eintracht Frankfurt](/wiki/Eintracht_Frankfurt "Eintracht Frankfurt").
* [Alte Oper](/wiki/Alte_Oper "Alte Oper") – A major concert hall. Venue of the [Frankfurt Radio Symphony](/wiki/Frankfurt_Radio_Symphony "Frankfurt Radio Symphony") (German: *hr-Sinfonieorchester*), the [radio orchestra](/wiki/Radio_orchestra "Radio orchestra") of the [Hessischer Rundfunk](/wiki/Hessischer_Rundfunk "Hessischer Rundfunk") and the [Frankfurter Opern- und Museumsorchester](/wiki/Frankfurter_Opern-_und_Museumsorchester "Frankfurter Opern- und Museumsorchester").
* [hr-Sendesaal](/wiki/Hr-Sendesaal "Hr-Sendesaal") – Venue of the Frankfurt Radio Symphony.
* [Jahrhunderthalle](/wiki/Centennial_Hall_(Frankfurt) "Centennial Hall (Frankfurt)") – *Century Hall* is a large concert and exhibition hall in [Unterliederbach](/wiki/Unterliederbach_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Unterliederbach (Frankfurt am Main)") district. Sometimes referred to as "Jahrhunderthalle Höchst", because it was built to celebrate the 100th anniversary of the chemical company [Hoechst AG](/wiki/Hoechst_AG "Hoechst AG") in 1963.
* The English Theatre – Located on the ground floor of the [Gallileo](/wiki/Gallileo_(skyscraper) "Gallileo (skyscraper)") high-rise building, this is the largest English theater in continental Europe. It was established in 1979.
* Tigerpalast – *Tiger Palace* is a [varieté](/wiki/Variet%C3%A9 "Varieté") near the Zeil. It was established in 1988 and houses the famous Tiger-Restaurant which was awarded a [Michelin star](/wiki/Michelin_Guide "Michelin Guide").
* Künstlerhaus Mousonturm – *House of Artists Mouson Tower* has a smaller budget than traditional theaters and uses more unconventional performing methods. It is located in an old factory in the [Ostend](/wiki/Ostend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Ostend (Frankfurt am Main)") district.
* Die Schmiere – *The Grease* is a [cabaret](/wiki/Cabaret "Cabaret") operational since 1950 focusing on satire.[[109]](#cite_note-112)
* Die Komödie – *The Comedy* is a [boulevard theater](/wiki/Boulevard_theatre_(aesthetic) "Boulevard theatre (aesthetic)") near downtown Frankfurt's Willy-Brandt-Platz.

### Foreign culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=77 "Edit section: Foreign culture")]

* [Instituto Cervantes](/wiki/Instituto_Cervantes "Instituto Cervantes") – Named after [Miguel de Cervantes](/wiki/Miguel_de_Cervantes "Miguel de Cervantes"), one of the most important Spanish authors, this is the world's largest organization for promoting the study and teaching of Spanish language and culture. 54 such Centros Cervantes across the world offer Spanish language and history courses. The Frankfurt branch was officially opened in September 2008 by [Felipe, Prince of Asturias](/wiki/Felipe_VI_of_Spain "Felipe VI of Spain") and his wife [Letizia, Princess of Asturias](/wiki/Letizia_of_Spain "Letizia of Spain"). It is located in the so-called *Amerika-Haus*.
* [Institut Français](/wiki/Institut_Fran%C3%A7ais "Institut Français") – A French public industrial and commercial organization (EPIC), started in 1907 by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs for promoting French, francophone as well as local cultures around the world. The French Institute works closely with the French cultural network abroad consisting of more than 150 branches and nearly 1,000 branches of the [Alliance française](/wiki/Alliance_fran%C3%A7aise "Alliance française") around the world.
* [Istituto Italiano di Cultura](/wiki/Istituto_Italiano_di_Cultura "Istituto Italiano di Cultura") – A worldwide non-profit organization created by the Italian government. It promotes Italian culture and is involved in the teaching of the Italian language; there are 83 Italian Cultural Institutes throughout major cities around the world.
* [Confucius Institute](/wiki/Confucius_Institute "Confucius Institute") – A non-profit public educational organization affiliated with the Ministry of Education of the People's Republic of China, whose aim is to promote Chinese language and culture, support local Chinese teaching internationally, and facilitate cultural exchanges. There are over 480 Confucius Institutes worldwide.
* [Central and Eastern European Online Library](/wiki/Central_and_Eastern_European_Online_Library "Central and Eastern European Online Library") – CEEOL is an online archive providing access to full-text articles from humanities and social science scholarly journals on Central, Eastern and South-Eastern European topics. Subject areas include anthropology, culture and society, economy, gender studies, history, Judaic studies, fine arts, literature, linguistics, political sciences and social sciences, philosophy and religion. CEEOL is operated by Questa.Soft GmbH.

### Festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=78 "Edit section: Festivals")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Museumsuferfest_2005_-_Riesenrad.jpg/250px-Museumsuferfest_2005_-_Riesenrad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museumsuferfest_2005_-_Riesenrad.jpg)

The Museumsuferfest in 2005

* Museumsuferfest – *Museums Riverbank Festival* is one of Germany's biggest cultural festivals, attracting more than 3 million visitors over three days at the end of August along the [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)") riverbank downtown. The 20 museums there open far into the night. It offers live music, dance shows, booths for crafts, jewelry, clothes and food stands from around the world.
* Dippemess – Frankfurt's oldest folk festival is the *Festival of Stoneware*, which takes place semi-annually around Easter and the end of September in the eastern area. "Dippe" is a regional [Hessian dialect](/wiki/Hessian_dialects "Hessian dialects") word meaning "pot" or "jar" which would not be understood in most other German regions. Mentioned for the first time in the 14th century as an annual marketplace it is now more of an amusement park. The name of the festival derives from its original purpose when it was a fair where traditionally crafted jars, pots and other stoneware were on offer.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Luminale_2012_-_OVO.jpg/250px-Luminale_2012_-_OVO.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luminale_2012_-_OVO.jpg)

"OVO" at Luminale 2012

* Luminale — The "festival of light" has taken place biannually since 2000, parallel to the *Light + building* exhibition at the trade fair. Many buildings are specially lit for the event. In 2008, more than 220 light installations could be seen, attracting 100,000 visitors.
* Wäldchestag – *Day of the forest* is known as a regional holiday because until the 1990s it was common that Frankfurt's shops were closed on this day. The festival takes place over four days after [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost") with the formal Wäldchestag on Tuesday. Its unique location is in the [Frankfurt City Forest](/wiki/Frankfurt_City_Forest "Frankfurt City Forest"), south-west of downtown in [Niederrad](/wiki/Niederrad "Niederrad"). "Wäldches" is a regional dialect of the German word "Wäldchen", meaning "small forest".
* Nacht der Museen – *Night of the museums* takes place every year in April or May. 50 museums in Frankfurt and in the neighboring city of [Offenbach am Main](/wiki/Offenbach_am_Main "Offenbach am Main") are open until 2:00 am surrounded by special music events, dance performances, readings and guided tours. A free shuttle operates between the museums. In 2010, approximately 40,000 visitors attended.
* Nacht der Clubs – *Night of the clubs* is an event similar to Nacht der Museen: On one night as many as 20 clubs can be visited with a single ticket for €12. Usually, club-door policies are loosened to attract new customers. A free shuttle runs between the clubs. 15,000 people participated in 2008.
* Wolkenkratzer Festival — The *Skyscraper Festival* is unique in Germany. It takes place irregularly, lately in May 2013, and attracted around 1.2 million visitors. For two days most skyscrapers are open to the public. Sky-divers, base jumpers, fireworks and laser shows are extra attractions.

### Nightlife

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=79 "Edit section: Nightlife")]

Frankfurt offers a variety of restaurants, bars, pubs and clubs. Clubs concentrate in and around downtownand in the [Ostend](/wiki/Ostend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Ostend (Frankfurt am Main)") district, mainly close to Hanauer Landstraße. Restaurants, bars and pubs concentrate in [Sachsenhausen](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Sachsenhausen (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Nordend](/wiki/Nordend_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Nordend (Frankfurt am Main)"), [Bornheim](/wiki/Bornheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bornheim (Frankfurt am Main)") and [Bockenheim](/wiki/Bockenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bockenheim (Frankfurt am Main)").

In [electronic music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music"), Frankfurt was a pioneering city in the late 1980s and early 1990s, with renowned DJs including [Sven Väth](/wiki/Sven_V%C3%A4th "Sven Väth"), [Marc Trauner](/wiki/Marc_Trauner "Marc Trauner"), [Scot Project](/wiki/Scot_Project "Scot Project") and [Kai Tracid](/wiki/Kai_Tracid "Kai Tracid"). One of the main venues of the early [Trance music](/wiki/Trance_music "Trance music") sound was the [Omen nightclub](/w/index.php?title=Omen_nightclub&action=edit&redlink=1 "Omen nightclub (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Omen_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "de:Omen (Frankfurt am Main)")] from 1988 to 1998. Another popular disco club of the 1980s–1990s and a hotspot for Techno/Trance music was the [Dorian Gray](/wiki/Dorian_Gray_(club) "Dorian Gray (club)"), which was located within Terminal 1 at Frankfurt Airport from 1978 to 2000. Further popular venues were the [U60311](/w/index.php?title=U60311&action=edit&redlink=1 "U60311 (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/U60311 "de:U60311")] (1998–2012) and the [Coocoon Club](/wiki/Cocoon_(club) "Cocoon (club)") in [Fechenheim](/wiki/Fechenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Fechenheim (Frankfurt am Main)") (2004–2012). Notable live music venues of the past include the [Sinkkasten Arts Club](/w/index.php?title=Sinkkasten_Arts_Club&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sinkkasten Arts Club (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sinkkasten_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "de:Sinkkasten (Frankfurt am Main)")] (1971–2011) and the [King Kamehameha Club](/w/index.php?title=King_Kamehameha_Club&action=edit&redlink=1 "King Kamehameha Club (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/King_Kamehameha_Club "de:King Kamehameha Club")] (1999–2013).

Among the most popular active rock and pop concert venues is the [Batschkapp](/wiki/Batschkapp "Batschkapp") in [Seckbach](/wiki/Seckbach_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Seckbach (Frankfurt am Main)"), which opened in 1976 as a center for autonomous and left-wing counterculture.

### Domestic culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=80 "Edit section: Domestic culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Frankfurter-kueche-vienna.JPG/250px-Frankfurter-kueche-vienna.JPG)](/wiki/File:Frankfurter-kueche-vienna.JPG)

A [Frankfurt kitchen](/wiki/Frankfurt_kitchen "Frankfurt kitchen") in the version of 1926 in an Austrian museum

* [Frankfurt kitchen](/wiki/Frankfurt_kitchen "Frankfurt kitchen") – Designed originally in 1926 by [Margarete Schütte-Lihotzky](/wiki/Margarete_Sch%C3%BCtte-Lihotzky "Margarete Schütte-Lihotzky") this kitchen is now recognized as one of the most influential designs in history and was [mass produced](/wiki/Mass_production "Mass production"). In 1920s [Weimar republic](/wiki/Weimar_republic "Weimar republic") Germany 10,000 modules of the Frankfurt kitchen were produced in Frankfurt.[[110]](#cite_note-113)
* [Frankfurt Cabinet](/wiki/Frankfurt_Cabinet "Frankfurt Cabinet") – The Baroque Frankfurt-style cupboards were used to store the family linen, one of them by Goethe's father, who took one cupboard to Rome. The most luxurious versions have wave-shaped parts, some are made of solid cherry wood inlaid with plumwood.

### Culinary specialties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=81 "Edit section: Culinary specialties")]

See also: [Hessian cuisine](/wiki/Hessian_cuisine "Hessian cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Apfelwein_Geripptes_Bembel.jpg/250px-Apfelwein_Geripptes_Bembel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Apfelwein_Geripptes_Bembel.jpg)

"Bembel" (jug) and "Geripptes" (glass)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Frankfurter_wuerstchen.jpg/250px-Frankfurter_wuerstchen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurter_wuerstchen.jpg)

Original *Frankfurter Würstchen* served with [potato salad](/wiki/Potato_salad "Potato salad")

* [Apfelwein](/wiki/Apfelwein "Apfelwein") – *Apple wine* or *hard cider* is regionally known as "Ebbelwoi", "Äppler" or "Stöffsche". It has an alcohol content of 5.5%–7% and a tart, sour taste. It is traditionally served in a glass, typically decorated with lozenges, called "Geripptes", a full glass is then called "Schoppen". Apfelwein is also available in a stoneware jar locally known as "Bembel". A group normally orders a "Bembel" and shares the contents. Apfelwein can be ordered as "sauergespritzer", which is apfelwein blended with 30% mineral water or as "süssgespritzer", which is Apfelwein blended with lemon soda, orange soda or fresh-pressed apple juice (lemon soda being the most common). Most of the pubs which serve Apfelwein are located in [Sachsenhausen](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Sachsenhausen (Frankfurt am Main)"), which is therefore known as "Ebbelwoi district". Due to its national drink Frankfurt is sometimes called "Big Ebbel" (pronunciation with [Hessian dialect](/wiki/Hessian_dialects "Hessian dialects")), an homage to [Big Apple](/wiki/Big_Apple "Big Apple"), the famous nickname of New York City.
* [Grüne Soße](/wiki/Green_sauce#German_Grüne_Soße "Green sauce") – *Green sauce* is a sauce made with hard-boiled eggs, oil, vinegar, salt and a generous amount of seven fresh herbs, namely borage, sorrel, garden cress, chervil, chives, parsley and salad burnet. Variants, often due to seasonal availability include dill, lovage, lemon balm and spinach. Original green sauce Frankfurt-style is made of herbs that were gathered only on fields within the city limits.
* [Frankfurter Würstchen](/wiki/Frankfurter_W%C3%BCrstchen "Frankfurter Würstchen") – "short Frankfurter" is a small [sausage](/wiki/Sausage "Sausage") made of smoked pork. They are similar to [hot dogs](/wiki/Hot_dog "Hot dog"). The name Frankfurter Würstchen has been trademarked since 1860.
* [Frankfurter Rindswurst](/wiki/Frankfurter_Rindswurst "Frankfurter Rindswurst") – Sausage made of pure beef.
* [Frankfurter Rippchen](/wiki/Frankfurter_Rippchen "Frankfurter Rippchen") – Also known as Rippchen mit Kraut, this is a traditional dish which consists of cured pork cutlets, slowly heated in [sauerkraut](/wiki/Sauerkraut "Sauerkraut") or meat broth, and usually served with sauerkraut, mashed potatoes and yellow mustard.
* [Handkäs mit Musik](/wiki/Handk%C3%A4se "Handkäse") – German regional [sour milk cheese](/wiki/Sour_milk_cheese "Sour milk cheese") (similar to [Harzer](/wiki/Harzer "Harzer")) and a culinary specialty in the [Rhine Main Region](/wiki/Frankfurt_Rhine-Main "Frankfurt Rhine-Main"). The traditional way of producing it is by hand. When it is topped with chopped onions it becomes "Handkäs mit Musik" (with music) because the onions are supposed to stimulate flatulence.
* [Frankfurter Kranz](/wiki/Frankfurter_Kranz "Frankfurter Kranz") – Cake speciality believed to originate from Frankfurt.
* [Bethmännchen](/wiki/Bethm%C3%A4nnchen "Bethmännchen") – "A little Bethmann" is a pastry made from marzipan with almond, powdered sugar, rosewater, flour, and egg. It is usually baked for Christmas.

## Shopping

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=82 "Edit section: Shopping")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/Frankfurt_Am_Main-Zeil-Ansicht_vom_Maintower_am_fruehen_Abend-20110328.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Am_Main-Zeil-Ansicht_vom_Maintower_am_fruehen_Abend-20110328.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Am_Main-Zeil-Ansicht_vom_Maintower_am_fruehen_Abend-20110328.jpg)

[Zeil](/wiki/Zeil "Zeil"), Frankfurt's central shopping street

* [Zeil](/wiki/Zeil "Zeil") – Frankfurt's central shopping street is a mile long and has been a shopping destination for more than a century. Only the western half of Zeil is pedestrianized. [MyZeil](/wiki/MyZeil "MyZeil") is a stereotypical shopping mall.[[111]](#cite_note-114) Three more shopping malls occupy the Zeil: UpperZeil (replacing the [Zeilgalerie](/wiki/Zeilgalerie "Zeilgalerie"), which was demolished in 2016), [Galeria Kaufhof](/wiki/Metro_AG "Metro AG") and [Karstadt](/wiki/Arcandor "Arcandor"), as well as large fashion retail clothing stores from [Peek & Cloppenburg](/wiki/Peek_%26_Cloppenburg "Peek & Cloppenburg") and [C&A](/wiki/C%26A "C&A"). During the month before Christmas, the extended pedestrian-only zone is host to [Frankfurt Christmas Market](/wiki/Frankfurt_Christmas_Market "Frankfurt Christmas Market"), one of the largest and oldest Christmas markets in Germany. Zeil is bordered by two large public squares, [Hauptwache](/wiki/Hauptwache_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Hauptwache (Frankfurt am Main)") in the west and [Konstablerwache](/wiki/Konstablerwache "Konstablerwache") in the east. It is the second most expensive street for shops to rent in Germany after the [Kaufingerstraße](/wiki/Kaufingerstra%C3%9Fe "Kaufingerstraße") in Munich. 85 percent of the shops are retail chains such as [H&M](/wiki/H%26M "H&M"), [Saturn](/wiki/Saturn_(store) "Saturn (store)"), [Esprit](/wiki/Esprit_Holdings "Esprit Holdings"), [Zara](/wiki/Zara_(retailer) "Zara (retailer)") or [NewYorker](/wiki/NewYorker "NewYorker").
* [Goethestraße](/wiki/Goethestra%C3%9Fe "Goethestraße") – Frankfurt's shopping destination for prestigious luxury shops like [Louis Vuitton](/wiki/Louis_Vuitton "Louis Vuitton"), [Prada](/wiki/Prada "Prada"), [Gucci](/wiki/Gucci "Gucci"), [Tiffany](/wiki/Tiffany_%26_Co. "Tiffany & Co."), [Giorgio Armani](/wiki/Giorgio_Armani "Giorgio Armani"), [Versace](/wiki/Versace "Versace"), [Cartier](/wiki/Cartier_SA "Cartier SA"), [Burberry](/wiki/Burberry "Burberry"), [Vertu](/wiki/Vertu "Vertu") and [Bulgari](/wiki/Bulgari "Bulgari").
* [Freßgass](/wiki/Fre%C3%9Fgass "Freßgass") – (officially *Kalbächer Gasse* and *Große Bockenheimer Straße*) is a central pedestrian-only street section between Börsenstraße and Opernplatz. The name translates as "feeding alley" because of its high concentration of gastronomy, but lately prestigious shops (e.g., [Apple Store](/wiki/Apple_Store "Apple Store"), [Hugo Boss](/wiki/Hugo_Boss "Hugo Boss"), [Porsche Design](/wiki/Porsche_Design_Group "Porsche Design Group")) have moved here due to the lack of space in the neighboring [Goethestraße](/wiki/Goethestra%C3%9Fe "Goethestraße"), displacing old, established restaurants, butchers and delicatessens.
* Berger Straße – Frankfurt's longest shopping street. It starts in the city center, runs through [Nordend](/wiki/Nordend,_Frankfurt "Nordend, Frankfurt") and [Bornheim](/wiki/Bornheim_(Frankfurt) "Bornheim (Frankfurt)") and ends in [Seckbach](/wiki/Seckbach_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Seckbach (Frankfurt am Main)"). The street is less crowded than the Zeil and offers a greater variety of smaller shops, restaurants and cafés.
* Leipziger Straße – Central shopping street in the Bockenheim district starting at Bockenheimer Warte going towards West. High density of shops for daily needs.
* Braubachstraße – In the [Altstadt](/wiki/Altstadt_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Altstadt (Frankfurt am Main)") district, close to the historic sites of the city, offers a large variety of art galleries, second-hand bookshops and antique shops.
* Münchener Straße – In the [Bahnhofsviertel](/wiki/Bahnhofsviertel_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bahnhofsviertel (Frankfurt am Main)") district, located between the central station and Willy-Brandt-Platz, is the most multicultural shopping street with many shops selling imported products mainly from Turkey, the Middle East and Asia.
* Kleinmarkthalle – (literally: *Small Market Hall*) is a market hall close to Konstablerwache square offering fresh food and flowers. In addition to regional delicacies like [green sauce](/wiki/Green_sauce "Green sauce") imported goods are offered. The Kleinmarkthalle is the largest public marketplace in Frankfurt.

* [![Sidewalk cafés at Fressgass](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Fressgass_Ffm_April_2011_EVA_8761.jpg/330px-Fressgass_Ffm_April_2011_EVA_8761.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fressgass_Ffm_April_2011_EVA_8761.jpg "Sidewalk cafés at Fressgass")
