## Quality of life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=83 "Edit section: Quality of life")]

In a 2001 ranking by the [University of Liverpool](/wiki/University_of_Liverpool "University of Liverpool"), Frankfurt was rated the richest city in Europe by [GDP per capita](/wiki/GDP_per_capita "GDP per capita"), followed by [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe "Karlsruhe"), [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") and [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich").[[112]](#cite_note-115)

Frankfurt was voted the seventh in the [Mercer Quality of Living Survey](/wiki/World%27s_most_livable_cities "World's most livable cities") by the Mercer Quality of Living Survey (2012),[[113]](#cite_note-116) seventh in the Mercer Quality of Living Survey (2010) and 18th at the [Economist's](/wiki/Economist_Intelligence_Unit "Economist Intelligence Unit") World's Most Liveable Cities Survey (2011).[[114]](#cite_note-117) According to an annual citizen survey (2010), arranged by the city council, 66 percent inhabitants are satisfied or highly satisfied with the city, while only 6 percent said that they are dissatisfied. Compared to the 1993's survey the number of satisfied inhabitants has grown about 22 percent while the number of dissatisfied inhabitants was reduced by 8 percent. 84 percent of the inhabitants like to live in Frankfurt, 13 percent would rather choose to live somewhere else. 37 percent are satisfied with the public safety (1993: only 9 percent), 22 percent are dissatisfied (1993: 64 percent).[[115]](#cite_note-118)

Frankfurt consistently has the highest levels of crime per 100,000 inhabitants in Germany (15.976 crimes per annum in 2008) and is therefore dubbed the German "crime capital".[[116]](#cite_note-119) However, this statistic is often criticized[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] because it ignores major factors: It is calculated based on the administrative 680,000-inhabitant figure while the urban area has 2.5 M inhabitants and on weekdays adds another million people[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] (not counting the 53 million passengers passing through the airport each year). The rate for personal safety-relevant crimes such as murder, manslaughter, rape or bodily harm, is 3.4 percent, placing Frankfurt twelfth in the ranking (related to the official 680,000-inhabitant figure) or number 21 (related to the one-million-figure).[[117]](#cite_note-120) In 2018, the state of Hesse, where Frankfurt is located, was ranked the third-safest state in Germany.[[118]](#cite_note-121)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=84 "Edit section: Transport")]

### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=85 "Edit section: Airports")]

#### Frankfurt Airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=86 "Edit section: Frankfurt Airport")]

Main article: [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/AirportFrankfurt_fromair_2010-09-19.jpg/250px-AirportFrankfurt_fromair_2010-09-19.jpg)](/wiki/File:AirportFrankfurt_fromair_2010-09-19.jpg)

[Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport") (with the fourth runway under construction in 2010) and the [Frankfurter Kreuz](/wiki/Frankfurter_Kreuz "Frankfurter Kreuz") (lower right corner)

The city can be accessed from around the world via [Frankfurt Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport "Frankfurt Airport") (*Flughafen Frankfurt am Main*) located 12 km (7 mi) southwest of downtown. The airport has four [runways](/wiki/Runway "Runway") and serves 265 nonstop destinations. Run by transport company [Fraport](/wiki/Fraport "Fraport") it ranks among the [world's busiest airports by passenger traffic](/wiki/World%27s_busiest_airports_by_passenger_traffic "World's busiest airports by passenger traffic") and is the [busiest airport by cargo traffic](/wiki/World%27s_busiest_airports_by_cargo_traffic "World's busiest airports by cargo traffic") in Europe. The airport also serves as a hub for [Condor](/wiki/Condor_Flugdienst "Condor Flugdienst") and as the main hub for German [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") [Lufthansa](/wiki/Lufthansa "Lufthansa"). It is the busiest airport in Europe in terms of cargo traffic, and the fourth busiest in Europe in terms of passenger traffic behind [London Heathrow Airport](/wiki/London_Heathrow_Airport "London Heathrow Airport"), [Paris Charles de Gaulle Airport](/wiki/Charles_de_Gaulle_Airport "Charles de Gaulle Airport") and [Amsterdam Airport Schiphol](/wiki/Amsterdam_Airport_Schiphol "Amsterdam Airport Schiphol"). Passenger traffic at Frankfurt Airport in 2018 was 69,510,269 passengers.

A third terminal is being constructed (planned to open in 2026). The third terminal will increase the capacity of the airport to over 90 million passengers per year.[[119]](#cite_note-122)

The airport can be reached by car or bus and has two railway stations, one for regional and one for long-distance traffic. The [S-Bahn](/wiki/S-Bahn "S-Bahn") lines S8 and S9 (direction *Offenbach Ost* or *Hanau Hbf*) departing at the [regional station](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport_regional_station "Frankfurt Airport regional station") take 10–15 minutes from the airport to [Frankfurt Central Station](/wiki/Frankfurt_Central_Station "Frankfurt Central Station") and onwards to [Hauptwache station](/wiki/Frankfurt_Hauptwache_station "Frankfurt Hauptwache station") downtown. The [IC](/wiki/InterCity "InterCity") and [ICE](/wiki/Intercity-Express "Intercity-Express") trains departing at the [long-distance station](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport_long-distance_station "Frankfurt Airport long-distance station") take 10 minutes to Frankfurt Central Station.

#### Frankfurt Hahn Airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=87 "Edit section: Frankfurt Hahn Airport")]

Despite the name, [Frankfurt Hahn Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt%E2%80%93Hahn_Airport "Frankfurt–Hahn Airport") (*Flughafen Frankfurt-Hahn*) is situated approximately 120 km (75 mi) from the city in [Lautzenhausen](/wiki/Lautzenhausen "Lautzenhausen") ([Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate")). Hahn Airport is a major base for [low-cost carrier](/wiki/Low-cost_carrier "Low-cost carrier") [Ryanair](/wiki/Ryanair "Ryanair"). This airport can only be reached by car or bus. An hourly bus service runs from [Frankfurt Central Station](/wiki/Frankfurt_Hauptbahnhof "Frankfurt Hauptbahnhof"), taking just over 2 hours.[[120]](#cite_note-123) Passenger traffic at Hahn Airport in 2010 was 3.5 million.

#### Frankfurt Egelsbach Airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=88 "Edit section: Frankfurt Egelsbach Airport")]

[Frankfurt Egelsbach Airport](/wiki/Frankfurt_Egelsbach_Airport "Frankfurt Egelsbach Airport") (*Flugplatz Frankfurt-Egelsbach*) is a busy [general aviation](/wiki/General_aviation "General aviation") airport located south-east of Frankfurt Airport, near [Egelsbach](/wiki/Egelsbach "Egelsbach").

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=89 "Edit section: Roads")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Frankfurter_Kreuz_-_BAB_3_und_5.jpg/250px-Frankfurter_Kreuz_-_BAB_3_und_5.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurter_Kreuz_-_BAB_3_und_5.jpg)

[Frankfurter Kreuz](/wiki/Frankfurter_Kreuz "Frankfurter Kreuz")

Frankfurt is a traffic hub for the German motorway (*[Autobahn](/wiki/Autobahn "Autobahn")*) system. The [Frankfurter Kreuz](/wiki/Frankfurter_Kreuz "Frankfurter Kreuz") is an Autobahn interchange close to the airport, where the [Bundesautobahn 3](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_3 "Bundesautobahn 3") (A3), [Cologne](/wiki/Cologne "Cologne") to [Würzburg](/wiki/W%C3%BCrzburg "Würzburg"), and the [Bundesautobahn 5](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_5 "Bundesautobahn 5") (A5), [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel") to [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover"), meet. With approximately 320,000 cars passing through it every day, it is Europe's most heavily used interchange. The [Bundesautobahn 66](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_66 "Bundesautobahn 66") (A66) connects Frankfurt with [Wiesbaden](/wiki/Wiesbaden "Wiesbaden") in the west and [Fulda](/wiki/Fulda "Fulda") in the east. The [Bundesautobahn 661](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_661 "Bundesautobahn 661") (A661) is mainly a commuter motorway that starts in the south (Egelsbach), runs through the eastern part and ends in the north ([Oberursel](/wiki/Oberursel "Oberursel")). The [Bundesautobahn 648](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_648 "Bundesautobahn 648") (A648) is a very short motorway in the western part which primarily serves as a fast connection between the A 66 and the [Frankfurt Trade Fair](/wiki/Frankfurt_Trade_Fair "Frankfurt Trade Fair"). The A5 in the west, the A3 in the south and the A661 in the northeast form a [ring road](/wiki/Ring_road "Ring road") around the inner city districts and define a [Low-emission zone](/wiki/Low-emission_zone "Low-emission zone") (*Umweltzone*; established in 2008), meaning that vehicles have to meet certain emission criteria to enter the zone.

The streets of central Frankfurt are usually congested with cars during [rush hour](/wiki/Rush_hour "Rush hour"). Some areas, especially around the shopping streets Zeil, Goethestraße and Freßgass, are pedestrian-only streets.

### Railway stations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=90 "Edit section: Railway stations")]

#### Frankfurt Central Station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=91 "Edit section: Frankfurt Central Station")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Hauptbahnhof_Frankfurt.jpg/250px-Hauptbahnhof_Frankfurt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hauptbahnhof_Frankfurt.jpg)

[Frankfurt Central Station](/wiki/Frankfurt_(Main)_Hauptbahnhof "Frankfurt (Main) Hauptbahnhof")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Frankfurt_Hauptbahnhof_tief_S-Bahn_S6.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Hauptbahnhof_tief_S-Bahn_S6.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Hauptbahnhof_tief_S-Bahn_S6.jpg)

[S-Bahn](/wiki/S-Bahn "S-Bahn") at [Central Station (underground)](/wiki/Frankfurt_Central_Station_(underground_station) "Frankfurt Central Station (underground station)")

Frankfurt Hauptbahnhof *(*often abbreviated as *Frankfurt (Main) Hbf* or *F-Hbf*) is the largest railway station in Germany by railway traffic. By daily passenger volume, it ranks second (493,000 each) after [Hamburg Central Station](/wiki/Hamburg_Hauptbahnhof "Hamburg Hauptbahnhof") (550,000). It is located between the [Gallus](/wiki/Gallus_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Gallus (Frankfurt am Main)"), the [Gutleutviertel](/wiki/Gutleutviertel "Gutleutviertel") and the [Bahnhofsviertel](/wiki/Bahnhofsviertel_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Bahnhofsviertel (Frankfurt am Main)") district, not far away from the trade fair and the financial district. It serves as a major hub for long-distance trains ([InterCity](/wiki/InterCity "InterCity"), [ICE](/wiki/InterCityExpress "InterCityExpress")) and regional trains as well as for Frankfurt's public transport system. It is a stop for most of ICE high-speed lines, making it Germany's most important ICE station. ICE Trains to London via the [Channel Tunnel](/wiki/Channel_Tunnel "Channel Tunnel") were planned for 2013.[[121]](#cite_note-124) All [Rhine-Main S-Bahn](/wiki/Rhine-Main_S-Bahn "Rhine-Main S-Bahn") lines, two [U-Bahn](/wiki/Frankfurt_U-Bahn "Frankfurt U-Bahn") lines (U4, U5), several tram and bus lines stop there. Regional and local trains are integrated in the Public transport system [Rhein-Main-Verkehrsverbund](/wiki/Rhein-Main-Verkehrsverbund "Rhein-Main-Verkehrsverbund") (RMV), the second-largest integrated public transport system in the world, after [Verkehrsverbund Berlin-Brandenburg](/wiki/Verkehrsverbund_Berlin-Brandenburg "Verkehrsverbund Berlin-Brandenburg").

#### Frankfurt Airport stations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=92 "Edit section: Frankfurt Airport stations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/DSCI0058_Flughafen_Frankfurt_Squaire.jpg/250px-DSCI0058_Flughafen_Frankfurt_Squaire.jpg)](/wiki/File:DSCI0058_Flughafen_Frankfurt_Squaire.jpg)

[ICE 3](/wiki/ICE_3 "ICE 3") departing westward from [Frankfurt Airport long-distance station](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport_long-distance_station "Frankfurt Airport long-distance station") underneath [The Squaire](/wiki/The_Squaire "The Squaire")

Frankfurt Airport can be accessed by two railway stations: [Frankfurt Airport long-distance station](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport_long-distance_station "Frankfurt Airport long-distance station") (*Frankfurt Flughafen Fernbahnhof*) is only for long-distance traffic and connects the airport to the main rail network, with most of the [ICE](/wiki/InterCityExpress "InterCityExpress") services using the [Cologne-Frankfurt high-speed rail line](/wiki/Cologne-Frankfurt_high-speed_rail_line "Cologne-Frankfurt high-speed rail line"). The long-distance station is located outside the actual airport ground but has a connecting bridge for pedestrians to Terminal 1, concourse B. [Frankfurt Airport regional station](/wiki/Frankfurt_Airport_regional_station "Frankfurt Airport regional station") (*Frankfurt Flughafen Regionalbahnhof*) is for local [S-Bahn](/wiki/Rhine-Main_S-Bahn "Rhine-Main S-Bahn") trains (lines S8, S9) and regional trains. The regional station is located within Terminal 1, concourse B.

#### Frankfurt South station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=93 "Edit section: Frankfurt South station")]

Frankfurt's third long-distance station is [Frankfurt South station](/wiki/Frankfurt_South_station "Frankfurt South station") (*Frankfurt Südbahnhof*, often abbreviated as *Frankfurt (Main) Süd* or *F-Süd*), located in [Sachsenhausen](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Sachsenhausen (Frankfurt am Main)"). It is an important destination for local trains and trams (lines 15, 16 and 18) and the terminal stop for four U-Bahn lines (U1, U2, U3, U8) as well as three S-Bahn lines (S3, S4, S5). Line S6 also serves the station.

#### Messe stations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=94 "Edit section: Messe stations")]

The [Frankfurt Trade Fair](/wiki/Frankfurt_Trade_Fair "Frankfurt Trade Fair") offers two railway stations: [Messe station](/wiki/Frankfurt_Messe_station "Frankfurt Messe station") is for local [S-Bahn](/wiki/Rhine-Main_S-Bahn "Rhine-Main S-Bahn") trains (lines S3-S6) and is centrally located amid trade fair premises, while Festhalle/Messe station is served by U-Bahn line U4 and is located at the north-east corner of the premises.

#### Konstablerwache station and Hauptwache station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=95 "Edit section: Konstablerwache station and Hauptwache station")]

Two other major downtown railway stations are Konstablerwache and Hauptwache, located on each end of the Zeil. They are the main stations to change from east-to-west-bound S-Bahn trains to north-to-south-bound U-Bahn trains. Konstablerwache station is the second-busiest railway station regarding daily passenger volume (98,000) after the central station. The third-busiest railway station is Hauptwache station (93,000).[[122]](#cite_note-125)[[123]](#cite_note-126)

#### Frankfurt West Station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=96 "Edit section: Frankfurt West Station")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Westbahnhof_Hochbahnsteig.jpg/250px-Westbahnhof_Hochbahnsteig.jpg)](/wiki/File:Westbahnhof_Hochbahnsteig.jpg)

[DBAG Class 423](/wiki/DBAG_Class_423 "DBAG Class 423") approaching the [elevated section](/wiki/Elevated_railway "Elevated railway") of [Frankfurt West station](/wiki/Frankfurt_West_station "Frankfurt West station")

This Station, located in Bockenheim, is served by north-heading Long-Distance ICE trains, multiple regional trains, and four commuter S-Bahn lines (S3, S4, S5, S6). Additionally, it is an important terminal stop for three "Metrobus" lines (M32, M36, M73).

### Coach stations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=97 "Edit section: Coach stations")]

There are three stations for [intercity bus services](/wiki/Intercity_bus_service "Intercity bus service") in Frankfurt: one at the south side of the Central Station, one at the Terminal 2 of the airport and another one at Stephanstraße.[[124]](#cite_note-127)

### Public transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=98 "Edit section: Public transport")]

Main article: [Public transport in Frankfurt am Main](/wiki/Public_transport_in_Frankfurt_am_Main "Public transport in Frankfurt am Main")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Frankfurt_am_Main_-_Netzplan_Schienennahverkehr.png/250px-Frankfurt_am_Main_-_Netzplan_Schienennahverkehr.png)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_am_Main_-_Netzplan_Schienennahverkehr.png)

Public transport network

The city has two [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") systems: the U-Bahn and the S-Bahn, as well as an above-ground tram system. Information about the U- and S-Bahn can be found on the website of the [RMV](/wiki/Rhein-Main-Verkehrsverbund "Rhein-Main-Verkehrsverbund").[[125]](#cite_note-128)

#### S-Bahn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=99 "Edit section: S-Bahn")]

Main article: [Rhine-Main S-Bahn](/wiki/Rhine-Main_S-Bahn "Rhine-Main S-Bahn")

Nine [S-Bahn](/wiki/S-Bahn "S-Bahn") lines (S1 to S9) connect Frankfurt with the densely populated [Rhine Main Region](/wiki/Frankfurt_Rhein-Main_Region "Frankfurt Rhein-Main Region"). Most routes have at least 15-minute service during the day, either by one line running every 15 minutes, or by two lines servicing one route at a 30-minute interval. All lines, except line S7, run through the [Frankfurt City Tunnel](/wiki/Frankfurt_City_Tunnel "Frankfurt City Tunnel") and serve stations at [Ostendstraße](/wiki/Frankfurt_Ostendstra%C3%9Fe_station "Frankfurt Ostendstraße station"), [Konstablerwache](/wiki/Frankfurt_(Main)_Konstablerwache_station "Frankfurt (Main) Konstablerwache station"), [Hauptwache](/wiki/Frankfurt_(Main)_Hauptwache_station "Frankfurt (Main) Hauptwache station"), [Taunusanlage](/wiki/Taunusanlage_station "Taunusanlage station") and [Frankfurt Central Station](/wiki/Frankfurt_Central_Station "Frankfurt Central Station"). When leaving the city the S-Bahn travels above ground. It provides access to the trade fair (S3, S4, S5, S6), the airport (S8, S9), the stadium (S7, S8, S9) and nearby cities such as [Wiesbaden](/wiki/Wiesbaden "Wiesbaden"), [Mainz](/wiki/Mainz "Mainz"), [Darmstadt](/wiki/Darmstadt "Darmstadt"), [Rüsselsheim](/wiki/R%C3%BCsselsheim "Rüsselsheim"), [Hanau](/wiki/Hanau "Hanau"), [Offenbach am Main](/wiki/Offenbach_am_Main "Offenbach am Main"), [Oberursel](/wiki/Oberursel "Oberursel"), [Bad Homburg](/wiki/Bad_Homburg "Bad Homburg"), [Kronberg](/wiki/Kronberg "Kronberg"), [Friedberg](/wiki/Friedberg,_Hesse "Friedberg, Hesse") and smaller towns that are on the way. The S8/S9 runs 24/7.

#### U-Bahn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=100 "Edit section: U-Bahn")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/DSC03475_B44_Ludwig-Landmann-Stra%C3%9Fe.jpg/250px-DSC03475_B44_Ludwig-Landmann-Stra%C3%9Fe.jpg)](/wiki/File:DSC03475_B44_Ludwig-Landmann-Stra%C3%9Fe.jpg)

Underground line *U7* running as a *[Stadtbahn](/wiki/Stadtbahn "Stadtbahn")* amidst [Ludwig-Landmann](/wiki/Ludwig_Landmann "Ludwig Landmann")-Straße in [Frankfurt-Rödelheim](/wiki/R%C3%B6delheim "Rödelheim")

Main article: [Frankfurt U-Bahn](/wiki/Frankfurt_U-Bahn "Frankfurt U-Bahn")

The [U-Bahn](/wiki/U-Bahn "U-Bahn") has nine lines (U1 to U9) serving Frankfurt and the larger suburbs of Bad Homburg and Oberursel in the north. The trains that run on the U-Bahn are in fact [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") (*[Stadtbahn](/wiki/Stadtbahn "Stadtbahn")*) as many lines travel along a track in the middle of the street instead of underground. The minimum service interval is 2.5 minutes, although the usual pattern is that each line runs at 7.5- to 10-minute intervals, which produce between 3- and 5-minute intervals on downtown tracks shared by more than one line.

#### Tram

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=101 "Edit section: Tram")]

Main article: [Trams in Frankfurt am Main](/wiki/Trams_in_Frankfurt_am_Main "Trams in Frankfurt am Main")

Frankfurt has ten tram lines (11, 12, 14 to 21), with trams arriving usually every 10 minutes. Many sections are served by two lines, combining to run at 5-minute intervals during rush-hour. Trams only run above ground and serve more stops than the U-Bahn or the S-Bahn.

#### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=102 "Edit section: Bus")]

Main article: [Public transport in Frankfurt am Main § Bus](/wiki/Public_transport_in_Frankfurt_am_Main#Bus "Public transport in Frankfurt am Main")

A number of bus lines complete the Frankfurt public transport system. Night buses replace U-Bahn and tram services between 1:30 am and 3:30 am.[[126]](#cite_note-129) The central junction for the night bus service is at the downtown square of Konstablerwache, where all night bus lines start and end.

### Taxis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=103 "Edit section: Taxis")]

[Taxicabs](/wiki/Taxicab "Taxicab") can usually be found outside the major S-Bahn and U-Bahn stations, at the central station, the south station, the airport, the trade fair and in the crowded inner-city shopping streets. The common way to obtain a taxi is to either call a taxi operator or to go to a taxi rank. However, although not the norm, one can hail a passing taxi on the street.

[Uber](/wiki/Uber "Uber") ceased operations in Frankfurt on 9 November 2015 after operating in the city for 18 months.[[127]](#cite_note-130) However, [UberX](/wiki/UberX "UberX") and local cabs are available through the Uber app.[[128]](#cite_note-131)

### Bicycles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=104 "Edit section: Bicycles")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Frankfurt_Velotaxi.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Velotaxi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Velotaxi.jpg)

Velotaxi at the Zeil

[Deutsche Bahn](/wiki/Deutsche_Bahn "Deutsche Bahn") makes bicycles available for hire through their [Call a Bike](/wiki/Call_a_Bike "Call a Bike") service. The bicycles are stationed all over the city, including at selected railway stations. They can easily be spotted because of their eye-catching silver-red color. To rent a specific bike, riders either call a service number to get an unlock code or reserve the bike via the smartphone application. To return the bike, the rider locks it within a designated return area (and calls the service number, if not booked via the app).[[129]](#cite_note-132)

[Nextbike](/wiki/Nextbike "Nextbike") also makes bicycles available for hire in Frankfurt. They are stationed all over the city. These can be spotted with their blue color scheme.

[Cycle rickshaws](/wiki/Cycle_rickshaw "Cycle rickshaw") (velotaxis), a type of [tricycle](/wiki/Tricycle "Tricycle") designed to carry passengers in addition to the driver, are also available. These are allowed to operate in pedestrian-only areas and are therefore practical for sightseeing.

Frankfurt has a network of cycle routes. Many long-distance bike routes into the city have cycle tracks that are separate from motor vehicle traffic. A number of downtown roads are "bicycle streets" where the cyclist has the right of way and where motorized vehicles are only allowed access if they do not disrupt the cycle users. In addition, cyclists are allowed to ride many cramped one-way streets in both directions. As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit), 15 percent of citizens used bicycles.[[130]](#cite_note-133)

### E-Scooters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=105 "Edit section: E-Scooters")]

Since 15 June 2019, the use of [e-scooters](/wiki/E-scooters "E-scooters") was officially permitted by the German federal government. In Frankfurt, companies like [Lime](/wiki/Lime_(transportation_company) "Lime (transportation company)"), TIER, [Bird](/wiki/Bird_(transportation_company) "Bird (transportation company)"), voi., [Dott](/wiki/Dott_(transportation_company) "Dott (transportation company)") or [Bolt](/wiki/Bolt_(company) "Bolt (company)") are offering their electric micro mobility vehicles for lease. However, their use is being regarded with increasing weariness due to frequent abuse (parking, speeding, vandalism, accidents) and has sparked a public debate about the need of further regulation of the e-scooter market.[[131]](#cite_note-134)

## Courts of justice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=106 "Edit section: Courts of justice")]

Several courts are located in Frankfurt, including:

* Hessisches Landesarbeitsgericht (Hessian State Employment Court)[[132]](#cite_note-135)
* Oberlandesgericht Frankfurt (Higher Regional Court Frankfurt)[[133]](#cite_note-136)
* Landgericht Frankfurt (Regional Court Frankfurt)[[134]](#cite_note-137)
* Amtsgericht Frankfurt (Local Court Frankfurt)[[135]](#cite_note-138)
* Sozialgericht Frankfurt (Social Court Frankfurt)[[136]](#cite_note-139)
* Arbeitsgericht Frankfurt (Employment Court Frankfurt)[[137]](#cite_note-140)
* Verwaltungsgericht Frankfurt (Administration Court Frankfurt)[[138]](#cite_note-141)

## Financial authorities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=107 "Edit section: Financial authorities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Frankfurt_Westhafen_Tower_2011a.jpg/250px-Frankfurt_Westhafen_Tower_2011a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frankfurt_Westhafen_Tower_2011a.jpg)

[Westhafen Tower](/wiki/Westhafen_Tower "Westhafen Tower"), home to the European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority (EIOPA)

### European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=108 "Edit section: European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority")]

The [European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority](/wiki/European_Insurance_and_Occupational_Pensions_Authority "European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority") (EIOPA) is an institution of the EU and part of the [European System of Financial Supervisors](/wiki/European_System_of_Financial_Supervisors "European System of Financial Supervisors") that was created in response to the [2008 financial crisis](/wiki/2008_financial_crisis "2008 financial crisis"). It was established on 1 January 2011.

### Federal Financial Supervisory Authority

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=109 "Edit section: Federal Financial Supervisory Authority")]

Frankfurt is one of two locations of the German [Federal Financial Supervisory Authority](/wiki/Federal_Financial_Supervisory_Authority "Federal Financial Supervisory Authority") (*Bundesanstalt für Finanzdienstleistungsaufsicht*, short: *BaFin*). The BaFin is an independent federal institution and acts as Germany's [financial regulatory](/wiki/Financial_regulation "Financial regulation") authority.

### Anti-Money Laundering Authority

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=110 "Edit section: Anti-Money Laundering Authority")]

The Anti-Money Laundering Authority (AMLA) of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") (EU) is based in Frankfurt.[[139]](#cite_note-142)

## International relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=111 "Edit section: International relations")]

### International Finance Corporation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=112 "Edit section: International Finance Corporation")]

Frankfurt is home to the German office of the [International Finance Corporation](/wiki/International_Finance_Corporation "International Finance Corporation") (IFC), which is part of the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") Group. The IFC promotes sustainable private sector investment in [developing countries](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country").

### Consulates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=113 "Edit section: Consulates")]

As a profoundly international city, Frankfurt hosts 93 diplomatic missions (consulates and [consulates-general](/wiki/Consulate-_general "Consulate- general")).[[140]](#cite_note-143) The [Consulate General of the United States](/wiki/Consulate_General_of_the_United_States_in_Frankfurt "Consulate General of the United States in Frankfurt") in [Eckenheim](/wiki/Eckenheim_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Eckenheim (Frankfurt am Main)") is the largest American consulate in the world.[[141]](#cite_note-144)

## Education and research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=114 "Edit section: Education and research")]

### Universities and schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=115 "Edit section: Universities and schools")]

Frankfurt hosts two universities and several specialist schools. The two business schools are [Goethe University Frankfurt](/wiki/Goethe_University_Frankfurt "Goethe University Frankfurt")'s [Goethe Business School](/wiki/Goethe_Business_School "Goethe Business School") and [Frankfurt School of Finance & Management](/wiki/Frankfurt_School_of_Finance_%26_Management "Frankfurt School of Finance & Management").

#### Johann Wolfgang Goethe University

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=116 "Edit section: Johann Wolfgang Goethe University")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Campus_Westend_Frankfurt_01.jpg/250px-Campus_Westend_Frankfurt_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Campus_Westend_Frankfurt_01.jpg)

[Johann Wolfgang Goethe University](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_Goethe_University "Johann Wolfgang Goethe University")

The oldest and best-known university is the [Johann Wolfgang Goethe University](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_Goethe_University "Johann Wolfgang Goethe University"), with locations in Bockenheim, Westend, and Riedberg, and the university hospital in Niederrad. [Goethe Business School](/wiki/Goethe_Business_School "Goethe Business School") is part of the university's [House of Finance](/wiki/House_of_Finance "House of Finance") at Campus Westend. The Business School's Full-Time MBA program has over 70% international students.

#### Frankfurt University of Applied Sciences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=117 "Edit section: Frankfurt University of Applied Sciences")]

The [Frankfurt University of Applied Sciences](/wiki/Frankfurt_University_of_Applied_Sciences "Frankfurt University of Applied Sciences") was created out of several older organisations in 1971, and offers over 38 study areas, in the arts, sciences, engineering and law. Some of the most important research projects: Planet Earth Simulator, [FraLine](/wiki/FraLine "FraLine")-IT-School-Service, quantitative analysis of [methane](/wiki/Methane "Methane") in human corpses with the help of a mass spectrometer, software engineering (e.g., fraDesk), analysis of qualitative and quantitative gas in human lungs, long-term studies on [photovoltaic](/wiki/Photovoltaic "Photovoltaic") modules (to name only a few).

#### Frankfurt School of Finance and Management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=118 "Edit section: Frankfurt School of Finance and Management")]

The city is also home to a business school, [Frankfurt School of Finance & Management](/wiki/Frankfurt_School_of_Finance_%26_Management "Frankfurt School of Finance & Management"), formerly known as the **Hochschule für Bankwirtschaft** (Institution of Higher Learning for Banking Economics), with its new campus near Deutsche Nationalbibliothek U-Bahn stop (recently moving from its previous location in the Ostend (Eastend) neighborhood). In 2001, it became a specialist institution for Economics and Management, or FOM. Frankfurt School is consistently ranked among the best business schools in the world, attributed to its high research output and quality of undergraduate and graduate training.[[142]](#cite_note-145)

#### Städelschule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=119 "Edit section: Städelschule")]

Frankfurt has the State Institution of Higher Learning for Artistic Education known as the [Städelschule](/wiki/St%C3%A4delschule "Städelschule"), founded in 1817 by [Johann Friedrich Städel](/wiki/Johann_Friedrich_St%C3%A4del "Johann Friedrich Städel"). It was taken over by the city in 1942 and turned into a state art school.

#### Music schools and conservatory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=120 "Edit section: Music schools and conservatory")]

Music institutions are the [Frankfurt University of Music and Performing Arts](/wiki/Frankfurt_University_of_Music_and_Performing_Arts "Frankfurt University of Music and Performing Arts"), and the [Hoch Conservatory](/wiki/Hoch_Conservatory "Hoch Conservatory") (Dr. Hoch's Konservatorium) which was founded in 1878. The International [Ensemble Modern](/wiki/Ensemble_Modern "Ensemble Modern") Academy is a significant institution for the study of contemporary music.[[143]](#cite_note-146)

#### Other notable schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=121 "Edit section: Other notable schools")]

The [Sankt Georgen Graduate School of Philosophy and Theology](/wiki/Sankt_Georgen_Graduate_School_of_Philosophy_and_Theology "Sankt Georgen Graduate School of Philosophy and Theology") (German:*Philosophisch-Theologische Hochschule Sankt Georgen*), a private institution with membership in the German Jesuit Association, has been located in [Sachsenhausen](/wiki/Sachsenhausen_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Sachsenhausen (Frankfurt am Main)") since 1950.

#### Education and media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=122 "Edit section: Education and media")]

Frankfurt schools rank among the best-equipped schools nationwide for the availability of PCs and other media facilities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In order to assure maintenance and support of the school PCs, the city in cooperation with the [University of Applied Sciences](/wiki/University_of_Applied_Sciences_Giessen-Friedberg "University of Applied Sciences Giessen-Friedberg") launched the project [Fraline](/wiki/Fraline "Fraline") – IT-Schul-Service, an initiative employing students to provide basic school IT-support.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Research institutes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=123 "Edit section: Research institutes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Brain_Research_at_Night_of_Science_2018_%281%29.jpg/250px-Max_Planck_Institute_for_Brain_Research_at_Night_of_Science_2018_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Max_Planck_Institute_for_Brain_Research_at_Night_of_Science_2018_(1).jpg)

[Max Planck Institute for Brain Research](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Brain_Research "Max Planck Institute for Brain Research")

The city is home to three [Max Planck Society](/wiki/Max_Planck_Society "Max Planck Society") institutes: the [Max Planck Institute for Legal History and Legal Theory](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Legal_History_and_Legal_Theory "Max Planck Institute for Legal History and Legal Theory"), [Max Planck Institute for Biophysics](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Biophysics "Max Planck Institute for Biophysics"), and the [Max Planck Institute for Brain Research](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Brain_Research "Max Planck Institute for Brain Research").

The [Frankfurt Institute for Advanced Studies](/wiki/Frankfurt_Institute_for_Advanced_Studies "Frankfurt Institute for Advanced Studies"), sponsored by several institutional and private sources, is involved in theoretical research in physics, chemistry, neuroscience, and computer science.

Frankfurt is host to the *Römisch-Germanische-Kommission* (RGK), the [German Archaeological Institute](/wiki/German_Archaeological_Institute "German Archaeological Institute") branch for [prehistoric archeology](/wiki/Prehistory "Prehistory") in Germany and Europe. The RGK is involved in a variety of research projects. Its library, with over 200,000 items, is one of the largest archeological libraries in the world.[[144]](#cite_note-147)

[Goethe University](/wiki/Goethe_University_Frankfurt "Goethe University Frankfurt") and [Frankfurt University of Applied Sciences](/wiki/Frankfurt_University_of_Applied_Sciences "Frankfurt University of Applied Sciences") are involved in the Hessian Center for Artificial Intelligence ("hessian.AI").

## Trade unions and associations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=124 "Edit section: Trade unions and associations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Ig-metall-haus-2010-ffm-028.jpg/250px-Ig-metall-haus-2010-ffm-028.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ig-metall-haus-2010-ffm-028.jpg)

Main Forum, home to [IG Metall](/wiki/IG_Metall "IG Metall")

Frankfurt is home to multiple trade unions and associations, including:

* [IG Metall](/wiki/IG_Metall "IG Metall"), Germany's largest [metalworkers](/wiki/Metalworking "Metalworking") trade union, based at the Main Forum high-rise building in the [Gutleutviertel](/wiki/Gutleutviertel_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Gutleutviertel (Frankfurt am Main)") district
* [IG Bauen-Agrar-Umwelt](/wiki/IG_Bauen-Agrar-Umwelt "IG Bauen-Agrar-Umwelt"), a union for [construction](/wiki/Construction_worker "Construction worker") and engineering workers,
* [Gewerkschaft Erziehung und Wissenschaft](/wiki/Education_and_Science_Workers%27_Union_(Germany) "Education and Science Workers' Union (Germany)"), a union for teachers
* [Gewerkschaft Deutscher Lokomotivführer](/wiki/Gewerkschaft_Deutscher_Lokomotivf%C3%BChrer "Gewerkschaft Deutscher Lokomotivführer"), a union for [train drivers](/wiki/Railroad_engineer "Railroad engineer")

[Trade associations](/wiki/Trade_association "Trade association") include:

* [Verband der Elektrotechnik, Elektronik und Informationstechnik](/wiki/Verband_der_Elektrotechnik,_Elektronik_und_Informationstechnik "Verband der Elektrotechnik, Elektronik und Informationstechnik") (Electrotechnical, Electronic and Information Technology Association)
* [DECHEMA Gesellschaft für Chemische Technik und Biotechnologie](/wiki/DECHEMA "DECHEMA") (Applied Chemistry and Biotechnology Association)
* [Börsenverein des Deutschen Buchhandels](/wiki/B%C3%B6rsenverein_des_Deutschen_Buchhandels "Börsenverein des Deutschen Buchhandels"), which organizes the [Frankfurt Book Fair](/wiki/Frankfurt_Book_Fair "Frankfurt Book Fair")
* Bundesverband des Deutschen Versandhandels (German Mail Order Industry Association)
* Verband der Chemischen Industrie (Chemical Industry Association)
* Verband der Photoindustrie (Photography Industry Association)
* Verband Deutscher Maschinen- und Anlagenbau (German Machine and Equipment Building Association)
* Verband der Köche Deutschlands (German Cooks Association)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=125 "Edit section: Media")]

### Newspapers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=126 "Edit section: Newspapers")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/FAZ_Building_2.jpg/250px-FAZ_Building_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:FAZ_Building_2.jpg)

Editorial department building of *Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung*

Two important daily newspapers are published. The conservative *[Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung](/wiki/Frankfurter_Allgemeine_Zeitung "Frankfurter Allgemeine Zeitung")*, also known as *FAZ*, was founded in 1949 and is the German newspaper with the widest circulation outside of Germany, with its editors claiming to deliver the newspaper to 148 countries every day. The FAZ has a circulation of over 380,000 copies daily. The other important newspaper, the *[Frankfurter Rundschau](/wiki/Frankfurter_Rundschau "Frankfurter Rundschau")*, was first published in 1945 and has a daily circulation of over 181,000.

### Magazines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=127 "Edit section: Magazines")]

Several magazines also originate from Frankfurt. The local *Journal Frankfurt* is the best-known magazine for events, parties, and "insider tips". *Öko-Test* is a consumer-oriented magazine that focuses on ecological topics. [*Titanic*](/wiki/Titanic_(magazine) "Titanic (magazine)") is a well-known and often criticized [satirical](/wiki/Satire "Satire") magazine with a circulation of approximately 100,000.

### Radio and TV

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=128 "Edit section: Radio and TV")]

Frankfurt's first radio station was the Südwestdeutsche Rundfunkdienst AG (Southwest German Broadcast Service), founded in 1924. Its successor service is the public broadcaster [Hessischer Rundfunk](/wiki/Hessischer_Rundfunk "Hessischer Rundfunk") (Hessian Broadcast Service). It is located at the "[Funkhaus am Dornbusch](/wiki/Broadcasting_House_Dornbusch "Broadcasting House Dornbusch")" in the [Dornbusch](/wiki/Dornbusch_(Frankfurt_am_Main) "Dornbusch (Frankfurt am Main)") district and is one of the most important radio and television [broadcasters](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting") in Hesse, with additional studios in [Kassel](/wiki/Kassel "Kassel"), [Darmstadt](/wiki/Darmstadt "Darmstadt") and [Fulda](/wiki/Fulda "Fulda").

[Bloomberg TV](/wiki/Bloomberg_TV "Bloomberg TV") and [RTL Television](/wiki/RTL_Television "RTL Television") have regional studios.

Other radio broadcasters include Main FM and Radio X.

From August 1945 to October 2004, the [American Forces Network](/wiki/American_Forces_Network "American Forces Network") (AFN) had broadcast from Frankfurt ([AFN Frankfurt](/wiki/AFN_Frankfurt "AFN Frankfurt")). Due to troop reductions the AFN's location has been closed with AFN now broadcasting from [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim").

### News agency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=129 "Edit section: News agency")]

Frankfurt is home to the German office of [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters"), a global [news agency](/wiki/News_agency "News agency"). [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press") and US-based international news agency Feature Story News have bureaux in Frankfurt.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=130 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Match_Frankfurt_-_Marseille_in_November_2018.jpg/250px-Match_Frankfurt_-_Marseille_in_November_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Match_Frankfurt_-_Marseille_in_November_2018.jpg)

The [Waldstadion](/wiki/Waldstadion_(Frankfurt) "Waldstadion (Frankfurt)") (As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit) known as the *Deutsche Bank Park*), home of the football club [Eintracht Frankfurt](/wiki/Eintracht_Frankfurt "Eintracht Frankfurt")

Frankfurt is home to several professional sports teams. Some of them have won German Championships. Basketball side [Skyliners Frankfurt](/wiki/Skyliners_Frankfurt "Skyliners Frankfurt") won the [German Basketball Championship](/wiki/German_Basketball_Championship "German Basketball Championship") in 2004 and the [German Cup](/wiki/German_Basketball_Cup "German Basketball Cup") in 2000. Football sides [Eintracht Frankfurt](/wiki/Eintracht_Frankfurt_(women) "Eintracht Frankfurt (women)") (formerly 1. FFC Frankfurt) is one of Germany's most successful clubs in women's football, with a shared record of title-holders of the [Frauen-Bundesliga](/wiki/Frauen-Bundesliga "Frauen-Bundesliga"), while [Eintracht Frankfurt](/wiki/Eintracht_Frankfurt "Eintracht Frankfurt") are one of Germany's most traditional clubs in men's football, being one-time [German champions](/wiki/List_of_German_football_champions "List of German football champions"), five-times winners of the [DFB-Pokal](/wiki/DFB-Pokal "DFB-Pokal"), two time winners of the [UEFA Europa League](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League") (formerly UEFA Cup) in 1980 and 2022 and one time runner-up of the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") in 1959-60 and [UEFA Super Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Super_Cup "UEFA Super Cup") in 2022. Other following sports teams or clubs in Frankfurt include:

* [Frankfurt Galaxy](/wiki/Frankfurt_Galaxy_(ELF) "Frankfurt Galaxy (ELF)"), [American football](/wiki/NFL_Europe "NFL Europe")
* [Frankfurt Universe](/wiki/Frankfurt_Universe "Frankfurt Universe"), [American football](/wiki/German_Football_League "German Football League")
* Frankfurt Pirates, [American football](/wiki/German_Football_League "German Football League")
* [Frankfurt Sarsfields GAA](/wiki/Frankfurt_Sarsfields_GAA "Frankfurt Sarsfields GAA"), [Gaelic football](/wiki/Gaelic_football "Gaelic football")
* [Frankfurt Lions](/wiki/Frankfurt_Lions "Frankfurt Lions") (until 2010), [Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey")
* [Löwen Frankfurt](/wiki/L%C3%B6wen_Frankfurt "Löwen Frankfurt") (since 2010), [Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey")
* [SC 1880 Frankfurt](/wiki/SC_1880_Frankfurt "SC 1880 Frankfurt"), [Rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union "Rugby union")
* [FSV Frankfurt](/wiki/FSV_Frankfurt "FSV Frankfurt"), Football (men)
* [Rot-Weiss Frankfurt](/wiki/Rot-Weiss_Frankfurt "Rot-Weiss Frankfurt"), Football
* [Frankfurter FC Germania 1894](/wiki/Frankfurter_FC_Germania_1894 "Frankfurter FC Germania 1894"), Football

Frankfurt is host to the [classic cycle](/wiki/Classic_cycle_races "Classic cycle races") race [Eschborn-Frankfurt City Loop](/wiki/Eschborn-Frankfurt_City_Loop "Eschborn-Frankfurt City Loop") (known as *Rund um den Henninger-Turm* from 1961 to 2008). The city hosts also the annual [Frankfurt Marathon](/wiki/Frankfurt_Marathon "Frankfurt Marathon") and the [Ironman Germany](/wiki/Ironman_Germany "Ironman Germany"). In addition to the former, it is one of 13 global host locations to the J.P. Morgan Corporate Challenge, Germany's biggest corporate sports event. Rhein-Main Eissport Club forms the base of the German [bandy](/wiki/Bandy "Bandy") community.

## Further reading

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Frankfurt&action=edit&section=142 "Edit section: Further reading")]

History

* Kramer, Waldemar (Hrsg.): *Frankfurt Chronik*. Verlag Waldemar Kramer, Frankfurt am Main 1987 (3. Auflage), [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [3-7829-0321-8](/wiki/Special:BookSources/3-7829-0321-8 "Special:BookSources/3-7829-0321-8").
* [Lothar Gall](/wiki/Lothar_Gall "Lothar Gall") (Hrsg.): *FFM 1200. Traditionen und Perspektiven einer Stadt*. Jan Thorbecke Verlag, Sigmaringen 1994, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [3-7995-1203-9](/wiki/Special:BookSources/3-7995-1203-9 "Special:BookSources/3-7995-1203-9") (Katalog zur 1200-Jahrfeier 1994 mit wiss. Aufsätzen).
* Mack, Ernst: *Von der Steinzeit zur Stauferstadt. Die frühe Geschichte von Frankfurt am Main*. Verlag Josef Knecht, Frankfurt am Main 1994, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [3-7820-0685-2](/wiki/Special:BookSources/3-7820-0685-2 "Special:BookSources/3-7820-0685-2").

Architecture

* Schohmann, Heinz: *Frankfurt am Main und Umgebung. Von der Pfalzsiedlung zum Bankenzentrum*. Dumont Kunstreiseführer. Dumont, Köln 2003, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [3-7701-6305-2](/wiki/Special:BookSources/3-7701-6305-2 "Special:BookSources/3-7701-6305-2"). (mit Schwerpunkt Architektur).
* Bodenbach, Christoph (Hrsg.): *Neue Architektur in Frankfurt am Main*. Junius Verlag, Hamburg 2008, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-3-88506-583-8](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-3-88506-583-8 "Special:BookSources/978-3-88506-583-8").
* Sturm, Philipp, Schmal, Peter Cachola: *Hochhausstadt Frankfurt. Bauten und Visionen seit 1945*. Prestel, München 2014, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-3-7913-5363-0](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-3-7913-5363-0 "Special:BookSources/978-3-7913-5363-0").

Others

* Setzepfandt, Christian: *Geheimnisvolles Frankfurt am Main*. Wartberg, Gudensberg-Gleichen 2003, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [3-8313-1347-4](/wiki/Special:BookSources/3-8313-1347-4 "Special:BookSources/3-8313-1347-4").
* Mosebach, Martin: *Mein Frankfurt*. Mit Photographien von Barbara Klemm. Insel, Frankfurt am Main 2002, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [3-458-34571-X](/wiki/Special:BookSources/3-458-34571-X "Special:BookSources/3-458-34571-X"). (Insel-Taschenbuch. Bd 2871)
